Disorders of lashes 3
DISORDERS OF LASHES
Madarosis
Signs
• Decrease in number or complete loss of lashes.
Causes
• See Table 1.1.
Table 1.1 Causes of madarosis
Fig. 1.1
1. Local
• Chronic anterior lid margin disease (Fig. 1.1)
• Infiltrative lid tumours may cause localised madarosis (Fig. 1.2)
• Burns (Fig. 1.3)
2. Following removal
• Iatrogenic for treatment of trichiasis or distichiasis (Fig. 1.4)
• Cryotherapy or radiotherapy of lid tumours
• Trichotillomania which is a psychiatric disorder of habitual hair removal
3. Generalised skin disease
• Generalised alopecia
• Psoriasis Fig. 1.2
• Atopic dermatitis (Fig. 1.5)
• Ichthyosis (Fig. 1.6)
4. Systemic disease
• Myxoedema
• Systemic lupus erythematosus
• Syphilis
• Lepromatous leprosy
Fig. 1.3
Fig. 1.4 Fig. 1.5 Fig. 1.6
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 4 of 404 ---
4 CHAPTER 1 • EYELIDS
Poliosis
Signs
• Localised patch of grey or white hair due to lack of pigment in the
epidermis (Fig. 1.7).
Causes
• See Table 1.2.
Table 1.2 Causes of poliosis Fig. 1.7
1. Idiopathic
2. Local conditions
• Chronic anterior blepharitis
• Sympathetic ophthalmitis
• Lid margin tumours
• Herpes zoster ophthalmicus
• Alopecia areata
3. Systemic conditions
• Vogt–Koyanagi–Harada syndrome in which it is associated with vitiligo (Fig. 1.8)
• Waardenburg syndrome
• Marfan syndrome
• Tuberous sclerosis
Fig. 1.8
Misdirection
Trichiasis
Signs
• Inward turning of previously normal lashes (Fig. 1.9).
Causes
• Idiopathic.
• Lid margin scarring (e.g. chronic blepharitis, herpes zoster ophthalmicus
and trachoma).
Fig. 1.9
Differential diagnosis
• Inward turning of lower lid lashes associated with entropion (pseudo-
trichiasis – Fig. 1.10).
Fig. 1.10
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 5 of 404 ---
Disorders of lashes 5
Congenital distichiasis
Definition
• A rare, bilateral, hereditary condition.
Signs
• Partial or complete second row of lashes originating from or slightly behind
the meibomian gland orifices (Fig. 1.11).
Look for
• Lymphoedema.
Fig. 1.11
Acquired distichiasis (metaplastic lashes)
Definition
• An uncommon unilateral or bilateral condition caused by metaplasia and
dedifferentiation of meibomian glands to become hair follicles in late
cicatrising conjunctivitis (e.g. ocular cicatricial pemphigoid).
Signs
• Crops of stunted lashes originating from meibomian gland orifices (Fig. 1.12).
Fig. 1.12
Eyelash ptosis
Signs
• Downward drooping of upper-lid lashes (Fig. 1.13).
Causes
• Idiopathic.
• Floppy eyelid syndrome.
• Long-standing facial palsy.
Differential diagnosis
• True ptosis.
• Upper lid entropion. Fig. 1.13
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 6 of 404 ---
6 CHAPTER 1 • EYELIDS
Epiblepharon
Definition
• A bilateral congenital condition that is common in Orientals.
Signs
• Extra fold of skin stretches across the anterior lid margin and the lashes
are directed vertically, especially medially (Fig. 1.14).
• When the fold of skin is pulled down the lashes return temporarily to their
normal position.
Fig. 1.14
Differential diagnosis
• Congenital entropion is characterised by in-turning of the entire lid and
lashes with absence of the lower lid crease (Fig. 1.15); when downward
pressure is applied to the lid the entire lid becomes pulled away from the
globe.
Fig. 1.15
Trichomegaly
Signs
• Excessive eyelash growth (Fig. 1.16).
Causes
• See Table 1.3.
Table 1.3 Causes of trichomegaly
Fig. 1.16
1. Congenital
• Oliver–McFarlane syndrome – RP, dwarfism and mental handicap
• Cornelia de Lange syndrome – synophrys, low hairline, and developmental and
musculoskeletal anomalies
• Goldstein–Hutt syndrome – cataract and hereditary spherocytosis
• Hermansky–Pudlak syndrome – albinism and bleeding diathesis
• Oculocutaneous albinism type 1
2. Acquired
• Drug-induced – phenytoin, ciclosporin and topical prostaglandin analogues (Fig. 1.17)
• Malnutrition
• AIDS Fig. 1.17
• Porphyria
• Familial
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 7 of 404 ---
Infl ammatory lid margin disease 7
INFLAMMATORY LID MARGIN DISEASE
Staphylococcal blepharitis
Signs
• Lashes – soft scales around lash roots (Fig. 1.18), madarosis, poliosis and
trichiasis.
• Anterior lid margin – ulceration, notching and microabscesses.
• Cysts – acute external hordeolum (stye – see Fig. 1.102).
• Tear film – dry.
• Conjunctiva – papillae and phlyctens.
Fig. 1.18
• Cornea – punctate erosions and marginal infiltrates.
• Associated dermatitis – atopic (see Fig. 15.1).
Seborrhoeic blepharitis
Signs
• Lashes – soft greasy scales in between the lash roots and oily lashes
stuck together (Fig. 1.19).
• Anterior lid margin – shiny.
• Tear film – dry.
• Conjunctiva – unremarkable.
• Cornea – punctate erosions and peripheral infiltrates.
• Associated dermatitis – seborrhoeic.
Fig. 1.19
Posterior blepharitis
Signs
• Lashes – unremarkable.
• Posterior lid margin – notching, oily capping or occlusion of meibomian
gland orifices (Fig. 1.20), expressed meibomian secretions may be turbid
and toothpaste-like.
• Cysts – meibomian (see Fig. 1.100).
• Tear film – dry and frothy.
• Conjunctiva – unremarkable.
Fig. 1.20
• Cornea – punctate erosions and infiltrates.
• Associated dermatitis – acne rosacea (see Fig. 15.2).
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Associated Atopic Seborrhoeic Acne rosacea
dermatitis
Childhood blepharokeratoconjunctivitis
Signs
• Lashes – crusty.
• Lid margin – chronic anterior and posterior blepharitis.
• Cysts – styes and meibomian, often multiple (Fig. 1.21).
• Tear film – dry.
• Conjunctiva – phlyctens, and follicular or papillary hypertrophy.
Fig. 1.21
• Cornea – punctate erosions, axial subepithelial haze, and peripheral
infiltrates and vascularisation.
• Associated dermatitis – absent.
Angular blepharitis
Signs
• Lashes – unremarkable.
• Lids – erythema, scaling and fissuring of skin at one or both canthi
(Fig. 1.22).
• Cysts – absent.
• Tear film – unremarkable.
• Conjunctiva – follicular conjunctivitis.
• Cornea – marginal infiltrates and phlyctens.
• Associated dermatitis – atopic. Fig. 1.22
Phthiriasis palpebrarum
Definition
• Infestation of the lashes with the crab louse (Phthirus pubis).
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Superfi cial infl ammation 9
Signs
• Lashes – adherent lice and ova (Fig. 1.23).
• Lids – erythematous.
Fig. 1.23
SUPERFICIAL INFLAMMATION
Allergic
Contact dermatitis
Definition
• A common, unilateral or bilateral
condition, frequently caused by
sensitivity to topical medication.
Signs
Fig. 1.24 Fig. 1.25
• Initially there is erythema and
oedema associated with
ipsilateral conjunctivitis (Fig. 1.24).
• Thickening and crusting develops
in long-standing cases (Fig. 1.25).
Atopic dermatitis
Definition
• An uncommon, bilateral condition which may occur in patients with more
generalised skin involvement.
Signs
• Erythema, thickening, vertical fissuring, often associated with madarosis
and staphylococcal blepharitis (Fig. 1.26). Fig. 1.26
Table 1.5 Ocular associations of
Look for
atopic dermatitis
• See Table 1.5.
• Chronic staphylococcal blepharitis
• Angular blepharitis
• Vernal conjunctivitis in children
• Atopic keratoconjunctivitis in adults
• Keratoconus
• Anterior shield-like cataract
• Retinal detachment

--- Page 10 of 404 ---
10 CHAPTER 1 • EYELIDS
Infectious
Herpes zoster ophthalmicus
Definition
• A common unilateral infection with varicella-zoster virus that typically
affects the elderly.
Signs
• Painful maculopapular rash involving the first division of the trigeminal
nerve followed by vesicles, pustules and crusting ulceration (Fig. 1.27).
Look for
Fig. 1.27
• Hutchinson sign (involvement of the side of the nose).
• Anterior uveitis.
• Keratitis.
• Scleritis.
• Neurological complications.
• AIDS in young patients.
Impetigo
Definition
• A bilateral infection with staphylococci or beta-haemolytic streptococci that
typically affects children.
Signs
• Small vesicles and bullae which on rupturing produce crusts composed of
golden-yellow crystals (Fig. 1.28).
Fig. 1.28
Erysipelas
Definition
• A unilateral subcutaneous cellulitis caused by entry of beta-haemolytic
streptococci at a site of minor skin trauma.
Signs
• Well-defined, erythematous, tender subcutaneous plaque, often with a
butterfly configuration (Fig. 1.29).
Fig. 1.29
Necrotising fasciitis
Definition
• A rare bilateral but life-threatening necrosis of subcutaneous soft tissues
caused by S. pyogenes or S. aureus.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Signs

(Fig. 1.30).

• Unilateral tender erythema and
oedema involving the upper
outer part of the lid causing a
characteristic S-shaped deformity
(Fig. 1.32).
• Injection of the lacrimal gland
Fig. 1.32
and conjunctiva may occur
(Fig. 1.33).
Fig. 1.33
Associated with dacryocystitis
Signs
• Very tender erythema and oedema at the inner canthus with spread to the
lids (Fig. 1.34).
Fig. 1.34

12 CHAPTER 1 • EYELIDS
Inflammatory orbital disease
Signs
• Unilateral or bilateral tender erythema and oedema, and mechanical ptosis
(Fig. 1.35).
Fig. 1.35
Angioedema
Signs
• Transient painless pitting oedema (Fig. 1.36) that may be associated with
oedema of the tongue or lips.
Fig. 1.36
Local non-inflammatory causes
Orbital fat herniation
Definition
• A common, usually bilateral, age-related condition.
Signs
• Asymmetrical periorbital swelling (Fig. 1.37).
Fig. 1.37
Blunt trauma
Signs
• Painful oedema and ecchymosis
(Fig. 1.38).
• Subcutaneous emphysema
caused by medial orbital floor
blow-out fracture (Fig. 1.39).
Fig. 1.38 Fig. 1.39

--- Page 13 of 404 ---
Diffuse eyelid swelling 13
Blepharochalasis
Definition
• An uncommon, usually bilateral condition which typically affects young
individuals characterised by recurrent attacks of non-pitting oedema of the
upper and occasionally the lower lid.
Direct carotid–cavernous fistula
Definition
• A high-flow shunt in which carotid artery blood passes directly into the
cavernous sinus.
Signs
• Severe lid swelling and haemorrhagic chemosis (Fig. 1.40).
Look for
• Pulsatile proptosis associated with a bruit.
• Anterior segment ischaemia.
Fig. 1.40
• Raised intraocular pressure.
• Ocular motor nerve palsies.
Rapidly growing tumours
Signs
• In rhabdomyosarcoma the swollen lid is frequently red but not warm
(Fig. 1.41).
Fig. 1.41
Systemic causes
Myxoedema
Signs
• Swelling typically involves the lower lids (Fig. 1.42).
Look for hypercholesterolaemia
• Xanthelasma.
• Arcus senilis.
Fig. 1.42
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 14 of 404 ---
14 CHAPTER 1 • EYELIDS
Primary amyloidosis
Definition
• A rare condition in which there is accumulation of insoluble fibrillary
proteins (amyloid) in various organs and tissues.
Signs
• Bilateral eyelid infiltration (Fig. 1.43).
Look for Fig. 1.43
• Involvement of other ocular structures such as the cornea, lacrimal gland,
conjunctiva and orbit.
Renal failure
Signs
• Asymmetrical oedema mainly of the upper eyelids (Fig. 1.44).
Look for
• Ankle oedema and ascites.
Fig. 1.44
Adult-onset periocular xanthogranuloma
Definition
• A very rare histiocytic granulomatous disease associated with asthma.
Signs
• Bilateral diffuse bilateral eyelid infiltration (Fig. 1.45).
Look for
Fig. 1.45
• Subconjunctival infiltrates.
Rosai–Dorfman disease
Definition
• A rare disease usually seen in young individuals characterised by
lymphadenopathy and constitutional features.
Signs
• Bilateral diffuse eyelid masses (Fig. 1.46).
Fig. 1.46
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 15 of 404 ---
Discoloration of eyelids 15
DISCOLORATION OF EYELIDS
Oculodermal melanocytosis (naevus of Ota)
Definition
• An uncommon congenital usually unilateral condition.
Signs
• Grey-blue discoloration of the skin involving the distribution of one or more
branches of the trigeminal nerve (Fig. 1.47).
Look for
• Conjunctival subepithelial melanosis (see Fig. 3.66).
• Iris hyperchromia (see Fig. 8.97) and mammillations (see Fig. 8.42). Fig. 1.47
• Hyperpigmentation of the trabecular meshwork (see Fig. 7.6) and fundus
(see Fig. 7.7).
• Glaucoma.
• Increased risk of uveal melanoma.
Sturge–Weber syndrome
Definition
• A sporadic phacomatosis characterised by a port-wine stain and ipsilateral
leptomeningeal haemangioma.
Signs
• Facial naevus flammeus (port-wine stain) involving the distribution of one or
more branches of the trigeminal nerve (Fig. 1.48).
Look for
• Conjunctival telangiectasia (see Fig. 3.58).
Fig. 1.48
• Iris hyperchromia.
• Glaucoma.
• Diffuse choroidal haemangioma.
Lentigo maligna (Hutchinson freckle)
Definition
• The pre-invasive stage of melanoma that typically affects elderly patients.
Signs
• Slowly expanding pigmented macule (Fig. 1.49).
• Nodular thickening is indicative of transformation into a frank melanoma.
Fig. 1.49
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 16 of 404 ---
16 CHAPTER 1 • EYELIDS
Congenital naevus
Signs
• Usually fairly large and may occasionally involve both the upper and lower
lids (split or kissing naevus – Fig. 1.50).
Dermatomyositis
Fig. 1.50
Definition
• An autoimmune disease characterised by involvement of skeletal muscle
and skin.
Signs
• Heliotrope discoloration of the eyelids that may be associated with
ulceration (Fig. 1.51).
Look for Fig. 1.51
• KCS.
• Scleritis.
• Retinopathy.
Vitiligo
Signs
• Well-defined symmetrical patches of progressive cutaneous depigmentation
that may be associated with poliosis (see Fig. 1.8).
Causes
• Idiopathic.
Fig. 1.52
• Myxoedema.
• Thyrotoxicosis (Fig. 1.52).
• Pernicious anaemia.
• Autoimmune Addison disease.
• Vogt–Koyanagi–Harada syndrome.
• Sympathetic ophthalmitis.
Minocycline
Minocycline is a tetracycline that may occasionally cause cutaneous
pigmentation (Fig. 1.53).
Fig. 1.53
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 17 of 404 ---
Lid retraction 17
LID RETRACTION
In lid retraction the lower margin of the upper lid is either level with or above
the limbus. According to aetiology the condition may be unilateral or bilateral,
symmetric or asymmetric, and transient or permanent. The causes are as
follows:
Thyroid eye disease
Eponymous signs
a. Dalrymple – lid retraction in primary gaze which may be bilateral or
unilateral (Fig. 1.54).
b. Kocher – staring and frightened appearance particularly marked on
attentive fixation (Fig. 1.55).
c. von Graefe – retarded descent of the upper lid on downgaze (lid lag –
Fig. 1.56).
Fig. 1.54 Fig. 1.55
Fig. 1.56
Myasthenia gravis
Myasthenia may cause lid retraction as a result of the following mechanisms:
• Unilateral myasthenic ptosis with contralateral lid retraction caused by
compensatory increased innervation to the levator (Fig. 1.57).
• Patients with myasthenia may also manifest transient bilateral lid retraction
(‘twitch’) as the eyes are brought from downgaze to the primary position.
Fig. 1.57
Third nerve misdirection
Definition
• A rare condition that may be congenital or may follow an acquired third
nerve palsy.
Signs
• Transient unilateral lid retraction or ptosis induced by various eye
Fig. 1.58
movements (Fig. 1.58).
Marcus Gunn jaw-winking syndrome
Definition
• An uncommon congenital, usually unilateral, condition caused by abnormal
synkinesis between the levator and the pterygoid muscles.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 18 of 404 ---
18 CHAPTER 1 • EYELIDS
Signs
• Transient lid retraction may be induced by stimulation of the pterygoid
muscles as by opening the mouth (Fig. 1.59).
• Less common stimuli to winking include jaw protrusion, smiling, swallowing
and clenching of teeth.
Fig. 1.59
Collier sign of the midbrain (Parinaud syndrome)
Signs
Table 1.6 Causes of Parinaud syndrome
• Bilateral lid retraction is associated with light-near
1. In children
dissociation, impairment of upgaze, convergence • Pinealoma
deficiency and convergence-retraction nystagmus. • Aqueduct stenosis
• Meningitis
Causes 2. In young adults
• Demyelination
• See Table 1.6.
• Trauma
• Arteriovenous aneurysm
3. In the elderly
• Midbrain vascular accidents
• Mass lesions involving the periaqueductal grey matter
• Posterior fossa aneurysm
Miscellaneous causes
a. Infantile hydrocephalus (setting sun sign – Fig. 1.60) in which there is
bilateral lid retraction, forceful downward deviation of the eyes and
impairment of upgaze.
b. Facial nerve palsy with
unopposed ipsilateral levator
overaction (Fig. 1.61).
Fig. 1.60
Fig. 1.61
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 19 of 404 ---
Ptosis 19
c. Duane retraction syndrome type 1 and 3 in which there is transient
widening of the palpebral fissure and lid retraction on attempted abduction
(Fig. 1.62).
d. Parkinsonism in which there is also lack of facial expression (Fig. 1.63),
rigidity and tremor.
e. Isolated congenital is very rare (Fig. 1.64).
f. Mechanical may be caused by surgical over-correction of ptosis or scarring
of the upper lid.
g. Down syndrome.
h. Transient ‘eye popping’ reflex in normal infants.
i. Uraemia (Summerskill sign).
j. Topical sympathomimetic drops.
k. Prominent globe may cause pseudo-lid retraction.
Fig. 1.62
Fig. 1.63 Fig. 1.64
PTOSIS
Neurogenic adult ptosis
Third nerve palsy
Signs
• Ptosis is unilateral and usually moderate to severe (Fig. 1.65).
• In the primary position the eye is slightly divergent and depressed.
• Limitation of adduction, elevation and depression.
• Paralysis of accommodation.
• The pupil may or may not be fixed and dilated depending on the cause.
Look for pupillary involvement Fig. 1.65
• If uninvolved – diabetes and hypertension.
• If involved – posterior communicating aneurysm.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 20 of 404 ---
20 CHAPTER 1 • EYELIDS
Horner syndrome (oculosympathetic palsy)
Signs
• Mild unilateral ptosis (Fig. 1.66).
• Ipsilateral miosis.
• Ipsilateral slight elevation of the lower eyelid.
• Ipsilateral reduction in sweating if the lesion is below the superior cervical
ganglion.
Fig. 1.66
• Hypochromic heterochromia iridis may be present if the lesion is congenital
or acquired and long-standing.
Causes
Table 1.7 Causes of Horner syndrome
• See Table 1.7.
1. Central lesions (first-order neuron)
• Brainstem tumour
• Syringomyelia
• Lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome
• Upper spinal cord tumour
2. Preganglionic lesions (second-order neuron)
• Neural crest tumour
• Neck lesions
• Intrathoracic lesions (Pancoast tumour, enlarged glands, aneurysm)
3. Postganglionic lesions (third-order neuron)
• Cluster headache (migrainous neuralgia)
• Nasopharyngeal tumour
• Otitis media
• Cavernous sinus mass
• Internal carotid artery disease
4. Miscellaneous
• Congenital
• Idiopathic
Third nerve misdirection
Signs
• Transient ptosis or ptosis accompanying various eye movements (see
Fig. 1.58).
Myogenic adult ptosis
Myasthenia gravis
Definition
• An autoimmune disease in which antibodies mediate damage and
destruction of acetylcholine receptors in striated muscle.
• The three types are ocular, bulbar and generalised.
Signs
• Insidious, bilateral but frequently asymmetric ptosis (Fig. 1.67), worse with Fig. 1.67
fatigue and in upgaze.
• If one lid is elevated manually as the patient looks up, the fellow lid will
show fine oscillatory vertical movements.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 21 of 404 ---
Ptosis 21
• Cogan twitch sign – brief lid retraction as the eyes are brought from
downgaze to the primary position.
• Intermittent diplopia, commonly vertical.
Look for systemic
• Excessive muscular fatigue particularly involving the arms and proximal
muscles of the legs.
• Problems with speech, chewing and swallowing.
• Thymoma.
Myotonic dystrophy
Definition
• A rare AD condition characterised by delayed relaxation of skeletal muscles
after contraction.
Signs
• Bilateral, symmetric ptosis.
• Mournful expression due to facial weakness (Fig. 1.68).
Look for eyes
• Bilateral miosis.
• Light-near dissociation.
• Presenile posterior stellate cataract (see Fig. 10.59).
• Pigmentary retinopathy.
• Mild ophthalmoplegia.
• Low IOP.
Look for systemic Fig. 1.68
• Myotonia of peripheral muscles resulting in difficulty in relaxing grip.
• Frontal baldness in men.
• Hypogonadism resulting in infertility and sterility.
• Weakness of the sternomastoids.
• Cardiomyopathy and conduction defects.
• Small pituitary fossa.
• Glucose intolerance.
Ocular myopathy
Definition
• A very rare mitochondrial cytopathy.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

22 CHAPTER 1 • EYELIDS
Signs
• Progressive symmetric ptosis and external ophthalmoplegia (Fig. 1.69).
Look for systemic
a. Oculopharyngeal dystrophy – weakness of pharyngeal muscles and wasting
of the temporalis.
b. Kearns–Sayre syndrome – pigmentary retinopathy, heart block and ataxia.
Fig. 1.69
Other types of adult ptosis
Aponeurotic
Signs
• Good levator function.
• High or absent upper-lid crease.
• Thinning of the eyelid above the tarsal plate and deep upper sulci (Fig. 1.70).
Causes
• Involutional is by far the most common.
Fig. 1.70
• Lid swelling following surgery or blepharochalasis.
Mechanical
Causes
• Severe lid oedema (see Fig. 1.44).
• Eyelid tumours such as plexiform neurofibromas (Fig. 1.71).
• Orbital lesions such as cellulitis and anteriorly located tumours (see
Fig. 1.46).
Fig. 1.71

Neurogenic childhood ptosis 23
NEUROGENIC CHILDHOOD PTOSIS
Marcus Gunn syndrome
Signs
• Retraction of the ptotic lid in
conjunction with stimulation of
the pterygoid muscles induced by
chewing, sucking, opening the
mouth (Fig. 1.72AB) or
contralateral jaw movements.
Fig. 1.72A
Fig. 1.72B
Horner syndrome (oculosympathetic palsy)
Horner syndrome is rare in children and may be associated with ipsilateral
hypochromic heterochromia.
Causes
• Congenital – intrauterine varicella (chickenpox – Fig. 1.73), obstetric trauma,
although many cases are idiopathic.
Fig. 1.73
• Postnatally acquired – brain stem disease, neck trauma and neuroblastoma.
Third nerve palsy
Causes
• Bilateral congenital – anomalous brain development or prenatal damage.
• Postnatally acquired – trauma, meningitis, tumours and ophthalmoplegic
migraine.
Differential diagnosis
• Early-onset esotropia.
• Duane syndrome types 1 and 3.
• Möbius syndrome.
Bassen–Kornzweig syndrome
Definition
• A rare AR disorder of metabolism characterised by fat malabsorption and
progressive symmetric ptosis.
Look for eyes
• Ophthalmoplegia.
• Nystagmus.
• Cataracts.
• Progressive retinal dystrophy characterised by white dots and pigmentary
retinopathy.

Look for systemic
• Spinocerebellar ataxia.
• Plasma acanthocytosis.

Myogenic childhood ptosis
Simple congenital ptosis
Pathogenesis
• Dystrophy of the levator muscle.
Signs
• Unilateral or bilateral ptosis of variable severity (Fig. 1.74A).
• Usually poor levator function (Fig. 1.74B).
Fig. 1.74A
• The ptotic lid is slightly higher in downgaze.
• Ipsilateral weakness of elevation is common.
• Compensatory chin-up position in severe bilateral cases.

Fig. 1.74B
Definition
• A rare bilateral congenital AD condition.
Signs

• Poor levator function.
• Short horizontal palpebral aperture.
Fig. 1.75
• Epicanthus inversus.
• Lateral ectropion.
• Poorly developed nasal bridge.
• Hypoplasia of the superior orbital rim.
• Telecanthus.
Congenital fibrosis syndrome
Definition
• A very rare non-progressive, usually AD, condition.
Signs
• Severe bilateral congenital ptosis and ophthalmoplegia (Fig. 1.76).

Causes of pseudo-ptosis
In pseudo-ptosis the lid margin is at the normal level but the lid appears ptotic.
Brow ptosis
Causes
• Excessive eyebrow skin or facial nerve palsy.
Signs
• It is diagnosed by manually elevating the eyebrow (Fig. 1.78).
Fig. 1.78
Dermatochalasis
Signs
• Excessive skin on both upper lids (Fig. 1.79).
Fig. 1.79
Contralateral lid retraction
Signs
• Detected by comparing the levels of the upper lid margins bearing in mind
that the margin of the upper lid normally covers the superior 2 mm of the
cornea (Fig. 1.80).
Fig. 1.80

Molluscum contagiosum
Cause
• A pox virus that typically affects children.

Cause

Cause
• Cellular proliferation of intraepidermal duct eccrine sweat gland epithelium.
Signs
• Multiple small papules (Fig. 1.87).
Fig. 1.87
Comedones
Cause
• Comedones occur in patients with acne vulgaris and consist of a plug of
keratin and sebum within the dilated orifice of a hair follicle.
Signs
• Open (blackheads) contain a plug of melanin containing keratin (Fig. 1.88).
• Closed (white heads) are cream-coloured papules.

NODULAR LESIONS
Benign
Chalazion (meibomian cyst)
Definition
• A very common chronic lipogranulomatous inflammation of the meibomian

Signs
• Painless, roundish, rubbery lesion within the tarsal plate (Fig. 1.100).

• Acne rosacea.

Definition
• A common acute purulent infection of a lash follicle and its associated

Signs

through the skin (Fig. 1.102).
Capillary haemangioma
Fig. 1.102
Presentation
• At birth or early infancy with a predilection for the upper lid.
Signs
• Red nodule that blanches with pressure and may increase in size on crying
(Fig. 1.103).

32 CHAPTER 1 • EYELIDS
Acquired naevus
Classification
• Acquired naevus can be subdivided into three types according to histology
(junctional, compound and dermal).
Signs
• The clinical appearance is determined by the histological location within
the skin.
• An intradermal naevus (most common) is a non-pigmented nodule through
which lashes may protrude (Fig. 1.104).
Fig. 1.104
Actinic (solar, senile) keratosis
Definition
• A very slow-growing condition that typically affects elderly, fair-skinned
individuals exposed to excessive sunlight. It predisposes to squamous cell
carcinoma.
Signs
• Hyperkeratotic nodule (Fig. 1.105) or plaque.
Fig. 1.105
Keratoacanthoma
Definition
• A fast-growing tumour that typically affects fair-skinned individuals with a
history of chronic sun exposure.
Signs
• Starts as a firm, pinkish, indurated nodule which quickly acquires a dome-
shaped configuration and a keratin-filled crater (Fig. 1.106).
Differential diagnosis
• Squamous cell carcinoma. Fig. 1.106
Neurofibroma
Definition
• A rare benign tumour that typically affects patients with NF1.
Signs
• Large subcutaneous nodule that causes a characteristic S-shaped
deformity of the upper lid (Fig. 1.107).
Fig. 1.107
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Bony abnormalities 41
CLINICAL SIGNS IN ORBITAL DISEASE
1. Bony
• Hypertelorism.
• Dystopia in which the orbits are not level.
• Shallow orbits.
2. Soft tissue
• Lid oedema.
• Ptosis.
• Chemosis.
3. Globe malposition
• Proptosis.
• Enophthalmos.
• Displacement.
4. Ophthalmoplegia
• By an orbital mass.
• Restrictive myopathy.
• Ocular motor nerve palsy.
• Muscle entrapment in a fracture.
5. Posterior segment
• Disc oedema.
• Optic atrophy.
• Chorioretinal folds.
• Retinal vascular congestion.
BONY ABNORMALITIES
Hypertelorism
Signs
• Wide separation of the orbits (Fig. 2.1).
Causes
• Facial trauma.
• Associated with congenital systemic abnormalities (see below).
Fig. 2.1
Differential diagnosis
• Telecanthus in which there is increased distance between the medial
canthi as a result of abnormally long medial canthal tendons.

Pfeiffer syndrome
Signs
• Acrocephaly (peaked skull), midfacial hypoplasia, orbital rotation and

Fig. 2.6
Other causes
a. Chromosomal abnormalities
• Trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome).
• Trisomy 18 (Edward syndrome).
b. Other bony defects
• Osteogenesis imperfecta.
• Osteopetrosis.
• Hyperphosphataemia.
ENOPHTHALMOS
In enophthalmos the globe is recessed within the orbit. It may be caused by the following conditions.
Small globe
a. Phthisis bulbi (Fig. 2.7).
b. Microphthalmos in which the net
volume of the globe is reduced; it
may be simple (Fig. 2.8) or
colobomatous.
c. Nanophthalmos which is
Fig. 2.7 Fig. 2.8
characterised by a small eye,
high hypermetropia, weak but
thick sclera, predisposition to
angle closure glaucoma and uveal
effusion.

a. Metastatic scirrhous breast carcinoma.

Duane retraction syndrome
Although this is usually characterised by retraction of the globe on
adduction rarely there may be true enophthalmos in the primary position
(see Fig. 14.17A).
PROPTOSIS
Signs
• The globe is pushed forward – best detected by looking down from above
(Fig. 2.12).
Direction
• Axial proptosis is caused by lesions within the muscle cone such as
Fig. 2.12
cavernous haemangioma, optic nerve tumours and thyroid eye disease.
• Eccentric proptosis is caused by extraconal lesions in which the direction
of proptosis is determined by the site of the lesion.
Causes of pseudo-proptosis
• Ipsilateral large globe as in buphthalmos (Fig. 2.13) or very high myopia.
• Ipsilateral lid retraction. Fig. 2.13
• Contralateral enophthalmos.

46 CHAPTER 2 • ORBIT
ORBITAL DISEASE IN CHILDREN
Inflammatory
Preseptal cellulitis
Definition
• An infection of the subcutaneous tissues anterior to the orbital septum.
Signs
• Unilateral, tender and red periorbital oedema (Fig. 2.14A).
• Normal ocular motility and visual acuity.
• CT shows opacification anterior to the orbital septum (Fig. 2.14B).
Fig. 2.14A
Fig. 2.14B
Orbital cellulitis
Definition
• A life-threatening infection of soft tissues posterior to the orbital septum,
which in children, is usually secondary to ethmoiditis.
Signs
• Very unwell patient with a high temperature. Fig. 2.15A
• Rapid onset of painful proptosis (usually down and out), chemosis, lid
oedema and ophthalmoplegia (Fig. 2.15A).
• Optic nerve dysfunction in severe cases.
• CT shows preseptal and orbital opacification (Fig. 2.15B).
Fig. 2.15B
Idiopathic orbital inflammatory syndrome (pseudo-tumour)
Definition
• An uncommon, non-neoplastic and non-infectious space-occupying condition
that may present in childhood or adult life. Acute orbital myositis is a
subtype which primarily affects one or more of the extraocular muscles.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 46 of 404 ---
Orbital disease in children 47
Signs
• Subacute onset of proptosis associated with lid oedema and displacement
of the globe (Fig. 2.16A).
• In acute orbital myositis, pain and diplopia are increased on attempted
gaze into the field of the affected muscle.
Fig. 2.16A
• CT shows a poorly-defined opacification with ill-defined orbital structures
(Fig. 2.16B).
Look for systemic disease in bilateral cases
• Wegener granulomatosis.
• Polyarteritis nodosa.
• Sarcoidosis.
• Tuberculosis. Fig. 2.16B
• Waldenström macroglobulinaemia.
Thyroid eye disease
Thyroid eye disease may rarely present in children as young as 10 years and
cause proptosis and lid retraction (Fig. 2.17).
Fig. 2.17
Benign tumours
Capillary haemangioma
Presentation
• At birth or early infancy.
Signs
• Anterior orbit lesion that may appear dark blue or purple through overlying
skin and may be associated with cutaneous lesions (Fig. 2.18).
• The lesion may become engorged and slightly increases in size when the
child cries.
Fig. 2.18
Lymphangioma
Presentation
• Between the ages of 1 and 15 years.

• Most commonly located in the superior orbit.

• Involvement of the sinuses and oropharynx (Fig. 2.19B). Fig. 2.19A

Plexiform neurofibroma
Presentation
• Between the ages of 2 and 5 years.
Signs
• Non-axial proptosis which may be associated with eyelid or facial
hypertrophy (Fig. 2.20).
• The proptosis is pulsatile if there is an associated congenital defect in the
Fig. 2.20
sphenoid bone.
Look for
• NF1.
Optic nerve glioma
Presentation
• Between the ages of 2 and 7 years.
Signs
• Slowly progressive axial proptosis (Fig. 2.21A) associated with decreased
visual acuity and an afferent pupillary conduction defect.
• The optic disc may show swelling, atrophy, or opticociliary shunts
(see Fig. 12.45). Fig. 2.21A
• There is a disproportionate loss of visual acuity compared to the severity
of proptosis.
• CT shows a fusiform enlargement of the optic nerve with a clear-cut margin
corresponding to the dural sheath (Fig. 2.21B).
Look for
• NF1, which is present in 50% of cases with unilateral tumours and in
100% with bilateral involvement.
Fig. 2.21B

--- Page 48 of 404 ---
Orbital disease in children 49
Langerhans-cell histiocytosis
Definition
• A rare multisystem disorder characterised by proliferation of abnormal
histiocytes.
Signs
• Bilateral or unilateral bony lysis and soft-tissue proliferation which typically
involves the superotemporal orbit (Fig. 2.22).
Fig. 2.22
Other benign tumours
a. Sinus histiocytosis is characterised by massive painless cervical
lymphadenopathy and extranodal involvement of other viscera, with bilateral
or unilateral orbital involvement.
b. Teratoma presents at birth with massive proptosis.
c. Deep dermoid cyst presents in adolescence with non-axial proptosis. It may
occasionally rupture and cause inflammation.
d. Fibrous dysplasia typically involves the orbital roof and causes proptosis
with downward displacement of the globe. It may be associated with
McCune–Albright syndrome.
e. Juvenile ossifying fibroma arises from either the roof or the ethmoids. It
causes chronic painless proptosis with downward or lateral globe
displacement.
f. Osteopetrosis is a hereditary condition characterised by increased density
and thickness of bone with increased susceptibility to fracture. It causes
optic atrophy and bilateral proptosis due to shallow orbits.
Malignant tumours
Embryonal sarcoma (rhabdomyosarcoma)
Definition
• A rare but aggressive tumour derived from undifferentiated mesenchymal
cell rests that typically affects boys at around the age of 7 years.
Signs
• Rapid onset of progressive painful proptosis associated with chemosis and
lid oedema and erythema (Fig. 2.23A).
Fig. 2.23A
• The most common location is retrobulbar, followed by superior and inferior.
• CT shows a moderately well-defined homogeneous mass that is isodense
with extraocular muscles (Fig. 2.23B).
Fig. 2.23B
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 49 of 404 ---
50 CHAPTER 2 • ORBIT
Metastatic neuroblastoma
Definition
• An aggressive tumour that presents during the first 5 years of life.
• The primary tumour usually develops in the abdomen.
Signs
• Rapid onset proptosis associated with lid ecchymosis which is bilateral in
40% of cases (Fig. 2.24).
Myeloblastoma (granulocytic granuloma)
Fig. 2.24
Definition
• A localised form of acute myeloblastic leukaemia that has a predilection
for the orbit. It may occasionally precede systemic manifestations.
Signs
• Rapid onset of proptosis, sometimes bilateral.
• Eyelid ecchymosis and oedema (Fig. 2.25). Fig. 2.25
Other malignant tumours
a. Neglected retinoblastoma may occasionally present with orbital involvement
(Fig. 2.26), although occasionally the tumour may incite an orbital
inflammatory response mimicking cellulitis.
b. Metastatic Ewing sarcoma may cause rapid proptosis, ecchymosis and
chemosis.
c. Metastatic Wilms tumour which may be associated with aniridia.
d. Fibrosarcoma typically occurs in children who have previously received
radiotherapy to the orbit for retinoblastoma. Fig. 2.26
e. Sinus tumour invading the orbit such as Burkitt lymphoma and osteogenic
sarcoma.

ORBITAL DISEASE IN ADULTS
Congestive proptosis
Congestive proptosis is characterised by a rapid onset of lid oedema,
chemosis, conjunctival congestion, ocular motility restriction and,
frequently, pain.
Thyroid eye disease
Signs
• Proptosis is axial, unilateral (Fig. 2.32A) or bilateral, and is associated with
lid retraction or lid lag.
• CT shows proptosis and enlargement of extraocular muscles with absence
of space between the muscles and the optic nerve in the posterior orbit
(Fig. 2.32B).
Fig. 2.32A
Look for
• Injection over horizontal recti (see Fig. 3.56).
• Superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis (see Fig. 5.47).
• Restrictive ocular motility defects (see Fig. 14.40).
• Signs of optic nerve compression.
• Chorioretinal folds (see Fig. 13.284).
Fig. 2.32B

Orbital disease in adults 53
Carotid–cavernous fistula
Cause
• Head trauma or a spontaneous rupture of an intracavernous aneurysm.
Signs
• Unilateral, painful, pulsatile proptosis associated with a bruit and severe
chemosis (Fig. 2.33).
Look for
• Grossly dilated epibulbar vessels may be present in the absence of
chemosis (see Fig. 3.54).
Fig. 2.33
• Ophthalmoplegia.
• Retinopathy.
Cavernous sinus thrombosis
Cause
• Skin or paranasal sinus infection.
Signs
• Similar to a carotid–cavernous fistula except that the patient is usually
more ill because of systemic infection.
Metastatic tumour
Signs
• Rapid onset of diplopia, lid oedema and pain.
• Scirrhous breast carcinoma may give rise to enophthalmos.
Other causes
a. Orbital cellulitis (see above).
b. Idiopathic orbital inflammatory syndrome (see above).

• In middle age with slowly

Presentation
• With a combination of ptosis,

the walls of the mucocele

Lacrimal gland enlargement 57
Other causes
a. Malignant eyelid tumours may invade the orbit if neglected.
b. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma may invade the orbit through the ethmoidal
sinus and displaces the globe down and out.
c. Myeloma may involve the orbit as a solitary bony deposit (plasmocytoma)
or as part of generalised disease.
d. Leukaemia may involve the orbit in adults usually as part of established
systemic disease.
e. Lacrimal gland lesions (see below).
LACRIMAL GLAND ENLARGEMENT
Unilateral
Dacryoadenitis
Definition
• An uncommon condition that may be infectious or may be associated with
idiopathic orbital inflammatory disease.
Signs
• The upper lid shows a typical ‘S’-shaped curve with erythema of the skin
(Fig. 2.46A).
• The gland itself feels gritty and there is injection of the gland and adjacent
conjunctiva (Fig. 2.46B).
• CT shows opacification that extends beyond the gland itself (Fig. 2.46C).
Fig. 2.46C
Fig. 2.46A Fig. 2.46B

58 CHAPTER 2 • ORBIT
Pleomorphic adenoma (benign mixed cell tumour)
Presentation
• In middle age with a long painless history.
Signs
• Fullness of the upper eyelid and displacement of the globe down and in
(Fig. 2.47A).
• A tumour of the palpebral lobe of the lacrimal gland may be visible to
inspection (Fig. 2.47B) and does not cause displacement of the globe.
• CT shows an oval mass with a smooth outline that may indent the globe
and lacrimal fossa (Fig. 2.47C).
Fig. 2.47C
Fig. 2.47A Fig. 2.47B
Lacrimal gland carcinoma
Presentation
• This aggressive tumour presents with a shorter history than an adenoma
and is associated with pain.
Signs
• Down and in displacement of the globe.
Fig. 2.48A
• Posterior extension may give rise to proptosis (Fig. 2.48A), epibulbar
congestion, periocular oedema and ophthalmoplegia.
• CT shows a globular mass with an irregular serrated border, often with
contiguous erosion or invasion of bone (Fig. 2.48B), and occasionally spotty
calcification.

Orbital rim lesions 59
Dacryops
Definition
• An epithelial cyst caused by obstruction and dilatation of major lacrimal
ducts.
Signs
• On eversion of the lid a bluish dome-shaped lesion is apparent (Fig. 2.49).
Fig. 2.49
Bilateral
a. Physiological enlargement associated with shallow orbits.
b. Late thyroid eye disease.
c. Sarcoidosis may cause bilateral firm enlarged lacrimal glands.
d. Lymphomas typically cause a firm or rubbery enlargement which may be
unilateral or bilateral.
e. Sjögren syndrome in which bilateral lacrimal gland enlargement is
associated with dry eyes and xerostomia.
Fig. 2.50
f. Mikulicz syndrome is characterised by hypertrophy of lacrimal and salivary
glands associated with dry eyes and xerostomia (Fig. 2.50). It may be
associated with other diseases such as Sjögren syndrome, sarcoidosis,
systemic lupus erythematosus, leukaemia, lymphoma and tuberculosis.
g. Amyloidosis may infiltrate the lacrimal glands.
ORBITAL RIM LESIONS
Orbital rim lesions are almost always unilateral and may occur anywhere
around the orbital rim. The causes are as follows:
a. Superficial dermoid cyst is an asymptomatic firm round lesion, most
frequently located in the upper outer quadrant of the orbital margin
(Fig. 2.51).
b. Subperiosteal haematoma is caused by severe trauma.
Fig. 2.51
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 59 of 404 ---
60 CHAPTER 2 • ORBIT
c. Subperiosteal abscess is very painful and is associated with orbital
cellulitis. It is very dangerous because of the potential for rapid
progression and intracranial extension.
DYNAMIC PROPTOSIS
Intermittent
a. Orbital varices cause a non-pulsatile proptosis which may be accentuated
by the Valsalva manoeuvre (see Fig. 2.38).
b. Mucocele may result in proptosis which fluctuates when its walls become
inflamed.
c. Capillary haemangioma may be associated with increase in proptosis on
crying.
d. Lymphangioma may cause a proptosis which increases during or after an
upper respiratory tract infection.
Pulsatile
a. Not associated with a bruit is due to transmitted CSF pulsation. It may be
caused by encephalocele in which the pulsation is transmitted to the orbit
through a defect in the sphenoid bone, or an orbital roof fracture.
b. Associated with a bruit due to arterial pulsation. It may be caused by
carotid–cavernous fistula or a congenital arteriovenous communication
which may occur in isolation or as part of Wyburn-Mason syndrome.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Conjunctivitis 63
CONJUNCTIVITIS
Signs of conjunctival inflammation
Conjunctival injection
The conjunctival vessels are most superficial and thin, and apart from the
major arteries and veins, are virtually invisible to the naked eye.
Signs
• In conjunctivitis the injection is diffuse, beefy-red and is least at the limbus
and maximal towards the fornices (Fig. 3.1).
Fig. 3.1
Papillary reaction
Papillae are hyperaemic flat-topped elevations separated by pale outlines
which occur on the palpebral tarsal conjunctiva and occasionally on the
perilimbal conjunctiva. The clinical appearance of papillae varies greatly
according to size and number, and can be arbitrarily graded 0–4.
• Grade 1 – micropapillae are tiny slightly elevated red dots that give rise to
a smooth velvety appearance (Fig. 3.2).
• Grade 2 – macropapillae are less than 1 mm in diameter (Fig. 3.3).
• Grade 3 – giant papillae are more than 1 mm in diameter (Fig. 3.4).
Fig. 3.2
• Grade 4 – enormous and protruding (Fig. 3.5).
Fig. 3.3 Fig. 3.4 Fig. 3.5
Follicular reaction
Signs
• Discrete, yellowish, slightly elevated lesions most prominent in the fornices
(Fig. 3.6).
• Blood vessels normally pass over the surface of a follicle and then as it
enlarges the vessels are displaced peripherally.
Fig. 3.6
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 62 of 404 ---
64 CHAPTER 3 • CONJUNCTIVA
Discharge
a. Watery occurs in acute viral and allergic disease.
b. Mucoid is typical of chronic allergic disease and dry eye.
c. Mucopurulent occurs in acute bacterial and chlamydial conjunctivitis.
d. Purulent is typical of gonococcal infection.
Membranes
Signs
• Coagulated exudate adherent to inflamed conjunctival epithelium (Fig. 3.7).
• Clinically, a true membrane causes bleeding on attempted removal and a
pseudo-membrane does not, but this rule is not infallible.
Causes
The causes of true membranes and pseudo-membranes are essentially
similar:
• Severe adenoviral conjunctivitis.
Fig. 3.7
• Gonococcal conjunctivitis.
• Ligneous conjunctivitis.
• Acute Stevens–Johnson syndrome.
• Bacterial infection with Streptococcus spp. and Corynebacterium
diphtheriae.
Haemorrhages
Causes
Adenoviral infection and occasionally bacterial infections caused by
S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae and N. meningitis.
Preauricular lymphadenopathy ( )
Fig. 3.8
Causes
• Viral infection.
• Chlamydial infection.
• Gonococcal infection.
• Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome.
Fig. 3.8
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Conjunctivitis 65
Acute papillary conjunctivitis
Simple bacterial
Signs
• Eyelids – crusty and covered by
exudate (Fig. 3.9).
• Papillae – grade 1 (bilateral).
• Discharge – initially catarrhal and
later mucopurulent (Fig. 3.10).
• Membranes – usually absent.
Fig. 3.9
• Cornea – normal.
Fig. 3.10
• Preauricular adenopathy – absent.
Gonococcal
Signs
• Eyelids – oedematous.
• Papillae – grade 1 (bilateral).
• Discharge – copious purulent.
• Membranes – present.
• Cornea – ulceration if treatment is delayed (Fig. 3.11).
• Preauricular adenopathy – present.
Look for
Fig. 3.11
• In males – urethral discharge and dysuria.
• In females – vaginal discharge and less frequently dysuria.
Allergic rhinoconjunctivitis
Signs
• Eyelids – oedematous.
• Papillae – grade 1 (bilateral) and often associated with chemosis (Fig. 3.12).
• Discharge – watery.
• Membranes – absent.
• Cornea – normal.
• Preauricular adenopathy – absent.
Fig. 3.12

66 CHAPTER 3 • CONJUNCTIVA
Acute follicular conjunctivitis
Adenoviral
Signs
• Eyelids – oedematous.
• Follicles – (bilateral) most apparent in the inferior fornices.
• Discharge – watery.
• Membranes – if severe.
• Subconjunctival haemorrhages – if severe.
• Cornea – punctate epithelial keratitis is common. It may be followed by
subepithelial opacities and anterior stromal infiltrates (Fig. 3.13).
Fig. 3.13
• Preauricular adenopathy – may be present.
Herpes simplex
Signs
• Eyelids – vesicles.
• Follicles – (unilateral) most apparent in the inferior fornix.
• Discharge – watery.
• Membranes – absent.
• Subconjunctival haemorrhages – absent.
• Cornea – small dendritic ulcers are uncommon (Fig. 3.14).
• Preauricular adenopathy – present.
Fig. 3.14
Other viral causes
a. Epstein–Barr.
b. Measles.
c. Mumps.
d. Newcastle disease.
e. Rubella.
f. Picornaviruses.

Conjunctivitis 67
Chronic papillary conjunctivitis
Vernal keratoconjunctivitis
Definition
• A common recurrent bilateral condition which affects children and young
adults.
Signs
• Eyelids – normal or may show eczema.
• Discharge – mucoid.
• Papillae – on superior tarsus grade 2–3, rarely 4.
• Limbitis – grade 3 papillae associated with small white (Trantas) dots
(Fig. 3.15). Fig. 3.15
• Conjunctival scarring – absent.
• Cornea – superior corneal punctate epithelial erosions, epithelial
macroerosions, shield ulcers and plaques (Fig. 3.16), pseudogerontoxon
and peripheral vascularisation.
• Preauricular adenopathy – absent.
Fig. 3.16
Atopic keratoconjunctivitis
Definition
• An uncommon, serious, bilateral condition which occurs in adults with
atopic dermatitis.
Signs
• Eyelids – eczema (see Fig. 1.26), madarosis and staphylococcal blepharitis.
• Discharge – mucoid.
• Papillae – grade 1–2 most intense on the inferior palpebral conjunctiva.
• Limbitis – absent. Fig. 3.17
• Conjunctival scarring – featureless appearance of the superior tarsal
conjunctiva (Fig. 3.17).
• Cornea – inferior punctate epithelial erosions, persistent epithelial defects
(Fig. 3.18) and peripheral vascularisation.
• Preauricular adenopathy – absent.
Fig. 3.18

Definition
• A unilateral or bilateral condition, depending on the cause such as

• Eyelids – normal.

• Preauricular adenopathy – absent.

Conjunctivitis 69
Chronic follicular conjunctivitis
Adult inclusion (TRIC)
Definition
• Usually a unilateral condition caused by C. trachomatis serotypes D–K.
Signs
• Eyelids – normal.
• Discharge – mucopurulent.
• Follicles – large in the inferior fornices and less prominent in the superior
tarsal conjunctiva (Fig. 3.22).
• Limbitis – occasionally limbal follicles.
Fig. 3.22
• Cornea – peripheral infiltrates (see Fig. 5.121).
• Preauricular adenopathy – present.
Look for
• In males – ‘non-specific urethritis’.
• In females – abacterial pyuria and cervicitis.
Trachoma
Definition
• A bilateral condition caused by C. trachomatis serotypes A, B, Ba and C
that is associated with poverty and poor hygiene.
Signs
• Eyelids – scarring, trichiasis and cicatricial entropion in advanced disease.
• Discharge – mucopurulent.
• Follicles – initially mixed with papillae (Fig. 3.23).
• Limbitis – may give rise to a row of shallow depressions (Herbert pits)
following resolution (Fig. 3.24).
Fig. 3.23
• Cornea – scarring.
• Preauricular adenopathy – absent.
Molluscum contagiosum
Fig. 3.24
Definition
• An uncommon unilateral condition caused by a poxvirus.

Signs
• Eyelids – umbilicated nodule on the lid margin (Fig. 3.25).
• Discharge – mild mucoid.
• Follicles – in inferior fornix and occasionally on the bulbar conjunctiva.
• Cornea – epithelial keratitis or micropannus in chronic cases.

Fig. 3.25
Chronic toxic
Causes
• Topical eye medication.
• Eye make-up.
• Variety of environmental pollutants.
Signs
• Eyelids – may show signs of contact dermatitis (Fig. 3.26). Fig. 3.26
• Discharge – watery.
• Follicles – initially the reaction is papillary; follicles develop after several
weeks and are most prominent in the inferior fornix and inferior palpebral
conjunctiva.
• Cornea – inferior punctate erosions.
• Preauricular adenopathy – absent.
Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome
Definition
• A rare usually unilateral condition that has many diverse causes (see
below).
Signs
• Eyelids – normal.
• Discharge – mucoid.
• Conjunctiva – ulceration or large follicles associated with granulomas
(Fig. 3.27).
Fig. 3.27
• Cornea – normal.
• Cervical and preauricular adenopathy – severe.
Causes
• Common – cat-scratch disease, tularaemia and sporotrichosis.
• Occasional – TB, syphilis and coccidioidomycosis.
• Rare – sarcoidosis, lymphogranuloma venereum and chancroid.

Conjunctivitis 71
Neonatal conjunctivitis
Neonatal conjunctivitis is defined as conjunctival inflammation occurring
during the first month of life.
Chemical
Presentation
• Within the first few days of life.
Cause
• Topical administration of silver nitrate at the time of birth.
Signs
• Mild diffuse conjunctival injection without discharge.
Gonococcal
Presentation
• Within 1–3 days of life.
Signs
• Copious purulent discharge (Fig. 3.28) which may be associated with
membrane formations.
Fig. 3.28
Chlamydial
Presentation
• Within 5–25 days but the interval may be longer if erythromycin has been
used prophylactically.
Signs
• Initially unilateral and then bilateral papillary conjunctivitis (infant cannot
produce follicles).
Simple bacterial
This may occur at any time.
Herpes simplex type 2
This is rare and may be associated with lid vesicles.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Cicatrising conjunctivitis in blistering dermatoses
Signs of cicatrising conjunctivitis
• Progressive subconjunctival fibrosis and obliteration of the fornices.
• Flattening of the plica and keratinisation of the caruncle (Fig. 3.30).
• Symblepharon formation (Fig. 3.31).
• Keratopathy resulting from drying, exposure and keratinisation (Fig. 3.32).
The following are associated blistering dermatoses.
Fig. 3.30 Fig. 3.31 Fig. 3.32

Conjunctivitis
• Affects the majority of cases.
Signs
• Oral blisters are very common (Fig. 3.33 and see Fig. 15.30).
• Skin blisters are sparse.

Conjunctivitis
• Is uncommon.
Signs
• Oral blisters are rare.

Stevens–Johnson syndrome
Pathogenesis
• Triggered by drugs or infections.
Conjunctivitis
• Is common and usually self-limiting although cicatrisation may occur in
some cases.
Signs
• Oral bullae with haemorrhagic crusting of the lips are characteristic (see
Fig. 3.35
Fig. 15.29).
• Target skin lesions involving the palms (Fig. 3.35) and soles are common
but blisters are uncommon.
Toxic epidermal necrolysis (Lyell disease)
Conjunctivitis
• Is common but milder than in Stevens–Johnson syndrome.
Signs
• Skin blisters are painful, transient and widespread, and resemble scalded
skin (Fig. 3.36).
Fig. 3.36
Epidermolysis bullosa
Conjunctivitis
• Is common.
Signs
• Skin blistering induced by minimal trauma (Fig. 3.37).
• Nail dystrophy, mitten-hands and feet with partial syndactyly are common.

• Is uncommon.

Secondary chronic ‘conjunctivitis’
The following conditions should be considered in patients with chronic ocular
irritation or discharge without an immediately apparent cause.
Chronic blepharitis
Symptoms
• Chronic non-specific ocular irritation, worse in the mornings.
Look for
• Hard scales and crusts around the lashes (Fig. 3.45).
• Atopic and seborrhoeic dermatitis, and acne rosacea.
Fig. 3.45
Keratoconjunctivitis sicca
Symptoms
• Bilateral non-specific chronic ocular irritation and mucoid discharge.
Look for
• Reduced or absent tear meniscus.
• Corneal filaments and mucous plaques (see Fig. 5.44).
• Interpalpebral staining of conjunctiva and cornea with rose Bengal (Fig. 3.46).
• Reduced tear film break-up time.
Fig. 3.46

• Bilateral, chronic, recurrent non-specific ocular irritation.

Symptoms
• Unilateral, chronic, mucopurulent discharge resistant to topical antibiotics.

• Pouting punctum and oedema over the canaliculi (Fig. 3.48).

Chronic dacryocystitis
Symptoms
• Unilateral, chronic, mucopurulent discharge and epiphora.

• Pressure over the sac causes regurgitation of pus and mucus.
Fig. 3.49

Definition
• An uncommon unilateral or bilateral condition which typically affects
obese men.

Haemorrhagic lymphangiectasia
Definition
• A rare condition that may occur spontaneously or be precipitated by trauma
or inflammation.
Signs
• Irregular sausage-shaped connections between a blood vessel and a
lymphatic channel so that the latter is permanently or intermittently filled
with blood (Fig. 3.57).
Fig. 3.57
Look for
• Involvement of the lids and orbit.
Sturge–Weber syndrome
Definition
• A sporadic phacomatosis characterised by facial naevus flammeus (port-

Signs
• Ipsilateral conjunctival telangiectasia (Fig. 3.58) or episcleral haemangioma.

• Glaucoma.
• Occasional heterochromia iridis.
Fig. 3.58

• Bulbar conjunctival telangiectasia often with aneurysm formation

PIGMENTED LESIONS
Flat
Freckle
Signs
• Small isolated spot of pigment (Fig. 3.63).
Fig. 3.63
Benign epithelial (racial) melanosis
Signs
• Bilateral, yellow-brown or brown-black, interpalpebral patches most
prominent at the limbus that can be moved easily over the globe.
• The pigmentation is often more intense around an intrascleral nerve as it
enters the sclera (Axenfeld loop) or the perforating branches of the anterior
ciliary vessels (Fig. 3.64).
Primary acquired melanosis Fig. 3.64
Signs
• Dark patches with indistinct margins which may involve any part of the
conjunctiva (Fig. 3.65).
• Malignant change should be suspected if the patches become nodular.
Fig. 3.65
Congenital melanocytosis
Signs
• Unilateral, subepithelial pigmentation that cannot be moved over the globe
(Fig. 3.66).
Classification
a. Dermal melanocytosis involves only skin.
b. Isolated melanocytosis oculi involves only the eye.
c. Oculodermal melanocytosis (naevus of Ota) involves the skin and eye (see
Fig. 1.47).
Fig. 3.66

Cause
• Long-term use of topical adrenaline (epinephrine) for glaucoma (now

Cause
• Deposition of silver caused by industrial exposure or topical medication

Signs
• Conjunctival pigmentation is diffuse and slate-grey (Fig. 3.68).

elsewhere is unlikely to be a

Invasive squamous cell carcinoma
Definition
• Slow-growing, locally invasive tumour which occurs most frequently at the

Signs
• Although similar to conjunctival intraepithelial hyperplasia the tumour is

Pterygium
Signs
• Triangular, fibrovascular, subconjunctival lesion that extends onto the nasal
cornea and is fixed to underlying structures throughout (Fig. 3.84).
Fig. 3.84
Pseudo-pterygium
Cause
• Adhesion of a fold of conjunctiva to a peripheral corneal ulcer or an area of
thinning.
Signs
• Similar to a pterygium but not necessarily located nasally and is fixed to
the cornea only at its apex (Fig. 3.85).
Fig. 3.85
Lipodermoid
Signs
• Large, usually bilateral, movable, subconjunctival lesion located at the
outer canthus (Fig. 3.86).
Fig. 3.86

Anterior non-necrotising disease 93
ANTERIOR NON-NECROTISING DISEASE
Without a nodule
Diffuse (simple) episcleritis
Signs
• Interpalpebral hyperaemia involving superficial episcleral vessels (large
tortuous and radially orientated) and deep episcleral vessels (smaller and
interlacing).
• Normal vascular architecture even after multiple attacks.
• Tenon capsule is separated from the sclera by oedema.
• Involvement may be generalised (Fig. 4.1) or sectoral (Fig. 4.2).
Fig. 4.1
Fig. 4.2
Diffuse scleritis
Signs
• Swollen eyelids and tender globe.
• Intense hyperaemia and distortion of the deep episcleral vessels (Fig. 4.3).
• Variable involvement of the superficial episcleral and conjunctival vessels.
• The inflamed sclera has a violaceous hue when viewed in daylight.
• Involvement may be sectoral or generalised.
Fig. 4.3
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 90 of 404 ---
94 CHAPTER 4 • EPISCLERA AND SCLERA
With a nodule
Nodular episcleritis
Signs
• Tender interpalpebral nodule
(Fig. 4.4) over which the
conjunctiva can be moved, and
the nodule itself can be moved
slightly over the sclera.
• Dilatation of the conjunctival and
superficial episcleral vessels
which will blanch on instillation of
10% phenylephrine.
• A thin slit-lamp section shows
that the deep beam is not
displaced indicating that the Fig. 4.4
Fig. 4.5
scleral surface is flat (Fig. 4.5).
Nodular scleritis
Signs
• Tender nodule which cannot be moved over the sclera (Fig. 4.6A).
• Dilatation of the conjunctival vessels, and the superficial and deep
episcleral vasculature.
• Instillation of 10% phenylephrine constricts the conjunctival and superficial
episcleral vessels but not the deep vessels (Fig. 4.6B).
• A thin slit-lamp section shows that the nodule displaces the entire beam
because the scleral surface is elevated (Fig. 4.7).
Fig. 4.6A Fig. 4.6B Fig. 4.7

For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Posterior scleritis 95
ANTERIOR NECROTISING DISEASE
Necrotising scleritis with inflammation
Signs
• Vascular distortion and occlusion
of the deep episcleral vessels.
• Painful avascular patches and
necrotic areas (Fig. 4.8).
• Exposure of underlying uvea
through thinned and necrotic
sclera (Fig. 4.9).
Fig. 4.8 Fig. 4.9
Scleromalacia perforans
Definition
• An asymptomatic condition that is seen exclusively in patients with
long-standing rheumatoid arthritis.
Signs
• Diffuse progressive scleral necrosis without inflammation resulting in
exposure of large areas of underlying uvea (Fig. 4.10).
Fig. 4.10
POSTERIOR SCLERITIS
Signs
• Associated anterior scleritis in about 80% of cases.
• Orbital inflammation and proptosis.
• Defective ocular motility.
• Vitritis.
• Disc oedema.
• Choroidal ring detachment and chorioretinal folds.
• Macular oedema, exudative detachment, intraretinal deposits, subretinal Fig. 4.11
exudation and a mass.
• Ultrasonography shows scleral thickening and fluid in sub-Tenon space
giving rise to the characteristic ‘T’ sign in which the stem is formed by the
optic nerve and the cross bar by the gap containing fluid (Fig. 4.11).
• CT shows scleral thickening and proptosis (Fig. 4.12).
Fig. 4.12

96 CHAPTER 4 • EPISCLERA AND SCLERA
CAUSES AND SYSTEMIC ASSOCIATIONS OF SCLERITIS
Surgically-induced
Presentation
Three weeks after a surgical
procedure such as:
• Strabismus repair.
• Scleral buckling.
• Trabeculectomy (Fig. 4.13).
• Application of mitomycin C for the
treatment of pterygium (Fig. 4.14).
Fig. 4.13 Fig. 4.14
Herpes zoster ophthalmicus
Signs
• Focal area of scleral atrophy (Fig. 4.15).
Fig. 4.15
Rheumatoid arthritis
Rheumatoid arthritis is by far the most common systemic association that
may be associated with necrotising scleritis with inflammation (see below) as
well as scleromalacia perforans (see Fig. 4.10).
Look for
• Symmetric, erosive arthritis involving the hands, ankles, knees and spine.
• Subcutaneous nodules (Fig. 4.16).
• Cutaneous vasculitis. Fig. 4.16
Relapsing polychondritis
Polychondritis is often associated with intractable scleritis that may be
non-necrotising or necrotising.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 93 of 404 ---
Causes and systemic associations of scleritis 97
Look for
• Inflammation and destruction of the pinnae (Fig. 4.17) and a saddle-shaped
nasal deformity (see Fig. 15.25).
• Stridor due to involvement of the larynx and trachea.
• Polyarthritis.
Fig. 4.17
Wegener granulomatosis
Wegener may be associated with rapidly progressive necrotising scleritis.
Look for
• Sinus inflammation and saddle-shaped nose.
• Lung cavitation (Fig. 4.18).
• Necrotising glomerulonephritis.
Fig. 4.18
Polyarteritis nodosa
Polyarteritis nodosa may be associated with aggressive necrotising disease
which may precede systemic manifestations.
Look for
• Arthralgia and myalgia.
• Purpura (Fig. 4.19), easy bruising and cutaneous vasculitis.
• Renal and cardiovascular disease.
Fig. 4.19
Systemic lupus erythematosus
Lupus is occasionally associated with non-necrotising disease and rarely with
necrotising scleritis.

• Facial rash with a characteristic ‘butterfly’ distribution (see Fig. 15.4).

• Hirsutism in females.

SCLERAL DISCOLORATION
Focal
Scleral hyaline plaque
Definition
• A common, bilateral, innocuous, asymptomatic age-related condition.
Signs
• Dark-greyish, oval area close to the insertion of a horizontal rectus muscle
(Fig. 4.21).
Fig. 4.21
Alkaptonuria
Definition
• A metabolic disorder that results in accumulation of black homogentistic
acid in collagenous tissues (ochronosis).
Signs
• Interpalpebral, bluish-grey or black pigmentation of the conjunctiva,
episclera, sclera and tendons of horizontal recti (Fig. 4.22).
Differential diagnosis
• Early scleromalacia perforans.

Systemic minocycline
Signs

Diffuse blue
Blue sclera is caused by thinning and transparency of scleral collagen fibres
allowing visualisation of the underlying uvea (Fig. 4.23). It may be associated
with the following systemic conditions.
Fig. 4.23
Osteogenesis imperfecta type 1 and 2
Look for
• In type 1 – joint hypermobility and cardiac valve lesions.
• In type 2 – multiple fractures, short limbs (Fig. 4.24) and early demise.
Fig. 4.24
Ehlers–Danlos syndrome type 6
Look for
• Thin and hyperelastic skin which bruises easily and heals slowly.
• Hyperextensible joints (Fig. 4.25) that easily dislocate.
• Dissecting aortic aneurysm and mitral valve disease.
Fig. 4.25

Nanophthalmos
Definition
• A rare, bilateral, congenital condition in which the globes have reduced
volume but are otherwise grossly normal.
Signs
• Very high hypermetropia (Fig. 5.2).
• Adult corneal diameter is reduced but the lens has a normal volume.
• Short axial length averaging 18 mm. Fig. 5.2
• Shallow anterior chamber.
• Thick sclera.
• Fundus may show a crowded disc, vascular tortuosity and macular
hypoplasia.
Late problems
• Angle-closure glaucoma.
• Uveal effusion characterised by choroidal detachment and retinal
detachment (Fig. 5.3).
Fig. 5.3
• Retinal detachment.
• Poorly tolerated intraocular surgery.
Microphthalmos
Definition
• An uncommon, unilateral or bilateral condition in which the axial length of
the globe is reduced.

Abnormal shape 107
Keratoglobus
Definition
• A very rare, bilateral, congenital condition.
Signs
• Mid-peripheral thinning, resulting in protrusion of the entire cornea
(Fig. 5.14).
• Very deep anterior chamber.
• Acute hydrops in advanced cases.
Look for
• A syndrome comprising blue sclera, hyperextensible joints and dental
abnormalities.
Fig. 5.14
Pellucid marginal degeneration
Definition
• An uncommon, acquired, bilateral condition.
Signs
• High astigmatism.
• Inferior, crescent-shaped area of peripheral corneal thinning, 1–2 mm in
width, extending from the 4 o’clock to 8 o’clock positions, which is
separated from the limbus by normal cornea (Fig. 5.15).
• Protrusion located above the area of thinning.
Fig. 5.15
Posterior keratoconus
Definition
• A very rare, usually unilateral developmental anomaly that bears no
relationship to anterior keratoconus.

Signs

Signs

• Forceps marks on the eyelids or cheeks.

Intrauterine rubella
Intrauterine rubella may cause corneal clouding as well as microphthalmos,
cataract, anterior uveitis, retinitis and strabismus.

• A rare, congenital, usually
bilateral but asymmetric
condition.

(Fig. 5.20).

110 CHAPTER 5 • CORNEA
Look for
• Peters anomaly (see next).
• Cornea plana.
Peters anomaly
Definition
• A usually bilateral, congenital condition that varies in severity.
Signs
• Central corneal opacities asymmetric in extent (Fig. 5.22) and an underlying
defect of the posterior stroma, Descemet membrane and endothelium.
Fig. 5.22
• May be associated with keratolenticular or iridocorneal adhesions best
detected by ultrasonography (Fig. 5.23).
Fig. 5.23
Congenital anterior staphyloma
Definition
• A very rare, congenital, usually unilateral condition.
Signs
• Opaque cornea lined posteriorly with uveal tissue that protrudes beyond
the eyelids (Fig. 5.24).
• Keratectasia is identical except that it is not lined with uveal tissue.
Fig. 5.24
Mucopolysaccharidoses
Mucopolysaccharidoses that occasionally may be associated with congenital
corneal clouding include:
a. Hurler syndrome (MPS I-H).
b. Scheie (MPS I-S) syndrome.
c. Morquio syndrome (MPS types IVA and IVB).
d. Maroteaux–Lamy syndrome (types VIA and VIB).
Signs
Fig. 5.25
• Diffuse corneal clouding caused by fine punctate grey opacities (Fig. 5.25).

Meesmann dystrophy
Definition
• A very rare, innocuous AD condition.
Signs
• Myriads of tiny epithelial cysts most numerous in the interpalpebral region.
• The lesions appear grey in direct illumination (Fig. 5.32) but clear on
retroillumination.
Fig. 5.32
Cogan microcystic dystrophy
Definition
• A common condition which is frequently associated with bilateral
spontaneous recurrent corneal erosion.
Signs
• Sparse microcysts, dots and fingerprint-like, or map-like epithelial lesions
seen singly or in various combinations (Fig. 5.33).

Oedema 113
OEDEMA
Corneal oedema is a common condition that may be caused by endothelial
disease or severe elevation of intraocular pressure in an eye with a normal
endothelium.
Iatrogenic endothelial damage
Cause
• Damage may occur during cataract surgery, particularly when associated
with problems with lens implantation (pseudophakic bullous keratopathy
– Fig. 5.34).
Fig. 5.34
Hydrops
Causes
• Advanced keratoconus (see Fig. 5.12) and occasionally keratoglobus
(Fig. 5.35).
Fig. 5.35
Fuchs endothelial dystrophy
Definition
• An uncommon bilateral condition which usually occurs in old age.
Signs
• Starts as tiny central excrescences of Descemet membrane (cornea guttata
– Fig. 5.36) which gradually become confluent.
• Subsequent endothelial dysfunction results in stromal oedema and bullous
keratopathy.
Fig. 5.36
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Acute angle-closure
Signs
• Corneal oedema.
• Severe elevation of intraocular pressure.
• Ciliary injection.
• Fixed dilated pupil.
• Shallow anterior chamber (Fig. 5.37).
• Closed angle on gonioscopy.

Disciform keratitis
Definition
• A unilateral condition associated with herpes simplex and occasionally
herpes zoster.
Signs
• Oval area of stromal and epithelial oedema with underlying keratic
precipitates (Fig. 5.38).
• Surrounding ring of stromal opacity (Wessely ring) may be seen in
long-standing cases.
Fig. 5.38
Acute postoperative endophthalmitis
Signs
• Endophthalmitis is frequently associated with corneal oedema (Fig. 5.39).
Look for
• Conjunctival injection and discharge.
• Fibrinous anterior uveitis and enlarging hypopyon.
• Impaired red reflex.
• Vitreous cells and debris.
Fig. 5.39

Punctate lesions 115
PUNCTATE LESIONS
Punctate epithelial erosions (PEE)
Definition
• A common, non-specific, unilateral or bilateral, condition with many diverse
causes.
Signs
• Tiny non-specific epithelial defects that stain with fluorescein (Fig. 5.40) and
rose Bengal.
Causes
Maximal intensity of may indicate the probably aetiology as follows:
a. Superior – vernal disease, superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis, floppy
eyelids, poorly fitting contact lenses, chlamydial infection and subtarsal Fig. 5.40
foreign body.
b. Interpalpebral – dry eye, reduced corneal sensation and exposure to
ultraviolet light.
c. Inferior – chronic blepharitis, lagophthalmos, trichiasis, rosacea, toxicity
from drops and self-induced.
Superficial punctate keratitis (SPK)
Definition
• SPK is the hallmark of Thygeson disease which is an idiopathic usually
bilateral condition that typically affects young adults.
Signs
• Coarse, distinct, granular, greyish, elevated epithelial lesions that may be
associated with a mild subepithelial haze (Fig. 5.41).
Fig. 5.41
Punctate epithelial keratitis (PEK)
Definition
• A unilateral or bilateral condition with many diverse causes.
Signs
• Small, multiple, frequently bilateral subepithelial opacities (Fig. 5.42) that
stain poorly with fluorescein but well with rose Bengal.
Causes
• Adenovirus infection.
• Herpes zoster ophthalmicus.
• Reiter disease. Fig. 5.42
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

• Hypersensitivity to contact lens solution.
• Epithelial corneal graft rejection in donor cornea.
• Microsporidia infection.
Nummular keratitis
Definition
• An uncommon, unilateral or bilateral, condition caused by a variety of
infections.
Signs
• Large, round, granular subepithelial deposits just beneath Bowman layer,
surrounded by a halo of stromal haze (Fig. 5.43).

• Herpes zoster ophthalmicus.

d. Chronic epithelial toxicity.

f. Latanoprost.

Exposure keratopathy
Cause
• Incomplete lid closure (lagophthalmos).
Signs
• Starts with inferior PEE.
• Followed by an inferior epithelial defect (Fig. 5.58).
• Stromal melting (Fig. 5.59) and perforation are rare.
Causes
Fig. 5.58
• Facial nerve palsy (Fig. 5.60).
• Eyelid scarring or tight facial skin that prevents lid closure (Fig. 5.61).
• Severe proptosis (Fig. 5.62).

Epithelial defects and ulcers 121
Microbial ulcers
Bacterial keratitis
Definition
• A serious unilateral condition which develops in persons with pre-existing
ocular surface disease or in those who wear contact lenses.
Signs
• White stromal infiltrate associated with an overlying epithelial defect
(Fig. 5.64) and secondary anterior uveitis.
• Enlargement of stromal infiltration associated with stromal oedema
(Fig. 5.65).
• Severe infiltration and hypopyon formation (Fig. 5.66). Fig. 5.64
• Progressive ulceration and enlargement of hypopyon (Fig. 5.67).
• Corneal perforation and endophthalmitis in neglected cases (see Fig. 5.62).
Fig. 5.65
Fig. 5.66 Fig. 5.67
Candida keratitis
Definition
• A rare, unilateral, insidious fungal infection which usually occurs in eyes
with pre-existing chronic corneal disease or in severely debilitated patients.
Signs
• Grey-white infiltrate (Fig. 5.68) similar to a bacterial ulcer.
Fig. 5.68
Filamentous keratitis
Definition
• A rare, unilateral, insidious fungal infection which is typically preceded by
ocular trauma with wood.

Epithelial iron lines
a. Rust ring consists of residual rust following the removal of a metallic
foreign body.
b. Hudson–Stähli line occurs at the junction of the upper two-thirds and the

d. Stocker line occurs in front of a pterygium.

degeneration, corneal grafts and radial keratotomy incisions.

Salzmann nodular degeneration
Definition
• A usually unilateral condition which is always secondary to a chronic

Signs
• Discrete, elevated, grey, superficial stromal nodules which may be

Definition

DYSTROPHIES OF BOWMAN LAYER AND
ANTERIOR STROMA
Reis–Bückler dystrophy (Bowman layer 1)
Definition
• An AD condition that presents in the 1st or 2nd decade with severe
recurrent corneal erosions.
Signs
• Fine, grey-white, round and polygonal opacities in Bowman layer (Fig. 5.86).
Fig. 5.86
Thiel–Behnke dystrophy (Bowman layer 2)
Definition
• An AD condition that presents at the end of the 1st decade with recurrent
erosions.
Signs
• Similar to Reis–Bückler dystrophy except that the opacities have a more
honeycomb pattern (Fig. 5.87).
Fig. 5.87
Central Schnyder (crystalline) dystrophy
Definition
• An AD condition that presents in the 2nd decade with visual impairment
and glare.
Signs
• Central, oval, subepithelial ‘crystalline’ opacity composed of phospholipids
and cholesterol (Fig. 5.88).
Fig. 5.88
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Deposits in stroma and descemet membrane 127
Gelatinous drop-like dystrophy
Definition
• An AR condition that presents in the 1st to 2nd decades with severe
photophobia, watering and visual impairment.
Signs
• Grey subepithelial nodules consisting of amyloid that gradually become
confluent and involve the stroma giving rise to a mulberry-like appearance
(Fig. 5.89).
Fig. 5.89
DEPOSITS IN STROMA AND DESCEMET MEMBRANE
Metabolic
Cystinosis
Definition
• An AR condition characterised by widespread tissue deposition of cystine
crystals.
Signs
• Initially iridescent crystals in the anterior peripheral stroma.
• Subsequent deep and central involvement (Fig. 5.90).
Look for
• Retinal deposits which are rare. Fig. 5.90
Kayser–Fleischer ring
Definition
• A rare, bilateral condition that occurs primarily in patients with Wilson
disease and occasionally in association with other liver disorders, multiple
myeloma and carotenaemia.
Signs
• Circumferential, greenish-brown, peripheral band 1–3 mm in width located
at the level of Descemet membrane (Fig. 5.91).
Fig. 5.91
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 124 of 404 ---
128 CHAPTER 5 • CORNEA
Immunoprotein deposits
Signs
• Refractile or polychromatic crystals usually involve the entire stroma
(Fig. 5.92).
Causes
• Primary amyloidosis.
• Multiple myeloma.
• Waldenström macroglobulinaemia.
• Lymphoma.
Fig. 5.92
• Benign monoclonal gammopathy.
• Cryoglobulinaemia.
Mucopolysaccharidoses
Definition
• Lysosomal storage diseases that result from deficiency of enzymes
involved in the degradation of glycosaminoglycans.
Classification
a. Hurler syndrome (MPS I-H) is associated with significant stromal clouding
(see Fig. 5.25) which helps to differentiate it from Hunter syndrome which
has similar systemic features but does not manifest corneal changes until
later in life.
b. Scheie syndrome (MPS I-S) is associated with severe clouding in which the
cornea appears thickened and oedematous.
c. Morquio syndrome (MPS types IVA and IVB) is associated with variable
haziness.
d. Maroteaux–Lamy syndrome (types VIA and VIB) is associated with mild
punctate opacities.
Hypoproteinaemias
Causes
• Abnormal reductions of serum lipoprotein levels.
Classification
a. Familial lecithin-cholesterol-acyltransferase deficiency (Norum disease) is an
AR disease characterised by hyperlipidaemia, early atherosclerosis,
anaemia and renal disease. Numerous minute greyish dots are seen
throughout the stroma and are often concentrated in the periphery in an
arcus-like configuration (Fig. 5.93).
b. Tangier disease is an AR condition characterised by hepatosplenomegaly, Fig. 5.93
neuropathy, hypertrophic orange-yellow tonsils and lymphadenopathy.
Corneal cholesterol deposits cause diffuse clouding and focal deep stromal
opacities.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 125 of 404 ---
Deposits in stroma and descemet membrane 129
c. Fish eye disease is an AR condition characterised by elevated serum
cholesterol, triglycerides and very-low-density lipoprotein levels but
depressed HDL. Diffuse clouding is caused by minute grey-white opacities
that are more concentrated peripherally.
Non-metabolic
Chrysiasis
Definition
• The deposition of gold in tissues as a result of long-term administration of
gold in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis.
Signs
• Dust-like or glittering yellow-brown granules throughout the deep stroma
and Descemet membrane (Fig. 5.94).
Fig. 5.94
Argyrosis
Definition
• Discoloration of ocular tissues secondary to industrial exposure or topical
medication with a silver nitrate compound (now seldom used).
Signs
• Diffuse greyish-brown discoloration of Descemet membrane (Fig. 5.95) often
associated with conjunctival involvement (see Fig. 3.69).
Fig. 5.95
Phenothiazines
Phenothiazines are used as sedatives and to treat psychotic illnesses.
Occasionally long-term administration may cause innocuous corneal deposits.
Signs
• Subtle, diffuse, yellowish-brown granular stromal deposits (Fig. 5.96).
Look for
• Anterior subcapsular lens deposits (see Fig. 10.47) which may antedate
the corneal changes.
Fig. 5.96
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
