Signs
• Anterior stromal crystals (Fig. 5.97).

• Crystalline retinopathy (see Fig. 13.166).

Definition
• A sporadic, bilateral, innocuous condition.

STROMAL DYSTROPHIES
Lattice dystrophy type 1 (Biber–Haab–Dimmer)
Definition
• An AD condition that presents at the end of the 1st decade with recurrent
erosions which precede the dystrophic changes.
Signs
• Numerous fine branching subepithelial lattice lines composed of amyloid
associated with stromal haze that is most dense centrally but spares the
limbus (Fig. 5.99).
Fig. 5.99
Lattice dystrophy type 2 (Meretoja syndrome)
Definition
• An AD condition that is associated with systemic amyloidosis. Onset is in
the 3rd decade but recurrent erosions are uncommon.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Stromal dystrophies 131
Signs
• Lattice lines extend to the limbus and are more delicate and more radial
(Fig. 5.100) than in lattice dystrophy type 1.
Fig. 5.100
Lattice dystrophy type 3
Definition
• An AR condition that presents between the 4th and 6th decades.
Signs
• Thick ropy lattice lines with minimal intervening haze (Fig. 5.101).
Fig. 5.101
Granular dystrophy type 1
Definition
• An AD condition that begins in the 1st decade but vision is not affected
until later.
Signs
• Small, white, central deposits that resemble crumbs, rings or snowflakes
(Fig. 5.102).
Fig. 5.102
Granular dystrophy type 2 (Avellino)
Definition
• An AD condition that presents in the 2nd decade with recurrent corneal
erosions.
Signs
• Initial anterior stroma opacities similar to granular dystrophy type 1.
• Later posterior stromal lattice-like lesions (Fig. 5.103).
Fig. 5.103

132 CHAPTER 5 • CORNEA
Macular dystrophy
Definition
• An AR condition that starts in the 2nd decade and causes progressive
visual deterioration.
Signs
• Initial diffuse superficial central stroma haze.
• Later greyish-white, dense, poorly delineated spots (Fig. 5.104).
Fig. 5.104
Central cloudy dystrophy of François
Definition
• A rare innocuous AD condition.
Signs
• Polygonal, cloudy grey opacities separated by relatively clear spaces, in the
posterior stroma most prominent centrally, creating a leather-like
appearance (Fig. 5.105).
Fig. 5.105
Fleck dystrophy
Definition

• Meesmann
• Reis–Bückler dystrophy (Bowman layer 1)
• Thiel–Behnke dystrophy (Bowman layer 2)
• Lattice dystrophy type 1 (Biber–Haab–Dimmer)
• Granular dystrophy type 1
• Macular dystrophy
• Gelatinous drop-like dystrophy

• Epithelial basement membrane (Cogan) dystrophy

• Granular dystrophy type 2 (Avellino)

• Lattice dystrophy type 2 (Meretoja syndrome)
• Lattice dystrophy type 3
• Fuchs endothelial dystrophy

Peripheral lesions 135
Corneal arcus
Definition
• A very common, bilateral condition which may be age-related (arcus senilis)
or associated with types II and III hyperlipidaemia in younger individuals
(arcus lipoides).
Signs
• White limbal band 1–2 mm in diameter with a sharp outline and a more
diffuse central boundary located in the superficial stoma (Fig. 5.114).
Fig. 5.114
Pseudogerontoxon (anterior embryotoxon)
Definition
• An uncommon, bilateral or unilateral condition which occurs at the site of
previous inflammation, most frequently associated with vernal disease.
Signs
• Superior corneal opacity with a cupid’s-bow configuration (Fig. 5.115).
Fig. 5.115
Posterior embryotoxon
Definition
• A bilateral condition which occurs to some extent in about 15% of normals,
in 80% of patients with Alagille syndrome and in all patients with Axenfeld–
Rieger anomaly (see Fig. 8.89).
Signs
• Thickened and anteriorly displaced Schwalbe line (Fig. 5.116).

--- Page 136 of 404 ---
140 CHAPTER 5 • CORNEA
Rheumatoid arthritis
Rheumatoid arthritis is the most common systemic association that may
manifest the following lesions which may or may not be associated with
adjacent scleritis:
a. Peripheral ulcerative keratitis (PUK) characterised by crescent-shaped limbal
ulceration with stromal infiltration that spreads circumferentially and
centrally and may also involve the sclera (Fig. 5.131); perforation may occur
in advanced cases.
b. Asymptomatic guttering in which there is peripheral stromal thinning with
intact epithelium unassociated with infiltration (contact lens cornea –
Fig. 5.132); perforation may occur in severe cases.
Fig. 5.131
c. Keratolysis is characterised by acute usually inferior melting (Fig. 5.133) that
can lead to perforation (Fig. 5.134).
Fig. 5.132 Fig. 5.133 Fig. 5.134
Other diseases
a. Wegener granulomatosis.
b. Polyarteritis nodosa.
c. Relapsing polychondritis.
d. Systemic lupus erythematosus.
e. Inflammatory bowel disease.
f. Leukaemia.
g. Vitamin A deficiency.

148 CHAPTER 6 • ANTERIOR CHAMBER
NON-INFLAMMATORY CELLS
Hyphaema
Hyphaema describes the presence of red cells in the anterior chamber that
may be caused by the following conditions.
Blunt ocular trauma
Trauma is a common cause of hyphaema due to bleeding from the iris or
ciliary body.
Signs
• The red cells sediment inferiorly to form a ‘fluid level’ (Fig. 6.22).
Fig. 6.22
Post-surgical
Mild hyphaema may occasionally occur following cataract or filtration surgery.
Causes of spontaneous hyphaema
a. Herpes simplex and herpes zoster
anterior uveitis.
b. Rubeosis iridis (Fig. 6.23).
c. Blood dyscrasias associated with
thrombocytopenia.
d. Iris tumours, particularly juvenile
xanthogranuloma, and
occasionally malignant primary
and metastatic tumours (Fig.
6.24).
e. Pupillary vascular (Cobb) tufts (see Fig. 6.23 Fig. 6.24
Fig. 8.65).
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 147 of 404 ---
Trabecular hyperpigmentation 153
TRABECULAR HYPERPIGMENTATION
Pseudoexfoliation syndrome
Definition
• An uncommon, usually bilateral, condition that predisposes to glaucoma. It
typically affects the elderly.
Signs
• Hyperpigmentation is most marked inferiorly. The pigment lies on the
surface of the trabeculum and has a patchy distribution (Fig. 7.1).
• A scalloped band of pigment may extend anterior to Schwalbe line Fig. 7.1
(Sampaolesi line).
• The trabeculum may also show ‘dandruff-like’ deposits of pseudoexfoliative
material (PEM).
Look for
• PEM on the pupil margin and sphincter atrophy giving rise to ‘moth-eaten’
and transillumination defects (see Fig. 8.79).
• PEM on the anterior lens surface (Fig. 7.2).
• Mild aqueous flare.
• Trabecular block glaucoma.
Fig. 7.2
Pigment dispersion syndrome
Definition
• An uncommon, bilateral, condition that predisposes to glaucoma. It
typically affects young myopic males.
Signs
• Hyperpigmentation has a homogeneous appearance and forms a dense
band involving the entire circumference of the meshwork uniformly
(Fig. 7.3).
Fig. 7.3
• The pigment is most marked over the posterior trabeculum. It is finer than
in pseudoexfoliation and appears to lie on and within the trabecular
meshwork.
Look for
• Fine pigment deposits on the endothelium (Krukenberg spindle – Fig. 7.4).
• Mid-peripheral radial slit-like transillumination defects (see Fig. 8.77).
• Partial loss of the pupillary ruff.
• Very deep anterior chamber.
• Pigment granules on the anterior iris surface.
Fig. 7.4
• Trabecular block glaucoma.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 148 of 404 ---
154 CHAPTER 7 • INTRAOCULAR PRESSURE AND ANGLE
Megalocornea
Definition
• A rare bilateral XL condition.
Signs
• Hyperpigmentation is similar to that in pigment dispersion syndrome and is
associated with a very wide angle (Fig. 7.5).
Look for
Fig. 7.5
• Enlarged but clear cornea that has a horizontal diameter ≥ 13 mm.
• Very deep anterior chamber (see Fig. 6.1).
• Glaucoma is uncommon.
Naevus of Ota
Definition
• A rare unilateral congenital condition.
Signs
• Hyperpigmentation homogeneously involves the entire circumference of the
trabeculum (Fig. 7.6).
Look for
• Hyperpigmentation of facial skin (see Fig. 1.47).
Fig. 7.6
• Episcleral pigmentation (see Fig. 3.66).
• Heterochromia iridis (see Fig. 8.97).
• Iris mammillations (see Fig. 8.42).
• Fundus hyperpigmentation (Fig. 7.7).
• Trabecular block glaucoma.
Fig. 7.7
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 149 of 404 ---
Trabecular hyperpigmentation 155
Iris melanoma
Melanoma may cause trabecular hyperpigmentation by two mechanisms.
a. Direct infiltration of the meshwork by neoplastic cells is by far the most
common (Fig. 7.8).
b. Indirect involvement by macrophages that have ingested pigment and
tumour cells, similar to pigment dispersion syndrome (Fig. 7.9).
Fig. 7.8
Fig. 7.9
Angle recession
Cause
• Severe blunt ocular trauma.
Signs
• Localised trabecular hyperpigmentation and irregular widening of the ciliary
body band (Fig. 7.10).
Fig. 7.10
Look for
• Rupture of the iris sphincter (Fig. 7.11).
• Iridodialysis.
• Lens dislocation.
• Cataract.
• Late glaucoma.
Fig. 7.11
Other causes
a. Pseudophakic pigment dispersion occurs as the result of rubbing of the
haptics and optics of a posterior chamber IOL against the iris epithelium
(see Fig. 8.78).
b. Subacute angle closure may be associated with heavy trabecular
pigmentation where the iris root has been in contact with the angle.
c. Anterior uveitis may give rise to scattered trabecular pigmentation.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 150 of 404 ---
156 CHAPTER 7 • INTRAOCULAR PRESSURE AND ANGLE
ANGLE CLOSURE
With pupil block
Primary
Pathogenesis
• Angle closure occurs in anatomically predisposed eyes.
• Aqueous flow from the posterior to the anterior chamber is obstructed
by relative pupil block resulting in iris bombé and closure of the angle by
the peripheral iris (Fig. 7.12).
Fig. 7.12
Inflammatory
Pathogenesis
• Angle closure occurs in eyes with chronic anterior uveitis in which seclusio
pupillae obstructs aqueous flow from the posterior to the anterior chamber.
• Increased pressure in the posterior chamber produces iris bombé and
apposition of the peripheral iris to the trabeculum and peripheral cornea
Fig. 7.13
(Fig. 7.13).
Lens-induced
Lens-induced angle closure occurs in the following circumstances:
a. Phacomorphic glaucoma caused by an intumescent (swollen) lens (see
Fig. 7.25).
b. Total dislocation of the lens into the anterior chamber either caused by
blunt ocular trauma or spontaneous dislocation of a microspherical lens
(see Fig. 6.2).
c. Incarceration of a microspherical lens in the pupil in which only part of
the zonule has been disrupted so that the intact zonule acts like a hinge
(Fig. 7.14).
Fig. 7.14
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 151 of 404 ---
Angle closure 157
Without pupil block
Inflammatory
Pathogenesis
• Angle closure is caused by organisation and contraction of inflammatory
cells and debris in the angle causes gradual and progressive formation of
peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS – Fig. 7.15).
Fig. 7.15
Rubeotic
Pathogenesis
• Angle closure is caused by contraction of fibrovascular tissue and
circumferential pulling of the peripheral iris over the trabeculum in a
zipper-like manner with the formation of PAS (Fig. 7.16).
Look for
• Ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion.
Fig. 7.16
Iridocorneal endothelial syndrome
Pathogenesis
• Angle closure is caused by the gradual formation of broad PAS that may
extend anterior to Schwalbe line (Fig. 7.17).
Look for
• Iris atrophy, corectopia and pseudopolycoria (Fig. 7.18).
• Diffuse iris naevus and multiple nodules (see Fig. 8.43). Fig. 7.17
• Hammered-silver endothelial changes.
• Glaucoma.
Fig. 7.18
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 152 of 404 ---
158 CHAPTER 7 • INTRAOCULAR PRESSURE AND ANGLE
Ciliary body tumour
Pathogenesis
• Angle closure is caused by anterior displacement of the iris (Fig. 7.19).
Fig. 7.19
Aniridia
Pathogenesis
• Angle closure is caused by pulling
forward of rudimentary iris tissue
over the trabeculum (Fig. 7.20).
Look for
• Tear film instability and dry eye.
• Conjunctivalisation of peripheral Fig. 7.20
cornea.
• Lens subluxation (Fig. 7.21).
• Foveal hypoplasia. Fig. 7.21
• Glaucoma.
Axenfeld–Rieger anomaly
Pathogenesis
• Angle closure is caused by PAS consisting of broad sheets of iris stromal
adherent to the cornea anterior to Schwalbe line (Fig. 7.22).
Look for
• Posterior embryotoxon (see Fig. 5.116).
• Iris shows ectropion uveae, diffuse stromal atrophy and corectopia
(Fig. 7.23).
Fig. 7.22
• Glaucoma is common.
Fig. 7.23
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 153 of 404 ---
Acute elevation of intraocular pressure 159
ACUTE ELEVATION OF INTRAOCULAR PRESSURE
Primary angle closure
Presentation
• Abrupt onset of unilateral visual loss and pain in an elderly person.
Signs
• Corneal epithelial oedema.
• Closed angle and shallow anterior chamber.
Fig. 7.24
• Fellow eye shows a narrow angle (Fig. 7.24) and a shallow anterior
chamber.
Phacomorphic glaucoma
Pathogenesis
• A unilateral condition caused by an intumescent (swollen) lens. Patients
have a long history of visual loss before the acute event.
Signs
• Corneal oedema, angle closure and advanced cataract (Fig. 7.25).
• Fellow eye shows normal angle and anterior chamber depth.
Fig. 7.25
Neovascular glaucoma
Pathogenesis
• A unilateral condition caused by rubeosis iridis, most frequently secondary
to ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion. Patients have a long history of
visual loss before the acute event.
Signs
• Advanced rubeosis iridis (Fig. 7.26).
• Fellow eye shows a normal angle and anterior chamber depth.
Fig. 7.26
Glaucomatocyclitic crisis (Posner–Schlossman syndrome)
Description
• A rare unilateral condition characterised by severe recurrent elevation of
intraocular pressure.
Signs
• Small keratic precipitates (Fig. 7.27).
• Both eyes show normal angles and anterior chamber depths.
Fig. 7.27
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 154 of 404 ---
160 CHAPTER 7 • INTRAOCULAR PRESSURE AND ANGLE
Secondary pupil block
Pathogenesis
• Seclusio pupillae caused by chronic anterior uveitis.
Signs
• Closed angle and iris bombé (see Fig. 7.13).
• Fellow eye shows a normal angle (Fig. 7.28) and anterior chamber depth.
Fig. 7.28
Other causes
a. Pigment dispersion syndrome may occasionally cause sudden elevation of
IOP and blurred vision, particularly after strenuous physical exertion.
b. Pseudoexfoliation syndrome is occasionally associated with unilateral acute
pressure rise despite an open angle.
CHILDHOOD GLAUCOMA
Primary glaucoma
Isolated trabeculodysgenesis (primary congenital glaucoma)
Presentation
• Within the first three years of life. It is bilateral in 75% of cases but
involvement is frequently asymmetric.
Signs
• Photophobia and blepharospasm (Fig. 7.29).
• Corneal haze and buphthalmos (see Fig. 5.7).
• The trabeculum and the ciliary body band are obscured by translucent
amorphous material (Fig. 7.30).
Fig. 7.29
Fig. 7.30
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 155 of 404 ---
Childhood glaucoma 161
Iridodysgenesis
Associations
• Aniridia, which is frequently associated with severe glaucoma.
• Congenital ectropion uveae (Fig. 7.31).
Fig. 7.31
Corneodysgenesis
Associations
• Axenfeld–Rieger anomaly and syndrome (see Figs 7.22 and 7.23).
• Peters anomaly (see Fig. 6.8).
• Microcornea (see Fig. 5.1).
Sturge–Weber syndrome
• Glaucoma occurs in about 30% of patients ipsilateral to the facial
haemangioma which is occasionally bilateral (Fig. 7.32).
• The pressure rise is caused either by an isolated trabeculodysgenesis or
raised episcleral pressure associated with an arteriovenous communication
in an episcleral haemangioma.
Fig. 7.32
Other causes
a. NF1 is associated with glaucoma in about 50% of patients with a plexiform
neurofibroma of the upper eyelid (Fig. 7.33) or those with facial
hemiatrophy.
b. Oculocerebrorenal (Lowe) syndrome is a very rare condition in which all
children have cataract and 50% glaucoma.
c. Mucopolysaccharidoses such as Hurler syndrome are occasionally
associated with glaucoma.
Fig. 7.33
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 156 of 404 ---
162 CHAPTER 7 • INTRAOCULAR PRESSURE AND ANGLE
Secondary glaucoma
Intraocular inflammation
• Chronic anterior uveitis most frequently associated with juvenile idiopathic
arthritis.
• Congenital rubella may cause glaucoma as well as keratitis, cataract, iris
atrophy and retinopathy.
Intraocular tumours
• Retinoblastoma may cause buphthalmos (Fig. 7.34).
• Juvenile xanthogranuloma which may cause spontaneous hyphaema that
may result in elevation of intraocular pressure.
Fig. 7.34
Ectopia lentis
Lens subluxation associated with the following conditions may cause
elevation of intraocular pressure.
a. Blunt trauma.
b. Marfan syndrome.
c. Weill–Marchesani syndrome.
d. Stickler syndrome.
e. Ehlers–Danlos syndrome.
f. Homocystinuria.
g. Sulfite oxidase deficiency.
h. Hyperlysinaemia.
i. Aniridia.
Other causes
a. Aphakia following surgery for congenital cataract is now a rare condition
due to refined surgical techniques.
b. Persistent anterior fetal vasculature (see Fig. 9.19) is a unilateral condition
that may be associated with swelling of the lens and secondary angle
closure.

Anterior uveitis 165
ANTERIOR UVEITIS
Clinical features
Acute
Signs
• Injection – intense and circumcorneal (ciliary – Fig. 8.1).
• Pupil – small.
• Keratic precipitates (KP) – endothelium dusting by myriads of cells.
• Flare and cells – often intense (Fig. 8.2).
• Fibrinous exudate – if severe (Fig. 8.3).
• Hypopyon – if very severe.
• Iris – usually unremarkable; occasionally shows dilated capillaries
(see Fig. 8.63).
Fig. 8.1 Fig. 8.2 Fig. 8.3
Complications
• Posterior synechiae (PS) – rare at presentation but may form later.
• Cataract – absent.
• Glaucoma – rare.
Chronic
Signs
• Injection – mild or absent.
• Pupil – unremarkable.
• KP – mutton-fat in granulomatous
disease (Fig. 8.4).
• Flare and cells – variable.
• Fibrinous exudate – absent.
• Hypopyon – rare.
• Iris – nodules in granulomatous Fig. 8.4
Fig. 8.5
disease, especially sarcoidosis
(Fig. 8.5).
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Anterior uveitis without systemic associations
Fuchs syndrome
Definition
• A unilateral idiopathic chronic anterior uveitis (CAU) with an insidious onset
that typically presents in early adult life.
Signs
• Injection – absent.
• Pupil – unremarkable.
• KP – small, grey-white, scattered throughout endothelium and frequently
associated with feathery fibrin filaments (Fig. 8.7).
• Flare and cells – mild to moderate.
• Fibrinous exudate – absent.
• Hypopyon – absent.
• Iris – small stromal nodules (Fig. 8.8), small pupillary nodules and stromal
atrophy (Fig. 8.9), and heterochromia. Fig. 8.7
• Vitritis – may be severe.
• Angle – fine twig-like vessels over the trabeculum (Fig. 8.10).
Fig. 8.10
Fig. 8.8 Fig. 8.9
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 160 of 404 ---
Anterior uveitis 167
Complications
• PS – absent.
• Cataract – common at presentation.
• Glaucoma – rare at presentation but common in long-standing cases.
HLA-B27 associated
HLA-B27 patients may suffer from severe recurrent acute anterior uveitis
(AAU) often associated with a fibrinous exudate (see Fig. 8.3).
Lens-induced
• Phacoanaphylactic endophthalmitis – bilateral CAU.
• Phacogenic – unilateral CAU.
Drug-induced
Drugs that may cause bilateral AAU are:
• Rifabutin.
• Cidofovir.
Posner–Schlossman syndrome
Definition
• A rare condition characterised by recurrent unilateral attacks of severe
elevation of intraocular pressure associated with mild AAU and small KP
(see Fig. 7.27).
Systemic associations of anterior uveitis
Ankylosing spondylitis
Incidence
• Severe recurrent AAU affects about 30% of cases and is frequently
associated with a fibrinous exudate.
Look for
• HLA-B27 – positive in 90% of cases.
• Pain and stiffness in the lower back or buttocks, more marked in the
mornings due to sacroiliitis.
• Rigid neck with lack of extension (Fig. 8.11).
Fig. 8.11
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 161 of 404 ---
168 CHAPTER 8 • IRIS
• Limitation of spinal movements due to syndesmophyte formation and
ossification of anterior spinal ligaments (bamboo spine – Fig. 8.12).
Fig. 8.12
Reiter syndrome
Incidence
• AAU is common but acute conjunctivitis is universal.
Look for
• HLA-B27 – positive in 85%.
• Urethritis is the presenting feature.
• Painless mouth ulceration.
• Asymmetrical arthritis most frequently involving the knees and ankles.
• Circinate balanitis (Fig. 8.13).
• Keratoderma blenorrhagica involving the palms and soles (see Fig. 3.40).
• Sacroiliitis, particularly in carriers of HLA-B27, resulting in irregularity and
loss of cortical margins with subsequent sclerosis, narrowing and fusion
(Fig. 8.14).
Fig. 8.13
Fig. 8.14
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Anterior uveitis 169
Psoriatic arthritis
Incidence
• AAU is uncommon.
Look for
• HLA-B27 – uncommon.
• Well-demarcated, scaly patches on extensor surfaces.
• Asymmetric arthritis involving the knuckles and distal interphalangeal joints
Fig. 8.15
that may result in severe deformity (arthritis mutilans – Fig. 8.15).
• Nail dystrophy (Fig. 8.16).
Fig. 8.16
Behçet disease
Incidence
• AAU associated with hypopyon is common but the eye usually lacks ciliary
injection (Fig. 8.17).
Look for
• HLA-B51 – common.
• Recurrent painful oral (Fig. 8.18) and genital ulceration (see Figs 15.99 and
15.100).
• Cutaneous hypersensitivity (dermatographia – Fig. 8.19), erythema nodosum
and acneiform lesions.
• Recurrent thrombophlebitis.
Fig. 8.17 Fig. 8.18 Fig. 8.19

Acute sarcoidosis
Incidence
• AAU is uncommon.
Look for
• Löfgren syndrome – fever, erythema nodosum (Fig. 8.20) and hilar
lymphadenopathy (Fig. 8.21).
• Heerfordt syndrome – fever and parotid enlargement (Fig. 8.22).

Fig. 8.20
Chronic sarcoidosis
Incidence
• Granulomatous anterior uveitis
(GAU) is common.
Look for
• Cutaneous lesions – lupus pernio
and granuloma (Fig. 8.23).
• Pulmonary disease – parenchymal
infiltrates and fibrosis (Fig. 8.24).
• Neurological lesions – multifocal
Fig. 8.24
peripheral neuropathy with a
predilection for the facial nerve,
Fig. 8.23
meningeal infiltration and CNS
granulomas.
Vogt–Koyanagi–Harada syndrome
Incidence
• Anterior uveitis which is initially acute and then becomes chronic and
granulomatous is common.
Look for
• Hispanic, Japanese or pigmented individual.
• Alopecia, poliosis and vitiligo (Fig. 8.25).

Other associations
a. Tuberculosis – CAU and GAU.
b. Acquired syphilis – AAU and GAU.

IRIS SPOTS AND NODULES
Solitary
Naevus
Signs
• Unilateral flat or slightly elevated
pigmented lesion involving the
superficial layers of the iris
(Fig. 8.30).
• Mild pupillary distortion and
ectropion uveae are occasionally
seen.
• In the Cogan–Reese syndrome
the naevus is more diffuse,
obscures the normal pattern Fig. 8.30 Fig. 8.31
of iris crypts and gives rise to
hyperchromic heterochromia
(Fig. 8.31).
Melanoma
Definition
• A very slow-growing tumour of relatively low malignancy which typically
presents during the 5th decade of life.
Signs
• Pigmented or non-pigmented nodule, at least 3 mm in diameter and 1 mm
in thickness, located in the inferior half of the iris (Fig. 8.32).
Fig. 8.32
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 166 of 404 ---
Iris spots and nodules 173
• Vascularisation is easier to detect in a non-pigmented tumour (Fig. 8.33).
• Pupillary distortion, ectropion uveae and secondary lens opacities.
• Angle involvement may give rise to raised intraocular pressure (see Figs
7.8 and 7.9).
Fig. 8.33
Leiomyoma
Signs
• Similar to an amelanotic melanoma except that it is not confined to the
inferior part of the iris (Fig. 8.34).
Fig. 8.34
Melanocytoma
Signs
• Black friable nodule with a mossy granular surface that has a predilection
for the inferior iris root (Fig. 8.35).
• Aqueous seeding may cause elevation of intraocular pressure.
Fig. 8.35
Metastatic carcinoma
Signs
• Fast-growing amelanotic lesion anywhere on the iris (Fig. 8.36).
• Anterior uveitis and pain are common.
Fig. 8.36

Forward extension of ciliary body melanoma
Signs
• May mimic an iris melanoma
(Fig. 8.37).

Fig. 8.37
Fig. 8.38
Multiple
Freckles
Incidence
• Present in 50% of adults but rare before the age of 12 years.
Signs
• Bilateral spots ranging in colour from yellow-tan to deep chocolate-brown
(Fig. 8.39).
• They are smaller than naevi and never distort the iris architecture.
Fig. 8.39
Brushfield spots
Incidence
• Present in 85% of patients with Down syndrome and occasionally seen in
normals.
Signs
• Bilateral white or yellowish spots arranged in a ring at the junction between
the outer and middle third of the iris stroma (Fig. 8.40).
Fig. 8.40
Lisch nodules
Incidence
• Present in virtually all patients with NF1 after the age of 16 years.

RUBEOSIS IRIDIS
Signs
• Tiny dilated capillary tufts or red spots at the pupillary margins (Fig. 8.61).
• Radial surface iris neovascularisation (Fig. 8.62).
Causes
• Ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion.
• Diabetes mellitus.
• Chronic intraocular inflammation.
• Central retinal artery occlusion.
Fig. 8.61
• Ocular ischaemic syndrome.
• Intraocular tumours.
• Long-standing retinal detachment.
• Retinopathy of prematurity.
Differential diagnosis
• Dilated iris capillaries (Fig. 8.63) associated with various types of anterior
uveitis. When seen in patients with secondary syphilis they are referred to
as roseolae.
• Iris haemangioma or vascular malformation (Fig. 8.64).
Fig. 8.62
• Pupillary neovascular (Cobb) tufts are tiny lesions which may cause
spontaneous hyphaema (Fig. 8.65).

Iris atrophy and translucency 181
IRIS ATROPHY AND TRANSLUCENCY
Fuchs syndrome
Signs
• Unilateral diffuse transillumination defect due to atrophy of the pigment
epithelium (Fig. 8.66).
Fig. 8.66
Albinism
Classification
• Albinism may be ocular, in which only the eyes are involved, or
oculocutaneous which involves the eyes, skin and hair (Fig. 8.67); the latter
may be tyrosinase-negative or tyrosinase-positive.
Signs
a. Tyrosinase-negative oculocutaneous albinism manifests gross translucency
of a blue iris with visibility of the edge of the lens and ciliary processes
(Fig. 8.68).
b. Tyrosinase-positive oculocutaneous albinism shows milder translucency
(Fig. 8.69).
c. Ocular albinos show variable translucency.
d. Carriers of X-linked ocular albinism may show mild translucency.
Fig. 8.67
Look for
• Photophobia and nystagmus.
• Fundus hypopigmentation and foveal hypoplasia (Fig. 8.70).
Fig. 8.70
Fig. 8.68 Fig. 8.69
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 175 of 404 ---
182 CHAPTER 8 • IRIS
• Chédiak–Higashi syndrome (recurrent infections).
• Hermansky–Pudlak syndrome (easy bruising).
Axenfeld–Rieger anomaly
Definition
• A rare, bilateral, non-progressive congenital condition.
Signs
• Diffuse hypoplasia of anterior iris stroma giving rise to prominence of the
sphincter muscle (Fig. 8.71).
Look for ocular
• Posterior embryotoxon.
• Pseudopolycoria.
• Ectropion uveae (see Fig. 8.58). Fig. 8.71
• Corectopia.
Look for systemic (Rieger syndrome)
• Dental anomalies – microdontia and hypodontia (Fig. 8.72).
• Facial anomalies – maxillary hypoplasia (Fig. 8.73).
• Redundant paraumbilical skin (Fig. 8.74).
• Hypospadias.

Post acute angle-closure
Signs

Iridoschisis
Definition
• A rare, usually bilateral but asymmetric condition which typically affects
elderly individuals.
Signs
• Intrastromal iris atrophy which is frequently inferior (Fig. 8.76).
Look for
• Shallow anterior chamber.
• Narrow angle and predisposition to angle closure; note peripheral iridotomy
in Fig. 8.76. Fig. 8.76

Definition
• An uncommon bilateral condition which typically affects young myopic men.

• Radial, mid-peripheral, slit-like transillumination defects (Fig. 8.77) are
characteristic.
Look for
• Krukenberg spindle (see Fig. 7.4).
• Iris pigment granules.
• Deep anterior chamber. Fig. 8.77

Heterochromia iridis 189
HETEROCHROMIA IRIDIS
In heterochromia iridis the irides have different colours. The condition may be
congenital or acquired and the affected iris may be hypochromic (lighter in
colour) or hyperchromic (darker). Rarely the irides of both eyes have sectors
with different colours.
Congenital
Isolated simple
Signs
• Hyperchromic heterochromia (Fig. 8.93).
Fig. 8.93
Waardenburg syndrome
Definition
• A very rare AD condition.
Signs
• Hyperchromic heterochromia (Fig. 8.94).
Look for Fig. 8.94
• Partial albinism.
• Telecanthus.
• Poliosis and white hair forelock.
• Synophrys or unusual facial hair distribution.
Parry–Romberg syndrome
Definition
• A very rare idiopathic condition characterised by progressive hemifacial
atrophy (Fig. 8.95).
Signs
• Hypochromic heterochromia is uncommon.
Look for
• Poliosis.
• Enophthalmos.
• Restrictive strabismus.
• Horner syndrome. Fig. 8.95

White pupil in childhood (leukocoria) 207
Incontinentia pigmenti (Bloch–Sulzberger syndrome)
Definition
• An extremely rare X-LD disorder that manifests skin, dental and ocular
abnormalities.
• Cutaneous bullae appear soon after birth (Fig. 9.21) and then slowly resolve
to leave a linear pattern of hyperpigmentation (Fig. 9.22).
Signs
• Leukocoria may be unilateral (Fig. 9.23) or bilateral.
• Retinal vascular abnormalities most marked in the temporal periphery.
• Total tractional retinal detachment in a minority of cases (Fig. 9.24).

208 CHAPTER 9 • PUPILS
Acquired
Retinoblastoma
Definition
• Retinoblastoma is the most common primary intraocular malignancy of
childhood. In hereditary cases the tumour is bilateral and often multifocal;
non-hereditary tumours are unilateral.
Presentation
• Within the first year of life in bilateral cases and around 2 years in
unilateral (Fig. 9.28), with an average of 18 months.
Signs
• Endophytic tumours grow into the vitreous cavity (Fig. 9.29).
• Exophytic tumours grow in the subretinal space and cause retinal
detachment (Fig. 9.30).
Fig. 9.28
Fig. 9.29 Fig. 9.30
Cicatricial retinopathy of prematurity
Presentation
• Retinopathy of prematurity is now a rare cause of leukocoria that presents
at about 4 months.
Signs
• Retrolental fibrovascular tissue and retinal detachment (retrolental
fibroplasia – Fig. 9.31) that may be bilateral.
Fig. 9.31

boys.

Abnormalities of shape 213
ABNORMALITIES OF SHAPE
Coloboma
Definition
• A rare, congenital, unilateral or bilateral, condition which may be
associated with other defects.
Signs
• Notching of the inferior or inferotemporal equator with absent zonules
(Fig. 10.1).
Look for
• Other colobomas and microphthalmos (Fig. 10.2).
• Ciliary body medulloepithelioma which may cause acquired notching of the
lens.
Fig. 10.1
Fig. 10.2
Microphakia
Definition
• A small lens (Fig. 10.3) that occurs in association with conditions that
cause arrest of ocular development such as microphthalmos.
• It may, however, be an isolated finding or associated with Lowe syndrome
(see below).

214 CHAPTER 10 • LENS
Lentiglobus
Definition
• A very rare, congenital, usually unilateral, condition which causes induced
myopia.
Signs
• A generalised hemispheric lens deformity which may be associated with
posterior polar lens opacity.
Anterior lenticonus
Definition
• A rare, congenital, bilateral, condition which is associated with Alport
syndrome which may be AD or X-LR.
Signs
• Localised anterior central conical projection (Fig. 10.5) that may later be
associated with opacification.
• In early cases the red reflex shows an ‘oil-droplet’ sign (Fig. 10.6) similar to
that seen in keratoconus.
Look for
Fig. 10.5
• Posterior polymorphous corneal dystrophy.
• Flecked retina (see Figs 13.232 and 13.233).
• Progressive deafness and renal disease.
Fig. 10.6
Posterior lenticonus
Definition
• A rare, congenital, bilateral,
condition which may occur in
isolation or in association with
Lowe syndrome.
Signs
• Localised posterior conical
projection of the lens (Fig. 10.7).
• Associated posterior with
opacification is common Fig. 10.7 Fig. 10.8
(Fig. 10.8).

• An uncommon AD widespread

• Bilateral, symmetric, slowly

life.

in older patients.

216 CHAPTER 10 • LENS
Look for
• Retinal detachment.
• Angle anomaly and glaucoma.
• Tall and thin stature with arm span > height.
• Arachnodactyly (Fig. 10.12).
• High-arched palate (see Fig. 15.38).
• Cardiovascular disease.
Fig. 10.12
Weill–Marchesani syndrome
Definition
• A rare, usually AR, disease of connective tissue, conceptually the reverse
of Marfan syndrome.
Signs
• Bilateral inferior subluxation may occur in late childhood or early adult life.
• Subluxation into the anterior chamber may also occur because the lens is
small (Fig. 10.13).
Look for
Fig. 10.13
• Short stature.
• Small, stubby toes (Fig. 10.14) and fingers (brachydactyly).
• Stiff joints.
• Mental handicap.
Fig. 10.14
Homocystinuria
Definition
• A rare AR condition caused by an
enzyme defect.
Signs
• Typically inferonasal subluxation
with disintegration of the zonules
(Fig. 10.15) occurs in all patients
by the age of 25 years.
• Lens incarceration in the pupil
(see Fig. 7.14) or total
subluxation into the anterior
Fig. 10.15
chamber may also occur.
Look for
• Coarse fair hair and a malar flush
(Fig. 10.16). Fig. 10.16
• Marfanoid habitus.
• Tendency to thromboses.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.

--- Page 206 of 404 ---
Ectopia lentis 217
Other causes
a. Ehlers–Danlos syndrome type 6 (ocular sclerotic) is an AR connective tissue
disorder in which ectopia lentis is uncommon.
b. Hyperlysinaemia is an AR inborn error of metabolism which is occasionally
associated with ectopia lentis.
c. Stickler syndrome is an AD condition in which ectopia lentis is uncommon.
d. Sulfite oxidase deficiency is an AR disorder of sulphur metabolism in which
ectopia lentis is universal.
Acquired
a. Blunt ocular trauma may cause subluxation and rarely dislocation into the
anterior chamber.
b. Ciliary body melanoma may cause a slight shift of the lens as well as a
localised opacity (Fig. 10.17).
c. Hypermaturity in which the cortex has liquefied and the hard nucleus
sinks inferiorly (Fig. 10.18); less frequently, the entire lens may be
subluxated (Fig. 10.19).
d. Degenerated eye (Fig. 10.20).
Fig. 10.17
Fig. 10.18 Fig. 10.19 Fig. 10.20
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Childhood cataract 221
Causes and associations of cataracts in neonates
Idiopathic
Approximately 50% of bilateral infantile cataract cases are idiopathic and 90%
of unilateral cataracts are idiopathic.
Hereditary
a. Anterior polar – AD.
b. Lamellar – usually AD, occasionally AR.
c. Central pulverulent – AD.
d. Sutural – XL.
Look for
• Similar opacities in family members.
Associated with other ocular anomalies
a. Aniridia (see Fig. 10.9).
b. Coloboma (Fig. 10.31).
c. Microphthalmos and persistent pupillary membrane (Fig. 10.32).
d. Peters anomaly (Fig. 10.33).
Fig. 10.32
Fig. 10.31
Fig. 10.33
Lowe oculocerebrorenal syndrome
Definition
• A very rare XL inborn error of amino acid metabolism.
• It is associated with microphakia and a variety of lens opacities, as well as
glaucoma in 50% of cases.

Galactosaemia
Definition
• A rare AR inborn error of metabolism caused by an enzyme defect.
Signs
• The lens has a characteristic central oil-droplet appearance (Fig. 10.37).
• Unless treated with a galactose-restricted diet, lamellar opacities develop
and maturity may ensue.

Childhood cataract 223
Look for
• Failure to thrive and lethargy (Fig. 10.38).
• Vomiting and diarrhoea.

Fig. 10.38
Hallermann–Streiff–François syndrome
Definition
• A rare sporadic disorder associated with cataract in the vast majority of
patients.
Signs
• The cataract is often membranous, the lens material having been resorbed
leaving residual chalky lens material sandwiched between the anterior and
posterior capsule (Fig. 10.39).
Look for
• Frontal prominence, sparse hair, micrognathia and beaked nose (Fig. 10.40).
• Progeria, short stature and hypodontia. Fig. 10.39
Fig. 10.40
Other causes
a. Maternal radiation during the first trimester of pregnancy.
b. Neonatal hypoglycaemia.
c. Neonatal hypocalcaemia.

Signs

Intermediate uveitis
Definition
• A common, chronic, frequently bilateral but often asymmetric condition that
typically affects young adults. It may be idiopathic or associated with
underlying systemic disease (Table 11.1).
Signs
• Vitreous cells (vitritis) vary in severity from mild to very severe (Fig. 11.2).
Look for
• Mild anterior uveitis.
• Inferior peripheral snowbanking (pars planitis – Fig. 11.3).
Fig. 11.2
• Vitreous cotton balls.
• Peripheral periphlebitis.
• Chronic cystoid macular oedema.

of intermediate uveitis

• Multiple sclerosis
• Whipple disease
• Lyme disease
• Cat-scratch fever
• Waldenström macroglobulinaemia

Fuchs syndrome is frequently associated with vitritis that must not be

d. Endogenous.

New vessel formation associated with the following conditions may cause

c. Sickle cell disease.
d. Eales disease.

b. Penetrating.
c. Iatrogenic during surgery for retinal detachment.

Vitreous opacities 241
VITREOUS OPACITIES
Common floaters
Innocuous floaters
Signs
• Muscae volitantes are bilateral, minute, multiple worm-like opacities best
seen by the patient against a white or bright background.
• Larger opacities are characteristic of degeneration of the vitreous gel and
are common in myopes.
Associated with acute posterior vitreous detachment
Signs
• Multiple cobwebs are condensations of collagen fibres within the collapsed
vitreous gel.
• Weiss ring is a discrete ring-like opacity located on the posterior vitreous
face consisting of glial tissue detached from the margin of the optic disc
(Fig. 11.15).
Fig. 11.15
Inflammatory
Cotton balls
Causes
• Intermediate uveitis (see
Fig. 11.3).
• Sarcoidosis – usually seen
inferiorly (Fig. 11.16).
• Candidiasis – cotton balls
composed of micro-abscesses
(Fig. 11.17).

Asteroid hyalosis (Benson disease)
Definition
• A common, usually unilateral, innocuous condition which is thought to
occur with increased frequency in diabetics.
Signs
• Numerous, tiny, yellow-white spherical particles throughout the vitreous
(Fig. 11.20).
Fig. 11.20
Synchysis scintillans
Definition
• An uncommon, usually unilateral condition which occurs in eye with
previous large vitreous haemorrhages.
Signs
• Numerous, golden-brown, flat refractive particles; in contrast to asteroid
hyalosis, the opacities tend to sediment inferiorly when the eye is
immobile.

Hereditary vitreoretinopathies 245
HEREDITARY VITREORETINOPATHIES
Associated with vitreous condensations
Familial exudative vitreoretinopathy
Definition
• A rare AD condition which is similar to retinopathy of prematurity but is not
associated with prematurity.
Signs
• Dragged disc vessels and macular heterotopia.
• Abrupt termination of retinal vessels along a scalloped edge at the equator
(Fig. 11.30).
Fig. 11.30
Autosomal dominant vitreoretinochoroidopathy
Signs
• Vitritis.
• Peripheral retinal pigmentation and neovascularisation with a sharp
demarcation between normal and abnormal retina.
• Cystoid macular oedema.
Autosomal dominant neovascular inflammatory vitreoretinopathy
Signs
• Vitritis.
• Peripheral retinal neovascularisation.
• Cystoid macular oedema.
Erosive vitreoretinopathy
Signs
• Nyctalopia.
• Progressive retinal pigment epithelial atrophy.

246 CHAPTER 11 • VITREOUS
Associated with ‘empty’ vitreous
Vitreous liquefaction results in an optically empty cavity except for a thin layer
of cortex behind the lens and avascular white membranes adherent to the
retina (Fig. 11.31). It is seen in the following conditions.

Stickler syndrome
Definition
• An uncommon AD connective tissue disorder.
Look for
• Congenital high myopia.
• Lattice-like radial perivascular pigmentation and sclerosis (Fig. 11.32).
• Retinal detachment caused by giant tears (Fig. 11.33).
• Midfacial hypoplasia (Fig. 11.34).
• Arthritis, cleft palate and Robin sequence.

Abnormal disc size 251
ABNORMAL DISC SIZE
Small
Hypermetropia
Definition
• A very common, usually bilateral, condition associated with reduced axial
length and smaller than normal scleral canal.
Signs
• Slightly elevated pink disc with a small or absent cup (Fig. 12.1).
• Crowding of central retinal vessels.
• Absence of capillary dilatation on the disc.
Look for
• Chorioretinal folds (rare).
Fig. 12.1
• Shallow anterior chamber.
Differential diagnosis
• Early papilloedema.
• Buried drusen.
Tilted disc
Definition
• A common congenital condition
frequently associated with
myopia.
Signs
• Oval or D-shaped disc in which
the axis is most frequently
directed obliquely (Fig. 12.2) but
may be horizontal or nearly
vertical (Fig. 12.3).
Fig. 12.2 Fig. 12.3

Large
High myopia
Definition
• Refractive error more than −6D and axial length more than 26 mm with a
corresponding larger than normal scleral canal.
Signs
• The appearance of the large disc may be exaggerated by surrounding
chorioretinal atrophy (Fig. 12.9).
Fig. 12.9
Congenital pit
Definition
• An isolated, usually unilateral, congenital, condition that is associated with
mild enlargement of the optic nerve head.
Signs
• The pit is a round or oval depression of variable size, most frequently
involving the temporal margin of the disc (Fig. 12.10).
Look for
• Serous macular schisis and detachment (Fig. 12.11) that eventually
develops in about 30% of cases. Fig. 12.10
Differential diagnosis of macular detachment
• Central serous retinopathy.
Fig. 12.11
Coloboma
Definition
• A unilateral or bilateral, congenital, condition caused by failure of fusion of
the fetal fissure that may be associated with a variety of ocular and
systemic anomalies (Table 12.2).

LARGE CUP
Large physiological excavation
Signs
• Large cup with a pink neuroretinal rim with absence of notching and
bayoneting of blood vessels.
• The inferior neuroretinal rim is the broadest followed by the superior, nasal
and temporal (‘ISNT’ rule – Fig. 12.16).
• The striations of the peripapillary nerve fibre layer can be seen up to the
disc margin.

OPTIC ATROPHY
Primary
Pathogenesis
• A process affecting the visual pathways from the retrolaminar portion of
the optic nerve to the lateral geniculate nucleus (Table 12.4).
Signs
• White, flat disc with a clearly delineated outline (Fig. 12.19).
• Reduction in the number of small vessels on the disc.
• Attenuation of the peripapillary vessels.
• Thinning of the retinal nerve fibre layer.
Fig. 12.19

Optic atrophy 257
Secondary
Pathogenesis
• Long-standing swelling of the optic nerve head.
Signs
• White or dirty grey slightly raised disc with poorly delineated margins
(Fig. 12.20).
• Reduction in number of small vessels on the disc.
• Surrounding ‘water marks’.
Causes
• Chronic papilloedema.
Fig. 12.20
• Anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy.
• Papillitis.
Consecutive
Pathogenesis
• Diseases of the inner retina or its blood supply (Table 12.5).
Signs
• The cause is usually obvious from examination of the fundus such as
retinitis pigmentosa (Fig. 12.21) and old vasculitis associated with Behçet
disease (Fig. 12.22).
Table 12.5 Fundus changes associated with consecutive optic atrophy
1. Vascular attenuation or sheathing Fig. 12.21

• Retinitis pigmentosa
• Toxic optic neuropathy
• Old vasculitis as in Behçet disease
2. Macular lesions giving rise to temporal disc pallor
• Toxoplasmosis
• Cone dystrophy
3. Chorioretinal atrophy
• Degenerative myopia
• Extensive panretinal photocoagulation
4. Diffuse retinal necrosis
• Cytomegalovirus retinitis
• Acute retinal necrosis
Fig. 12.22
• Progressive outer retinal necrosis

DISC SWELLING AND ELEVATION
Unilateral
Central retinal vein occlusion
Definition
• A common, usually unilateral, condition that may be associated with severe
visual impairment.
Signs
• Mild hyperaemic disc swelling.
• Venous engorgement, widespread retinal haemorrhages and variable
cotton-wool spots (Fig. 12.24).
Fig. 12.24
Papillophlebitis (optic disc vasculitis)
Definition
• An uncommon condition that may be a variant of central retinal vein
occlusion although it affects younger healthy individuals and has a better
prognosis.
Signs
• Severe hyperaemic disc swelling.
• Peripapillary haemorrhages, and variable venous engorgement and cotton-
wool spots (Fig. 12.25).

--- Page 248 of 404 ---
262 CHAPTER 12 • OPTIC NERVE HEAD
Signs
a. Early (Fig. 12.35)
• Mild disc hyperaemia and elevation with preservation of the physiological
cup.
• Indistinct disc margins and peripapillary retinal nerve fibre striations.
• The nasal sector is usually involved first, followed by the superior and
inferior sectors.
• Spontaneous venous pulsation is absent – this may not be significant
because it is also absent in about 20% of the general population.
• Differential diagnosis – hypermetropia and buried drusen.
b. Established (acute – Fig. 12.36)
Fig. 12.35
• Moderate disc elevation, blurring of the entire disc margin, absence of the
physiological cup and severe hyperaemia.
• Venous engorgement, flame-shaped haemorrhages and cotton-wool spots.
• Circumferential peripapillary retinal folds (Paton lines) due to fluid
accumulation.
• Differential diagnosis – malignant hypertension.
c. Chronic (Fig. 12.37)
• Marked disc elevation of the disc without haemorrhages or cotton-wool
spots.
• Telangiectasia, optico-ciliary shunts and drusen-like crystalline deposits
may be present.
• A macular star is common.
Fig. 12.36
d. Atrophic (secondary optic atrophy – Fig. 12.38)
Fig. 12.37 Fig. 12.38
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Disc swelling and elevation 263
Malignant (accelerated) hypertension
Definition
• Very high blood pressure associated with rapidly progressive end-organ
damage.
Signs
• Disc swelling, flame-shaped haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots and a
macular star (Fig. 12.39).
• According to the age of the patient there are also variable retinal
hypertensive vascular changes.
Fig. 12.39
Diabetic papillopathy
Definition
• An uncommon condition of unclear aetiology that typically affects young
diabetic patients.
Signs
• Mild hyperaemic disc swelling associated with surface telangiectasia
(Fig. 12.40).
Differential diagnosis
• Disc neovascularisation.
Fig. 12.40
Other causes
a. Compressive thyroid optic neuropathy may give rise to bilateral disc swelling
in the absence of significant proptosis.
b. Acute methanol toxicity is a very rare condition characterised by acute
bilateral visual loss and dilated pupils.
c. Bilateral, simultaneous, arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy.
d. Bilateral papillitis which typically affects children.
e. Compromised venous drainage may result in bilateral disc swelling in
patients with cavernous sinus thrombosis or direct carotid–cavernous
fistula.

Pre-papillary loop
Signs
• A usually unilateral anomaly in which an arterial loop extends from the disc
into the vitreous cavity and then back (Fig. 12.41).
Fig. 12.41
Bergmeister papilla
Signs
• A unilateral condition characterised by a raised glial tissue on the disc
(Fig. 12.42).

Acquired
Collaterals
Definition
• Veno-venular shunts that develop within the framework of the existing
vascular network to bypass an area of closure most frequently associated
with retinal vein occlusion.
Signs
• Distended flat vessels that start and end on the disc (Fig. 12.43).

Vascular abnormalities 265
New vessels
Definition
• New vessel formation may be unilateral or bilateral depending on the
cause (see Chapter 13).
Signs
• Fine, lace-like vessels that may extend onto adjacent retina (Fig. 12.44).
• The vessels may be flat or elevated above the disc and may be associated
with gliosis.
Fig. 12.44
Optico-ciliary shunts
Definition
• Usually unilateral anastomoses between the retinal and choroidal
circulations.
Signs
• Slightly tortuous vessels that disappear at the retina–disc junction
(Fig. 12.45).
Causes
• Optic nerve sheath meningioma (in 20–30% of cases).
• Optic nerve glioma (uncommon).
Fig. 12.45
• Chronic papilloedema.
Haemorrhage
Signs
• Peripheral flame-shaped haemorrhage that may be solitary or multiple
(Fig. 12.46).

• Acute papilloedema
• Anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy
• Papillitis
• Chronic glaucoma, particularly low tension
• Infiltrative optic neuropathy
• Following optic nerve sheath decompression
• In association with acute posterior vitreous detachment
• Optic disc drusen

Definition
• A unilateral or bilateral, solitary or
multiple, benign but vision-
threatening tumour that more
frequently involves the peripheral
retina than the optic nerve head.
Signs
• Orange-red mass that may be
associated with macular hard
exudates (Fig. 12.47).

with multifocal tumours.
Cavernous haemangioma
Definition
• A unilateral benign and innocuous tumour that more frequently involves the
retina than the optic nerve head.
Signs
• Clumps of thin-walled saccular aneurysms filled with dark blood vessels
reminiscent of a cluster of grapes (Fig. 12.49).
Fig. 12.49
Melanocytoma
Definition
• A unilateral, benign tumour that typically affects the optic nerve head of
heavily pigmented individuals.

‘Dragged’ disc 269
‘DRAGGED’ DISC
Retinopathy of prematurity
Definition
• A bilateral proliferative retinopathy that affects premature infants of low
birth weight who have been exposed to high ambient oxygen
concentrations.
Signs
• Temporal disc and macula dragging occurs in advanced cicatricial disease
(Fig. 12.57).
Fig. 12.57
Familial exudative vitreoretinopathy (Criswick–Schepens syndrome)
Definition
• A rare bilateral AD condition characterised by failure of vascularisation of
the peripheral retina, similar to retinopathy of prematurity, but not
associated with low birth weight and prematurity.
Signs
• Temporal vascular straightening and dragging of the macula and disc occur
in advanced cases (Fig. 12.58).
Fig. 12.58
Peripheral toxocara granuloma
Signs
• Vitreous bands extending from the lesion to the posterior fundus may
cause disc and macular dragging that may involve any quadrant (Fig. 12.59).

--- Page 263 of 404 ---
Vascular lesions 277
VASCULAR LESIONS
Haemorrhages
Preretinal (subhyaloid)
Location
• The haemorrhage originates from the superficial retinal vessels and is
located between the retina and posterior vitreous face.
Signs
• Usually solitary, round and frequently located at the posterior pole, where it
obscures all underlying retinal landmarks (Fig. 13.1).
• With time the blood may settle with gravity, giving rise to the typical
boat-shaped crescentic configuration (Fig. 13.2).
• In some cases, a large haemorrhage may break through into the vitreous
cavity.
Causes
Fig. 13.1
• Proliferative retinopathies (e.g. diabetic, following venous occlusion,
radiation, sickle cell, ocular ischaemic syndrome, retinopathy of prematurity
and vasculitis).
• Retinal artery macroaneurysm.
• Trauma.
• Subarachnoid haemorrhage (Terson syndrome).
• Valsalva retinopathy caused by a severe increase in venous pressure
brought on by strenuous physical exertion.
• Non-accidental injury (‘shaken-baby’ syndrome).
Fig. 13.2
Flame-shaped
Location
• The haemorrhage originates from the superficial pre-capillary arterioles and
is located within the retinal nerve fibre layer.
Signs
• Bright red, usually multiple haemorrhages spreading along the nerve fibre
layer (Fig. 13.3).
Causes
• Retinal vein occlusion.
• Hypertensive retinopathy. Fig. 13.3
• Background diabetic retinopathy.
• Retinal periphlebitis.
• Blood dyscrasias.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 264 of 404 ---
278 CHAPTER 13 • FUNDUS
• Optic nerve disease – acute papilloedema, anterior ischaemic optic
neuropathy and glaucoma.
Roth spots
Signs
• Flame-shaped haemorrhages with white centres (Fig. 13.4).
Causes
• Severe anaemia.
• Leukaemia.
• Dysproteinaemia.
• HIV microangiopathy.
• Bacterial endocarditis.
Fig. 13.4
Intraretinal
Location
• The haemorrhage originates from the venous end of the deep retinal
capillaries and is located within the sensory retina.
Signs (Fig. 13.5)
a. Dot haemorrhages are small and round, and located in the compact middle
layers of the retina; they may be difficult to distinguish from
microaneurysms.
b. Blot haemorrhages occupy the full thickness of the retina and are larger,
darker and deeper than dot haemorrhages.
c. Large blotchy haemorrhages are usually indicative of retinal ischaemia.
Fig. 13.5
Causes
• Background diabetic retinopathy.
• Retinal vein occlusion.
• Radiation retinopathy.
Subretinal
Location
• The haemorrhage is located between the photoreceptors and the retinal
pigment epithelium (RPE).
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 265 of 404 ---
Vascular lesions 279
Signs
• Usually large and bright red with slightly indistinct borders (Fig. 13.6).
• The retina overlying the haemorrhage is usually slightly elevated, but the
retinal vessels are clearly seen.
Causes
• Choroidal neovascularisation (CNV).
• Ruptured retinal artery macroaneurysm.
• Coats disease.
• Sickle cell anaemia.
• Blunt ocular trauma.
Fig. 13.6
Sub-RPE
Location
• The haemorrhage is located between the RPE and Bruch membrane.
Signs
• Slightly elevated, very dark-red, almost black, with relatively well-defined
borders.
• They are often solitary and located at the macula (Fig. 13.7).
Causes
• CNV is by far the most common (haemorrhagic RPE detachment).
Fig. 13.7
Choroidal
Signs
• Dark red and frequently extensive.
Causes
• Blunt trauma.
• Complication of drainage of subretinal fluid during retinal detachment
surgery (Fig. 13.8).
Fig. 13.8

Cotton-wool spots
Cotton-wool spots are accumulations of neuronal debris that results from
disruption of nerve axons. They appear as small, whitish, fluffy superficial
lesions in the post-equatorial fundus (Fig. 13.9). The causes are as follows:
Fig. 13.9

dilatation, and flame-shaped
and blotchy haemorrhages
(Fig. 13.10A).

capillary non-perfusion, blockage
by blood and pruning of vessels
in the affected area (Fig. 13.10B).

Signs

microvascular abnormalities
(IRMA), venous dilatation and
beading, and haemorrhages
(Fig. 13.11A).

silver-wiring and occlusion.

non-perfusion, punctate staining
of microaneurysms and
enhancement of the venous
changes (Fig. 13.11B). Fig. 13.11A Fig. 13.11B

Differential diagnosis
• Congenital myelinated nerve fibres – white feathery streaks within the
nerve fibre layer (Fig. 13.22).
• Retinitis – superficial yellow-white cloudy lesions associated with vitritis
(Fig. 13.23).
Fig. 13.22
Fig. 13.23
Hard exudates arranged in clumps
Retinal hard exudate formation is caused by chronic capillary leakage. The
exudates develop at the junction of normal and oedematous retina and are
composed of lipoprotein and lipid-filled macrophages. They are located mainly
within the outer plexiform layer. Clinically they appear as waxy yellow plaques
with relatively distinct margins, most frequently located at the posterior pole
(Fig. 13.24). The causes are as follows:
Fig. 13.24

For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Signs

Wet age-related macular degeneration

Vascular lesions 287
Differential diagnosis
• Macular drusen are bilateral, focal yellow spots which are not arranged in
clumps or rings and are not associated with retinal microvascular changes
(Fig. 13.31).
Fig. 13.31

290 CHAPTER 13 • FUNDUS
Neovascularisation
Neovascularisation is caused by angiogenic growth factors elaborated by
hypoxic retinal tissues in an attempt to revascularise affected retina. The new
vessels may be located in the central fundus or in the periphery.
Central and peripheral
Signs
• New vessels may involve the optic nerve head (new disc vessels – NDV
– Fig. 13.39) or develop along the major vascular arcades (new vessels
elsewhere – NVE – Fig. 13.40).
• The vessels may be flat or elevated, and they may be bare or associated
with fibrosis (Fig. 13.41).
• FA shows intensive leakage of dye and capillary non-perfusion (Fig. 13.42).
Causes
• Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (Fig. 13.43).
• Old branch retinal vein occlusion (Fig. 13.44).
• Ocular ischaemic syndrome. Fig. 13.39
• Radiation retinopathy.
• Retinal vasculitis (Fig. 13.45).
Fig. 13.40 Fig. 13.41
Fig. 13.42
Fig. 13.43 Fig. 13.44 Fig. 13.45
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 277 of 404 ---
Vascular lesions 291
• Previous retinal artery occlusion is an uncommon cause.
• Carotid–cavernous fistula.
Complications
• Vitreous haemorrhage that may be subhyaloid (Fig. 13.46 and see Figs 13.1
and 13.2) or intragel (Fig. 13.47 and Fig. 13.48).
• Tractional RD (Fig. 13.49).
Fig. 13.46
Fig. 13.47 Fig. 13.48
Fig. 13.49
Peripheral
Signs
• New vessels at or anterior to the equator (Fig. 13.50).

retina.

Causes

• Vitreous haemorrhage.
• Tractional retinal detachment.

Common
a. Fibrinoplatelet emboli are dull
grey, elongated particles which
are usually multiple (Fig. 13.55)

Vascular lesions 293
b. Cholesterol (Hollenhorst) plaques are often associated with carotid stenosis
(Fig. 13.57). They are small, glistening, usually multiple and typically located
at arteriolar bifurcations (Fig. 13.58).
c. Calcific emboli originate from cardiac valve disease. They are white, usually
solitary and commonly located on the disc (Fig. 13.59). They may give rise
to major arterial occlusion (Fig. 13.60).

a. Heart valve vegetations in bacterial endocarditis.

294 CHAPTER 13 • FUNDUS
Retinal vascular malformations
Retinal artery macroaneurysm
Definition
• An uncommon, usually unilateral condition which typically affects elderly
hypertensive females.
Signs
• Localised, fusiform or saccular dilatation usually arising within the first
three orders of bifurcation (Fig. 13.61).
• More than one lesion in the same eye occurs in about 20% of cases.
Fig. 13.61
Complications
• Haemorrhage with surrounding hard exudates are common (see Fig. 13.28).
Coats disease
Definition
• An uncommon, developmental vascular anomaly which typically presents at
about the age of 8 years with leukocoria (see Fig. 9.34) or strabismus.
• It is more common in boys than in girls.
Signs
• Vascular dilatation, tortuosity and aneurysm formation at the posterior pole
and periphery (Fig. 13.62).
• Retinal and subretinal exudate formation (see Fig. 13.30).
Complications
• Exudative RD and a retrolental mass which may give rise to leukocoria. Fig. 13.62
Differential diagnosis
• Late-onset retinoblastoma.
• Toxocara canis which may also be associated with hard exudate formation.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 281 of 404 ---
Vascular lesions 295
Congenital retinal macrovessel
Signs
• Unilateral, large, aberrant vessel, usually a vein, in the posterior pole that
may cross the fovea and horizontal raphe (Fig. 13.63).
Differential diagnosis
• Retinal racemose haemangioma (see Fig. 13.66).
Congenital arteriovenous communication
Fig. 13.63
Signs
• Unilateral anomaly in which both the vein and artery are dilated (Fig. 13.64).
• Location is in the papillomacular bundle or the superotemporal quadrant.
Fig. 13.64
Retinal vascular tumours
Haemangioblastoma (capillary haemangioma)
Definition
• A benign but vision-threatening tumour that may be solitary or multifocal.
• Both eyes are affected in 50% of cases.
Signs
• Round and orange-red tumour.
• Associated with dilated and tortuous artery and vein (Fig. 13.65).
Complications
• Hard exudates near the lesion or at the macula.
• Vitreous haemorrhage. Fig. 13.65
• Retinal detachment.
Look for
• Von Hippel–Lindau syndrome (see Fig. 12.48).

Retinal racemose haemangioma
Definition
• A usually unilateral, congenital arteriovenous malformation that may occur
in isolation or in association with systemic lesions (Wyburn-Mason
syndrome).
Signs
• Grossly dilated and tortuous arteries and veins which are more numerous
than in a normal fundus (Fig. 13.66).
Fig. 13.66
Retinal cavernous haemangioma
Definition
• A congenital, innocuous unilateral tumour.
Signs
• Clumps of thin-walled, grape-like saccular aneurysms filled with blood
(Fig. 13.67).

Periphlebitis
Signs
• Patchy, fluffy, white haziness surrounding the venous blood column
(Fig. 13.79) that may be associated with haemorrhage.
• Later there is venous sheathing (Fig. 13.80).

• Sarcoidosis – severe periphlebitis results in perivascular ‘candle-wax

venous occlusion (Fig. 13.83); in some cases periarteritis may coexist (see
below).

• AIDS – periphlebitis is frequently associated with CMV retinitis (Fig. 13.84).

Signs

• Acquired syphilis.

Frosted branch angiitis
Definition
• A usually bilateral condition that may rarely be idiopathic and more
commonly is secondary to CMV retinitis.
Signs
• Florid translucent perivascular sheathing of arteries and veins.
• Anterior uveitis, vitritis and retinal oedema are common.
Complications
• Venous occlusion (Fig. 13.87).
• Papillitis.
• Hard exudate formation.
Fig. 13.87

Toxoplasmosis
Definition
• A protozoan infection which
typically affects one eye of a
young individual.
Signs
• Anterior uveitis and vitritis.
• Solitary focal retinitis adjacent to
an old scar (Fig. 13.88).
• Inactive lesions are atrophic
scars with pigmentation
(Fig. 13.89).

302 CHAPTER 13 • FUNDUS
Toxocariasis
Definition
• A very rare worm infection that is acquired in early childhood; ocular
involvement is always unilateral.
Signs
• Posterior (Fig. 13.90) or peripheral granuloma that may be associated with
‘dragging’ of the disc (see Fig. 12.58).
• Chronic endophthalmitis (sees Figs 9.32 and 9.33).
Differential diagnosis
• Retinoblastoma may resemble a posterior-pole or peripheral granuloma.
Fig. 13.90
Candidiasis
Definition
• A fungal infection which is associated with intravenous drug abuse,
parenteral nutrition, chronic lung disease and neutropenia.
Signs
• Solitary or multiple retinal infiltrate.
• Vitritis and fluffy white ‘cotton’ balls (Fig. 13.91).
Other causes of vitreous cotton balls
• Intermediate uveitis.
• Sarcoidosis.
• Lyme disease. Fig. 13.91
• Whipple disease.
• Seeding of endophytic retinoblastoma.
Sarcoidosis
Definition
• A common granulomatous multisystem disorder which affects the eyes in
about 30% of cases.
Signs
• Anterior uveitis, which is most frequently chronic and granulomatous.
• Periphlebitis (see Fig. 13.82).
• Discrete, grey-white to waxy-yellow retinal and preretinal nodules (Lander
sign – Fig. 13.92).
Fig. 13.92

For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

• Multifocal choroiditis with panuveitis.

Behçet disease
Definition
• An uncommon multisystem disorder which is frequently associated with

Chorioretinitis without systemic associations
Sympathetic ophthalmitis
Definition
• A bilateral granulomatous panuveitis which occurs following penetrating
trauma.
Signs
• Chronic granulomatous anterior uveitis.
• Extensive multifocal choroiditis (Fig. 13.105).
Fig. 13.105
Multifocal choroiditis with panuveitis syndrome
Definition
• A unilateral or bilateral, frequently recurrent condition, that typically affects
middle-aged women.
Signs
• Anterior uveitis and vitritis.
• Small, discrete, old and fresh choroidal lesions located at the posterior
pole and/or the midperiphery (Fig. 13.106).
Complications
• CNV is uncommon.
• Subretinal fibrosis is rare.
Fig. 13.106

Infl ammatory lesions 307
Differential diagnosis
• Sarcoidosis.
• Acute lesions may resemble punctate inner choroidopathy but the latter is
not associated with vitritis.
• POHS may show similar lesions but is not associated with vitritis
(see next).
Presumed ocular histoplasmosis syndrome
Definition
• A fungal infection which is common in certain parts of the USA but rare in
Europe.
Signs
• No vitritis.
• Peripapillary atrophy and small, discrete, yellowish lesions often associated
with pigment scattered in the posterior fundus and mid-periphery
(Fig. 13.107).
Complications
• CNV is common.
Fig. 13.107
Differential diagnosis
• Paving-stone degeneration may resemble the peripheral atrophic lesions.
• Degenerative myopia may cause both the atrophic lesions and CNV.
• Punctate inner choroidopathy (see below).
• Inactive multifocal choroiditis with panuveitis syndrome.
Punctate inner choroidopathy
Definition
• A usually eventually bilateral chronic condition that typically affects young
myopic women.
Signs
• No vitritis.
• Small, yellow, indistinct choroidal lesions all of same age at the posterior
pole (Fig. 13.108).
Complications
• CNV is common.
Fig. 13.108

• A usually bilateral chronic

Acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy
Definition
• A bilateral, self-limiting, condition which typically affects young adults.

Serpiginous choroidopathy
Definition
• A bilateral chronic condition which typically affects middle-aged individuals.
Signs
• Mild vitritis.
• Multiple choroidal lesions with hazy borders that typically start near the

Complications
• CNV is uncommon.

Necrotising retinitis
Cytomegalovirus retinitis
Definition
• A chronic, often bilateral, viral infection that affects patients with AIDS.
Signs
• Mild vitritis.
• Yellow-white areas of retinitis and haemorrhage which typically start at
the posterior pole and then frequently spread along the vascular arcades
(Fig. 13.113).
Complications
• Retinal atrophy.
Fig. 13.113
• Retinal detachment.
Acute retinal necrosis
Definition
• An infection caused by the varicella-zoster or herpes simplex virus which is
often bilateral and usually affects otherwise healthy individuals.
Signs
• Anterior uveitis and vitritis.
• Confluent, peripheral occlusive necrotising vasculitis.
• Progression is faster than in cytomegalovirus retinitis but the macula is
usually spared till late (Fig. 13.114).
Fig. 13.114

Differential diagnosis
• Intraocular lymphoma may be associated with vitreous cells and diffuse retinal involvement.
Progressive outer retinal necrosis
Definition
• An infection caused by varicella-zoster virus which is often bilateral and
predominantly affects patients with AIDS.
Signs
• No vitritis.
• Necrotising retinitis that starts in the periphery or the posterior pole.
• Rapidly progresses with early macular involvement (Fig. 13.115).
Fig. 13.115
MACULAR LESIONS
Oedema
Macular oedema is characterised by increased fluid within the sensory retina
that may assume a cystoid or non-cystoid configuration.
Non-cystoid
Signs
• Retinal thickening at the posterior pole.
• FA shows intraretinal leakage during the late phase (Fig. 13.116).
• OCT shows macular thickening with loss of the foveal pit (Fig. 13.117).
Fig. 13.116
Fig. 13.117
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 297 of 404 ---
Macular lesions 311
Cystoid
Signs
• Thickening of the fovea associated with microcysts (Fig. 13.118).
• Best seen with red-free light (Fig. 13.119).
• FA shows a flower-petal pattern of leakage at the fovea during the late
phases (Fig. 13.120).
• OCT shows cystoid spaces within thickened retina (Fig. 13.121).
Differential diagnosis
• Foveal schisis which is a bilateral XL hereditary condition characterised by
a ‘bicycle-wheel’ appearance (Fig. 13.122); 50% of cases have associated
peripheral retinoschisis (Fig. 13.123).
Fig. 13.118

312 CHAPTER 13 • FUNDUS
Serous detachment
In serous detachment there is a separation of the sensory retina from the RPE by fluid; the causes are the following:
Idiopathic central serous retinopathy
Signs
• Unilateral, shallow elevation of the sensory retina at the posterior pole,
with indistinct borders (Fig. 13.124).
• Associated tiny subretinal precipitates may be seen.
• FA shows progressive ‘smoke-stack’ hyperfluorescence under the
sensory retina (Fig. 13.125). An expanding ‘ink-blot’ leak is less common
(Fig. 13.126).
• OCT shows shallow separation of the sensory retina from the RPE
(Fig. 13.127).
Fig. 13.124
Fig. 13.127
Fig. 13.125 Fig. 13.126
Congenital optic disc pit
Signs
• Macular serous detachment
develops in about 45% of eyes
with disc pits.
• Initially there is a schisis-like
separation of the inner retinal
layers which communicates with
the pit (Fig. 13.128).
• Separation of the outer layers
develops later (Fig. 13.129).
Fig. 13.128 Fig. 13.129
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 299 of 404 ---
Macular lesions 313
Other causes
• Retinitis (Fig. 13.130) may occasionally cause a serous detachment.
• Harada disease (see Fig. 13.98).
Fig. 13.130
Detachment of the RPE
Detachment of the RPE typically affects elderly patients with age-related
macular degeneration.
Signs
• Unilateral, sharply circumscribed, dome-shaped elevation at the posterior
pole of varying size (Fig. 13.131).
• Sub-RPE fluid may be clear or cloudy.
• FA shows an area of early hyperfluorescence which increases in intensity
but not in size during the later phases (Fig. 13.132).
• ICG shows hypofluorescence with a faint hyperfluorescent outline
(Fig. 13.133).
• OCT shows a dome-shaped separation of the RPE from Bruch membrane
Fig. 13.131
(Fig. 13.134).
Fig. 13.134
Fig. 13.132 Fig. 13.133
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 300 of 404 ---
314 CHAPTER 13 • FUNDUS
Choroidal neovascularisation
In choroidal neovascularisation (CNV) new vessels originating from the
choriocapillaris grow through defects in Bruch membrane under the RPE (type
1 CNV) or into the subretinal space (type 2), or both.
Signs
In chronological order:
• Leaking from CNV causing macular oedema, hard exudates and
haemorrhages (Fig. 13.135).
• Subretinal and sub-RPE bleeding (Fig. 13.136).
• Subretinal (disciform) scarring (Fig. 13.137).
FA
• ‘Lacy’ pattern of hyperfluorescence in the very early phase (Fig. 13.138A).
• Bright hyperfluorescence during peak dye transit (Fig. 13.138B).
• Late leakage into the subretinal space and around the CNV (Fig. 13.138C).
Fig. 13.135 Fig. 13.136 Fig. 13.137
Fig. 13.138A Fig. 13.138B Fig. 13.138C
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Macular lesions 317
Atrophic maculopathy
Dry age-related macular degeneration
Definition
• The most common form of age-related macular degeneration in which
involvement is bilateral but frequently asymmetric.
Signs
• Sharply delineated areas of hypopigmentation or depigmentation in which
choroidal vessels are more visible (Fig. 13.149).
Fig. 13.149
Myopic maculopathy
Definition
• A usually bilateral but asymmetric condition which affects highly myopic
adults.
Signs
• Roundish yellow-white areas at the macula due to focal chorioretinal
atrophy that may be associated with a pigmented (Fuchs) spot (Fig. 13.150).
• Lacquer cracks at the posterior pole (see Fig. 13.141).
• Macular hole which may give rise to retinal detachment.
• Tilted disc and peripapillary atrophy.
• Peripheral chorioretinal atrophy, lattice degeneration and retinal breaks.
Fig. 13.150
• Vitreous degeneration.
Stargardt macular dystrophy
Definition
• An AR condition which starts during the teen years and results in early
severe visual loss.
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

318 CHAPTER 13 • FUNDUS
Signs
Three patterns may be seen:
• Isolated atrophic maculopathy (Fig. 13.151).
• Atrophic maculopathy surrounded by fish-tail-shaped flecks (Fig. 13.152).
• Atrophic maculopathy associated with diffuse flecks (Fig. 13.153).
Fig. 13.151 Fig. 13.152 Fig. 13.153
Central areolar choroidal dystrophy
Definition
• An AD condition which causes loss of central vision during the 5th decade.
Signs
• Circumscribed, atrophic macular lesion between one and three disc
diameters in size not associated with drusen (Fig. 13.154).
Fig. 13.154
Downloaded from ClinicalKey.com at Mayo Foundation May 23, 2016.
For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

--- Page 305 of 404 ---
Macular lesions 319
Bull’s eye maculopathy
Signs
• Central foveolar hyperpigmentation surrounded by an oval depigmented
zone which is itself outlined by a ring of hyperpigmentation (Fig. 13.155).
• FA shows an RPE window defect corresponding to the depigmented zone
(Fig. 13.156).
Causes
a. Chloroquine may occasionally give rise to bull’s eye maculopathy but
hydroxychloroquine very rarely if ever causes retinal toxicity.
b. Cone dystrophy is a rare condition in which bull’s eye maculopathy may be
associated with temporal disc pallor, retinal vascular attenuation and mild
peripheral pigmentary changes (Fig. 13.157).
Fig. 13.155
c. Benign concentric annular macular dystrophy is an AD condition which may
cause mild impairment of central vision.
d. Inverse RP in which the pigmentary changes are confined to the posterior
pole.
e. Fenestrated sheen macular dystrophy is an AD condition in which bull’s eye
maculopathy is a late feature.
f. Leber congenital amaurosis is an AR retinal dystrophy that causes perinatal
blindness in which bull’s eye maculopathy is one of many fundus features.
Oculodigital syndrome (Fig. 13.158), roving nystagmus and poor pupillary
light reactions are characteristic.
g. Stargardt disease (see above) may occasionally assume a bull’s eye
configuration.
Fig. 13.156
Fig. 13.158
Fig. 13.157

For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright ©2016. Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

Fibrotic lesions
Cellophane maculopathy
Signs
• Transparent membrane which may be associated with subtle retinal striae
and vascular tortuosity.
• Best detected with red-free light (Fig. 13.159).
Fig. 13.159
Pucker
Signs
• An opaque membrane that frequently radiates from an epicentre and
causes vascular distortion (Fig. 13.160).
Causes
• Idiopathic.
• Chronic intraocular inflammation.
• Retinal vascular disorders.
• Iatrogenic – scleral buckling procedures, retinal cryotherapy and retinal
photocoagulation.
• Combined hamartoma of retina and RPE.
Fig. 13.160

Signs
• White-grey oval or round subretinal scar at the macula (Fig. 13.161) in eyes
with end-stage of exudative maculopathy associated with CNV (see above).

Macular lesions 323
Drug-induced crystalline maculopathy
Tamoxifen
Indications
• To treat breast carcinoma; retinal toxicity is uncommon.
Signs
• Fine, superficial, yellow, crystalline deposits and deeper punctate grey
lesions (Fig. 13.168).
Fig. 13.168
Canthaxanthin
Indications
• To enhance sun tanning; retinal toxicity is uncommon and innocuous.
Signs
• Tiny, glistening, inner retinal yellow deposits, arranged symmetrically in a
doughnut shape at the posterior pole (Fig. 13.169).
Fig. 13.169
Methoxyflurane
Indications
• An inhalant general anaesthetic, prolonged administration of which may
lead to renal failure and secondary hyperoxalosis.
Signs
• The retinal changes are identical to those seen in primary oxalosis type 1
(see Fig. 13.165).

Myopic
Unlike age-related macular hole it may give rise to retinal detachment which

Pathogenesis
• Severe Berlin oedema (commotio retinae – Fig. 13.178) may subsequently

Retinal detachment 329
Signs
• Superficial grey area with a geographic configuration (Fig. 13.189).
Fig. 13.189
RETINAL DETACHMENT
Rhegmatogenous
Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RD) is caused by a retinal break which
may be a hole or a tear.
Fresh
Signs
• The detached retina has a slightly opaque and corrugated appearance, and
blood vessels appear darker than in attached retina (Fig. 13.190).
• The subretinal fluid (SRF) has a convex configuration (Fig. 13.191) and
retinal breaks appear as discontinuities of the retinal surface (Fig. 13.192).
• Ultrasonography shows a mobile membrane attached anteriorly to the ora
serrata and posteriorly to the optic nerve head (Fig. 13.193).
Fig. 13.190
Fig. 13.191 Fig. 13.192 Fig. 13.193

Malattia levantinese
Definition
• A very rare AD condition that shares phenotypic overlap with familial

Signs

Fundus flavimaculatus
Definition
• An AR condition, which in the absence of macular involvement, may remain
asymptomatic throughout life.
Signs
• Yellow-white, fish-tail-shaped flecks at the posterior pole and midperiphery
(Fig. 13.226).
• The flecks may occur in isolation or in association with maculopathy
(Stargardt macular dystrophy – see Figs 13.151, 13.152 and 13.153).
• FA in long-standing cases shows hyperfluorescence due to RPE atrophy. A Fig. 13.226
dark choroid is seen in 85% of cases (Fig. 13.227).
Fig. 13.227
Fundus albipunctatus
Definition
• An AR condition characterised by congenital non-progressive
night-blindness.
Signs
• A multitude of tiny yellow-white spots extends from the posterior pole,
where they are most numerous, to the periphery (Fig. 13.228).

Sorsby pseudo-inflammatory dystrophy
Definition
• A very rare AD condition that usually causes severe visual loss.
Signs

Complications
• CNV is very common.

Choroidal metastatic carcinoma
Signs

Serpiginous chorioretinopathy
Signs

• Cataract.

Definition
• An infestation caused by the filarial parasite Onchocerca volvulus which is

Signs
• End-stage disease is characterised by extensive chorioretinal atrophy and
