Ill Status postepikeratciplasty (cpikeraiophakiaJ
During epikeraioplasty, the epithelium of the recipient cornea is removed with
Bowman's layer lefi in place. An epikeratopiasty lenticle is fashioned from a donor
conical hiiuon in the shape that gives the desired refractive power. This is done in
such a way that the epithelium wilh its basemen) membrane, Bowman's layer, and
anterior Stoma are left lataci and only Descemct's membrane and the deep stroma
are removed. This lissue is transplanted onto the denuded Bowman's layer of tile
recipient. This condition is ihe only one in which iwo_ Bowman's layei;s arc found in
one corneal button. 1'RK ablates superiiciaLhiyeis. and pail or full thickness
Bowgpn's layer in ihe center of the cornea. Inlrastromal keratumilctisis leaves
Bowman's layer intact. Trauma can cause rapture of Bowman's layer but noi
complete duplication.
2. C) li originates from ihe exnaocular muscles.
3. D) Alveolar
Children wilh rhabdomyosarcoma present with rapidly progressive unilateral,
painless proptosis. Swelling and ecchyinosis may resemble orbital celluliiis. The cell
of origin is presumably an uudilfcreniiaicd, phiripoieni cell of ihe soft tissue-. These
cells have the capacity to differentiate toward muscle with production of myosin and
actin and lo show cros.s-s trial ions. Injmunohiskiehemisiry shows positivity for
vimentin, myosin1, myoglobin. muscle-specific aclin, and desmin. The embryonal
type is ihe most common type, followed by the alveolar type. The prognosis is
poorest for ihe alveolar type. The pleomorphic or well-differentiated type occurs in
older patients and has ihe besi prognosis.
4. C) This lesion can be found in approximately 259J of older patients.
Figure 7-1 shows a ivpit ;tl Kuchs' adenoma (Fuchs' reactive hyperplasia, coronal
adenoma, Fuchs1 epilhelioma. bgjaign ci}|ajx_cpUh_eliariia). This lesion is considered
lo be proliferate rather than neoplasiic. The lesion is composed of basemen!
membrane material (type IV collagen and laminin), acid mucopolysaccha rides,
glycoprolcins, and proliferating cells of the nonpigmenied rili:try hnriy npitlwlium
It rarely may cause localized occlusion of the chamber angle. A meiastatic lesion
from a malignant melanoma would display mostly pleomorphic, piginented cells
with malignant features {high nuclear-cytoplasmic ratio, nucleoli, and mitotic
figures).
5. IS) The cells of origin for these lesions are the retinal pigment epithelial cells (cells
of neuroeclodcrmal origin).
The retinal pigmeul epithelium has ilie_capability for Him uis and osseous
metaplasia. This is a nonspecific reaction and is observed most often in pl]|]ijsical
eves. This metaplasia can lead to intraocular ossification, even with intraocular
hematopoiesis. The lesion is benign.

--- Page 287 of 562 ---
Pathology 285
6. C) Exudative
The histologic section (Fig. 7-5) displays a complete retinal detachment with
amorphous eosinophilic material under the retina. This material is not serous as it is
in a rhegmatogenous retinal detachment, but contains an abundance of protein.
The subretinal fluid in a retinal detachment caused by traction is mostly serous, and
the histologic section would show fibrous preretinal membranes in the vitreous.
Artifactitious detachments do not have subrelinal fluid.
7. D) Cavernous spaces filled with erythrocytes arc present.
Histologic section through this choroidal lesion shows a typical cavernous
hemangioma, which is characterized by lakes of erythrocytes separated by thin
fibrous septa. The lesion is histologically benign. A capillary hemangioma consists of
multiple capillaries surrounded by epithelial cells and pericytes.
8. A) Two clinical growth patterns, that is, a localized and a diffuse form, are
observed.
The patient has a choroidal hemangioma, mostly a unilateral lesion. This is an
uncommon hamartoma that has been reported to exhibit two clinical growth
patterns: 1) circumscribed tumors without systemic disease, and 2) diffuse tumors
often associated with the Sturge-Weber syndrome. The lesion is histologically
benign; however, the lesion can lead to an exudative retinal detachment if
untreated. Therapeutic options are photocoagulation and cryotherapy. The solitary
type is histologically characterized by a cavernous hemangioma with sharply
demarcated pushing borders, often compressing surrounding melanocytes and
choroidal lamellae. This is visible clinically as a ring of hyperpigmentation in the
periphery of the lesion (Fig. 7-2) and on fluorescein angiography as a ring of
blockage of the underlying choroidal fluorescence (Fig. 7-3). von Hippel-Lindau
disease has vascular lesions of the retina and cerebellum; however, these are
capillary hemangioblastomas.
9. D) Ipsilateral hemangiomas of the meninges are common.
The girl has meningoculaneous angiomatosis (Sturge-Weber syndrome,
encephalotrigeminal angiomatosis). The syndrome consists of mostly unilateral
meningeal calcifications, facial nevus flammeus (port-wine stain) frequently along
the distribution of the trigeminal nerve, and congenital glaucoma (30%). Heredity
- does not seem to be an important factor. Histologically, the syndrome is
characterized by cavernous hemangiomas of the skin, lids, choroid, and meninges.
The disease has no tendency for spontaneous regression as observed with capillary
hemangiomas of the child.
10. D) The lesion appears clinically as grayish bubbles posterior to the ora serrata.
The condition is called typical peripheral cystoid degeneration (TPCD) and is
inevitable in adult eyes. The cystoid spaces develop first at the oul[er plexiform layer.
If these spaces coalesce, typjjgal d*'g<*nprgHi>p rpfino^^'"" ""snitt In most cases, this
is without clinical consequence. Very rarely, a retinal detachment can develop in the
presence of holes in the inner and outer layers of the schisis cavity. In contrast,
reticular cystoid degeneration is less common and develops in the nerve fiber layer.

--- Page 288 of 562 ---
286 Review Questions in Of)litlialtiiolot>y
11. 11) Surface epithelium is found in the anterior chamber.
This condition is known as epithelial ingrowth (downgrowth) and occurs when
surface epithelium of the eye gains access to the internal structure of the globe. This
is generally seen after accidental or surgical penetration of the eve. The ninltilayered
uonkcialini/.ed squatROUS epithelium grows over any available surface and can cause
an obstruction of the trabecular mesh work, thus causing a secondary glaucoma. In
most advanced cases, the eye is lost. TheflCF^yndronic is characterized by a
unihiter.U disease in young m inidrile-:i»etl adnl|s. Abnormal cornea! endodielinni
grows over the traberular nu-shwnrk and over the anterior iris surface. Secondary
open-angle glaucoma may develop. Three different clinical phenotypes are known:
Chandler's syndrome, Cogan-Reese (iris nevus), and essential iris atrophy.
12. C] Periodic ucid-SchiH (PAS)
The epithelium in the anterior chamber covering the trabecular meshwork and
growing over the iris is a nonkeran'ni/cd si ratified s<iuamotts epithelium. To
differentiate tins epithelium according to amjimcny;ilj>rj:orneal origin, die
identification of goblet cells may help. Goblet cells are found in the conjunctival
epithelium and are" highlighted with a PAS stain (Fig. 7-10). IIenv.itox\lin-eosin is
the standard stain for iiiom lissnes. Oil Red () is un excellent stain for lipid: however,
this must be done on fresh tissue. Congo Red is used to look for amyloid material.
With a polarized microscope, amyloid stained with Congo Red demonstrates
birefringence and dichroism.
13. C) Special stains such as Oil Red O can be used to identify lipids.
During the embedding process in paraffin, different organic solvenis (alcohol,
\ylene) that dissolve and leach out the lipids are used. Therefore, fresh iissue or
frozen section has to be used to preserve the lipids and apply a lipid stain such as Oil
Red O or Sudan Red. Ldpid stains are helpful when looking for sebaceous cell
carcinoma.
14. D) The diagnosis is confirmed with CT and serologic studies.
15. A) Developmental abnormality
The patient has the anterior form of persistent hypcrplasiic primary vi neons. The
embryonic primary vitreous and hyaloid vasculature system persist. This condition is
mostly unilateral and is characterized clinically by leukocoria (the most common
lesion simulating retinoblastoma). However, contrary to retinoblastoma, persistent
hyperplastic primary vitreous is associated with a microphthalnilc eye. 1 listologicallv.
it is characterized by elongated ciliary processes, persistent hyaloid vessel (Fig. 7-12),
rctioleinal C^ff'^LiiU1'-"" ''<<ni' (with posterior lens capsule dehisceiice) sometimes
containing adipose tissue ("lipomaiosis lent is") (Fig. 7-1'5). cartilage, and smooil"T~
muscle. The diagnosis is made clinically, and adjunctive tests are unrevealmg,
IB. B) This is a benign cutaneous disorder rarely involving the eye.
The child lias juvenile \ai]thoi:ratinloniar(ncvoxanlhocndotl]elioina) of the iris.
This is a benign cutaneous disorder of infants with typical raised orange skin lesions
occurring singly or in crops and regressing spontaneously. Ocular involvement is

and occurs mostly under (> months of age. Children may present with
spontaneous anterior chamber hemorrhage and secondary glaucoma. I listologically,
the lesion is characterized by a diffuse granuloniatous inflammatory reaction with

17. 11) Oncocyloma
Ocular mi a >rst twins (adenolvinphomalous iiimor. apocrine cysiadeiimna. oxyphilic
cell adenoma) are rare, mostly benign neoplasms and are mostly found mi ihe
caruncle. This tumor arises from accessory lacrimal glands in ihe caruncle, especially
in elderlv women. It also can arise from accessory lacrimal glands. 1 listologic
examination reveals solid nests and cords of polyhedral cells exhibiting abundant,
finely granular acidophUic < ytoplasm and round to ova! paraccntral nuclei, usually
containing a single prominent nucleolus. Cystic cavities are identified within the
tumor. Adenoid cystic carcinoma (malignant cvlindroina) occurs in the lacrimal
gland in young adults. The tumor causes pain (infiltration of perineum! lymphatics)
and is liistologicallv characterized by a "Swiss cheese" pattern. A pyogmit granubma is
a type of granulation tissue composed of inflammatory cells and budding capillaries
that form a radial pattern. A papilloma is characterized by fronds or fingerlike
projections of acanthotic epithelium overlying a fibrovascular stroma.
18. li) multiple partially atypical mitochondria
Electron microscopic examination shows a cytoplasm densely packed with
mitochondria. The mitochondria are sometimes atypical. This is characteristic of
oncocyiomas.
19. A) This can be a chronic inflammation of the Zeis sebaceous glands.
This lesion is a chala/ion. A chronic inflammation of the meibomian glands (deep
chala/ion) or Zeis sebaceous glands (superficial chalazion) results in a hard, painless
nodule. I listologically. it is characterized by zonal granulomalous inflammation,
including muliimicleaied giant cells, around clear spaces previously filled with
lipid thai is dissolved in ihe processing (Fig. 7-17). It is important to do a biopsy to ex
clude a sebaceous gland carcinoma in patients with recurrent chala/ion. This type of
carcinoma can mimic a recurrent chala/ion or a chronic blepharitis, Moll's glands are
apocrine sweat glands in the eyelid and are not associated with chala/ion format inn.
20. C,> are consistent with a zonal lipogranulinna
The clear spaces in this chala/ion were previously filled with lipid thai dissolved out
during tissue processing. The pathogenic principle of a chala/ion is a lipogrannloma-
lous inflammatory process. In contrast to xanthomas, the lipid is located in die extra
cellular space. In a frozen section without paraffin embedding, the lipid is preserved
and visible. A vascular channel should at least show endolhelial cells.

22. I)) The disease is phacolyiic glaucoma.
The paiicni has a phacoanaphylactic (phacoimmune) endophthalmitis. This disease
develops afier exposure of large amounts of lens antigen to the immune system and
abrogation of tolerance to lens protein. It is clinically characterized by signs of
chronic nvi-iiis, rhmoidal detachment, and liypmonia bulhi. Normally, lens protein
is recognized as "self." In the case ofa breakdown of the T-cell tolerance, antibodies
against the lens proteins mav lie produced, initiating a chronic inflammation, In this
case, the lens nucleus was losi during rataraci surgery (Fig. 7-20) and a
phacoanaphylactic endophthaltniris developed. Hislologically, it is characterized by
activated.ni-unophils surrounding ami "eating away" lens material (Fig. 7-21). These
in turn are surrounded In rpitheljoid cells and occasionally mnltinucleaicd niimi
cells and granulation (issue (zonal granulonia). In c ontrasl.bhacolyiic glauconi;) is
characterized by the leakage of denatured lens proteins through an intact capsule
(e.g., hypei[nature cataract). This initiates a mild foreign body reaction, and
marrophagaLgcLswullcn and engulf denatured lens material. These macrophages
can block die trahn ular outllow and can cause a secondary open angle gbncomn
(phacolytic glaucoma).
2'i. ('.) The incidence of/on ular dialysis during cataract surgery is higher in these
patients.
Tlii' paiicui has pseudoexfoliation syndrome (basement membrane exfoliation
syndrome). This is characterized by a deposition ofa peculiar white. Fluffy material
on die lens capsule, the zonules. the ciliary epithelium, the iris pigment epithelium,
and the uabecular meshwork. h is more common in Scandinavians and is quite rare
in African Americans. The amorphous eosinophiiic material lines up perpendicular
lo the lens (like iron filings lining up on a magnet). The iris pigment epithelium
displays a sawtooth posterior configuration. The incidence of'/onnlar dialysis during
cataract surgery is higher in these paiirni.s. The iris may haw peripapillary
iransilluminaiion defects in lln's condition in contrast to die radial defects seen with
pigment dispersion syndrome. Infold radiation mav cause "true exloliation" of the
lens capsule in glass blowers or welders.
24. C) Nodular drusni
Nodular (hard) druseii consist ofa focal thickening of die basement membrane of the
retinal pigment epithelium. Clinically, they appear as small yellow or yellow-while
spois measuring 50 u.m in diameter. Soji (exudative, flufiy) drusen are bigger and
appear less dense and more fluffy, llnuil laminar tfi-/nL\jl\ consist of banded basement
membrane material (wide-spaced collagen) located between tlie basal pla.smalenuna
of the retinal pigmenl epitlielium and its basement membrane. Hn\nl litirai ilrfwsif*

25. H) Basal cell carcinoma, nodular type
linsiil cell carcinoma is characterized histologically by a proliferation of basophilic
Cells. It is hv far the most common nialiunanl lumor of the eyelid, I he tumoi < an be

--- Page 291 of 562 ---
Pathology 289
grouped into three types: nodular, superficial, and morpheaform. CJleeration and
pigmentation may or may not occur. Typical Features of the nodular type- are
peripheral palisading of the tumor cells. The superficial type shows irregular buds of
basaloid cells arising from multiple foci of the epidermal undcrsuiface. The
morpheaform type is characterized by Eumor ceils growing in thin, elongated strands
or cords, often only one cell layer [hick ("Indian file" pattern).
2(i. B) Lower eyelid—inner camlius—upper eyelid—Literal canlhus
The reason for this order is probably the amount of ultraviolet light exposure
during life.
27. B) A special stain for acid lnucopolysaccharides (e.g., colloidal iron) can be
positive within the optic nerve.
Figure 7-26 shows an excavated, deeply cupped optic disc. The most likely cause is
kin elevated IOP over the course of many years. In the case of a cavernous optic
atrophy (Schnabej's), cystic spaces generally posterior to the lamina cribrosa are
found. The cystic spaces are filled with hyaluronic acid that stains positive with a
stain for acid mucopoiysacrharides (e.g., colloidal iron). A central retina! occlusion
leads to an airophic optic nerve; however, the disc is not excavated. Reduction and
normalization of IOP can result in reversal of optic disc cupping, which is common
in children and less frequent in adults.
28. B) This neoplastic lesion contains epithelial embryonic rests. -
The his to logic lesion shown in Figures 7-27, 7-28, and 7-29 consists of a proliferation
of cells with bland nuclei in the epithelium and subepiihelial layer. The
subepithelial component contains cysts with a nnnkeratini/ed squamous epithelium
with goblet cells. The lesion represents a typical compound nevus of the conjunctiva.
The subepiihelial hamaiiomatons component, in addition to the nevus cells,
frequently contains epithelial embryonic rests that may develop into epithelial cysts,
as this case shows. The lesion can be pigmenlcd or nonpigmented. In chronic
conjunctivitis, a chronic inflammatory cell infiltrate is seen. Infoldings of the
proliferated epithelium and goblet cells may resemble glandular structures in tissue
sections and are called ftseudugUtnds (Henle).
29. B) Status po.sircpair of retinal detachment
The hisiologic picture (Figs. 7-30 and 7-31) shows an empty cysu'c space in the sclera
with an overlying polyiilamcntous suture associated with a foreign body giant cell
reaction. The empty scleral space represents a dissolved encircling band from prior
Surgery for retinal detachment. The polyfilamentous suture was used to fix die band
to the sclera. A ruptured sclera should be associated with full thickness sclera] scars.
A scleral staphyloma is ectatic and thinned sclera lined by choroid with or without
retina.
30. D) Rhabdomyosarroma
1 lomer-Wright rosettes are characterized by cells that line up around an area
containing cobweb-like material, but no acid lnucopolysaccharides are present
These rosettes are not specific for letinohhisioma and are found also in
neuroblastoma and medulloepiihelioma. Rhabdomyosarcoma displays no rosette

--- Page 292 of 562 ---
290 Review Questions in Ophthalmology
formation. In contrast, Flexner-Winiersteiner rosettes are the characteristic rosettes
of reiinoblastoma but arc not always present. Tlie presence: of Flexner Winiersteiner
rosettes makes the diagnosis of a well-differentiated retinoblastoma. In Klexner-
Wintersteiner rosettes, the cells line up around an apparently empty central lumen.
However, special stains show hyaluronidase-resistant acid mucopolysaccharides in
the lumen.
31. (",) Medulloepithelioma
The medulloepithelioma (diktyoma) arises from tlie ciliary epithelium. N'omeratoid
and teratoid varieties (presence of heteroplastic elements) are known. Both can be
benign or malignant. The teratoid type may contain cartilage and/or
rhabdomyoblasts. Phthisis bulbi can result in metaplasia of the retinal pigment
epithelium with bone formation. Cartilage formation is virtually unknown. Posterior
persistent hyperplastlc primary vitreous is characterized by vitreous membranes
extending from the disc usually toward the equatorial /one, posterior radial retinal
folds, disturbance of macular function, and retinal detachment. Cartilage formation
is not a feature of posterior persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous, contrasting with
the anterior form in which it has been described in rare cases. Retinoblastoma
displays calcification secondary io rapid proliferation and necrosis of cells, but no
cartilage formation occurs.
32. A) Figure 7-36
33. D) Figure7-84

36. C) Oil RedO
The lesions pictured are enumerated:
Figure 7-32 = sebaceous cell carcinoma
Figure 7-33 = basal cell carcinoma, nodular
Figure 7-34 = sqnainous cell carcinoma

Figure 7-37 = seborrheic keratosis

Sebaceous cell carcinomas may present insidiously as chronic, recurrent chala/ia
or blepharitis. II they are suspected, fresh [issue should be processed for lipid stains
such as Oil Red O. They are notorious for skip lesions and require wide excision.
Metastasis to regional lymph nodes is possible.
Rji^il i-ijlpii-rmnma k the most frequently occurring eyelid lesion, often in sini-
exposed areas. These may present as raised lesions with pearly borders,
telangiectatic vessels, and an ulcerated naicr (rodent ulcer). 1 listopalholo<n'cally,

--- Page 293 of 562 ---
Pathology 291
Sgtiammis cell rar^'"""*3 js also related to sun exposure. Proliferation of
cosinophilic epilhelium with rests of keratin pearls traverse the basement membrane
into the underlying demiis. Surgical excision using the Mohs' technique is
recommended. Metastasis occurs to regional nodes.
The keratoacanthoma may be confused with the squamous cell carcinoma;
however, it presents much more rapidly. The central crater contains keratin. These
may resolve spontaneously over several months.
Seborrheic keratoses are benign lesions that have a greasy, "stuck on" appearance.
Pathologic examination shows hyperkeratosis and papillomatosis with keratin
pseudo-horn cysts.
Molluscum contagiosum is caused by a pox virus and appears as raised,
umbilicated lesions on the eyelid, face, and genital regions. Epilhelium thickened by
intracytoplasmic molluscum bodies are present in the central crater. These may be
shed into the tear film from eyelid margin lesions, causing a chronic follicular
conjunctivitis. Treatment is by cryotherapy, curettage, or excision.
37. D) Metastasis
Congenital cataracts, relinoblastoma, and retinopathy of prematurity, among others,
are in the differential of bilateral leukocoria. Intraocular metastatic disease to the
eyes is extremely rare in children.
38. D) Cryotherapy
The CT scan shows bilateral high density lesions consistent with calcification within
the tumors. In this case of retinoblastoma, the right eye was enucleated. Treatment
for the left eye includes enucleation, radiation, and chemotherapy. Cryotherapy may
be effective for small lesions. For posterior lesions, as pictured, effective placement
of cryotherapy would be difficult to achieve. Moreover, it would be ineffective for
multicentric tumors. Chemotherapy or radiation is preferable to tiy to preserve
vision in the left eye.
39. D) Necrosis of tissue with calcification
Relinoblastoma is a rapidly growing tumor that can outgrow its vascular supply. This
rapid growth leads to necrosis of tissue and secondary' calcification. Whitish flecks of
calcification can be seen in the whitish substance of the tumor (Fig. 7-45).

40. B) Photoreceptor
Figure 7-42 shows a Flexnej-Wintersleiner rosette, characteristic of relinoblastonm.
These rosettes represent abnormal photorecepjor differentiation. Homer-Wright
rosettes and lleurettes are also along the spectrum of photoreceptor differentiation.
41. D) Tumor diameter greater than 10 DD
Extraocular spread of retinoblastoma indicates a poor prognosis. Patients with
bilateral involvement may be at higher risk of trilateral retinoblastoma. The size of
the tumor, per se, does not_airect_gn)gnosis. Figure 7-40 shows tumor cells within
the substance of the optic nerve.

42. A) Osteogenic sarcoma
Secondary tumors are more common in these children and include osteogenic
sarcoma, malignant melanoma, lymphoma and leukemia, rhabdomyosarcoma, and
medulloblastoma. The most common malignancies are sarcomas, particularly
osteogenic sarcoma.
43. A) -10%
In this bilateral case, there is a high likelihood that a somatic, heritable mutation
exists. Half of this child's oflspring will receive the abnormal chromosome. With
80% penetrance, 40% of his children will be affected.
44. C) Fuchs' dystrophy
The cornea has multiple excrescences at the level of Descemet's membrane
consistent with Fuchs' dystrophy. Keratoconus would have breaks in Bowman's layer
and epithelium along with thinning and scarring of the cornea, but with a normal
Descemet's membrane. Pseudophakic bullous kcratopathy and congenital
hereditary endothelial dystrophy have thickening of the corneal stroma and loss of
endothelial cells.

45. C) Ganglion cell
The ganglion cell nuclei are found in the innermost layer ol" nuclei. The inner
nuclear layer contains cell bodies of the bipolar, amacrinc, horizontal, and
Muller cells. The outer nuclear layer has the cell bodies of the pholoreceptors
(Fig. 7-47).

46. B) Metastases to liver and bone commonly occur.
If isolated to ihejris, malignant melanoma behaves in a more benign fashion than

47. C) Asirocyiic hamartoma
Findings associated with tuberous sclerosis include astrocvtic hamartomas,
intracerebral calcification, seizures, mental retardation, adenoma sebaceum, and ash
leaf spots. (lapidary hemangiomas may be found in von I lippel-I.indau disease.

--- Page 296 of 562 ---
294 Review Questiom in Ophthalmology
48. A) Inner plexiform layer
The retinal arteries supply oxygen to the superficial layers of the retina, including
the nerve fiber layer, ganglion cell layer, inner plexiform layer, and inner third of
the inner nuclear layer. The other layers are nourished primarily by the choroid.
49. C) Spherical red blood cells
Ghosts cells represent hemolyzed red blood cells. Alter a longstanding hyphema or
vitreous hemorrhage, the red blood cells lose their normal shape and become more
spherical. These cells are more rigid and less able to deform to exit through the
trabecular meslnvork, resulting in a secondary open angle glaucoma. On slit lamp
microscopy, they appear as khaki-colored cells in the anterior chamber.
50. B) Juvenile xanthogranuloma
Histiocytosis X (I^ingerhans cell histiocytosis, Langerhans granulomatosis) is
characterized by a proliferation of Langerhans cells in an inflammatory background.
Immunohisiocheinically, the cells stain positively for S-100 and vimentin.
L'ltrastructurally, the cells contain Birbeck granules (containing a central dense core
and a thick outer sheath). Three interrelated clinicopathologic entities are known:
1) eosino/jfiilic fpanitloma of bone (benign tumor arising often in the outer part of the
upper orbital rim). 2) Hand-Schiiller-Christian disease (bony lesions in the skull,
exophthalmos, and diabetes insipidus), and 3) Lettem-Siwe disease (fatal disease with
diffuse histiocytosis). Juvenile xanthogranuloma (JXG) does not belong to the
histiocytosis X group. JXG is negative for S-100 and contains no Birbeck granules.

A) Gram-negative coccobacilli
This patienl has filtering bleb—associated endophthalmitis. The most common
organisms responsible lor iliis condition include Slrepticocciu species (Gram-positive
cocci in pain or chains) and Haemophilia influenzae (Gram-negative coccobaciili).
< tther organisms, such as Slaphylococcus epidermidu (Gram-positive cocci in clusters)
and Gram-negative species, have been implicated less frequently. Fungal causes arc
rarely associated with filtering bleb—associated endophthalmitis. The organisms arc
thought to enler the eye through either intact or leaking conjunctiva! filtering blebs.
Often, Seidel tesiing is negative. Common presenting symptoms include
conjunctiva] discharge and inje< [ion, pain, and decreased vision. The prognosis for
eyes with bleb-associated endophthalmitis is gcnerallyffioijil and the end result is
often hjji
2. II) Propionibacterium antes
This patient's condition is suspicious for chronic postoperative endophthalmitis.
Organisms commonly responsible for delayed-onsei endophthalmitis after cataract
surgery include Stnphyhrurrits ejiidermidis, fungal species such as Candida, and
Propionibaderiim aims. Endophthalmitis caused by /*. acnes typically presents from JL.
months lo^jffiars after surgery, whereas S. epidermidis generally presents wiihin iL
weejes anil Canjlkja within 3_mtmths. The presence of a while plaque within the
capsular bag specifically suggests the diagnosis of P. notes. Microscopic examination
of plaques removed at the time ofvitrectomy has revealed /'. acnes.
%. I)) Vitrectomy, posterior capsulectomy, and intravitreal injection ofvancomycin
The clinical picture suggests delayed-onset postoperative endophthalmitis caused l>v
I', anus. This condition often responds initially to topical steroids; however,
inflammation will recur. Although simple intravitreal injection of vancomycin has
resulted in clearing of this infection, recurrences are common. Currently,
recommendations for initial therapy include a pars plana vitrectomywiih posterior
capsutectomy and intravitrea] injection ofvancomycin. An alternative advocated In
some surgeons is a posterior pole vitrectomy, 1OI. explain, posterior capsulectomy,
intravitreal vancomycin, and a sutured IOL.
4. C) Klebsielia
In addition io the more common organisms responsible for delayed-onsei
postoperaiive endophthalmitis such as I*, anus, S. epidermidis, anil Candida, other
organisms, including l^ropionibacterium grunutosum, Achromobacter, and
Corynebacterium, have been reported to cause this syndrome. Klebsiella or oilier
Gram-negative bacilli may be seen in endogenous endophthalmitis but would be an
unlikely cause of delayed-onsei postoperative endophthalmitis.

5. ('.) mnliinucleated giam cells of the Langhans type
Tlic clinical description of a chronic diffuse uveitis with evidence of a choroidal
granuloma and hilar adenopathy is highlv suggestive <>[ sarcoidasis, A lacrimal gland
biopsy is useful in the diagnosis ofsarcoidosis, especially il ii is enlarged ai the time
of tlie biopsy. The gland is involved in approximately 25% of patients. The histolagic
lesion found in sarcoid biopsies is the noncaseating epithelioid cell tubercle. The
tubercle is composed ofmultinucleaicd giam cells of the Langhans type surrounded
bya lini ol'lymphocytes. Toulon giant cells classically arc found in juvenile
6. C) Follkular conjunctivitis
Sarcoid has many protean manifestations affecting almost all of the ocular
structures. Inflammation and granulomas may be found as conjunctiva! nodules,
iniilMillion ol the lai rimal gland, anterior uveitis with Koeppe and Busucca nodules.
vitreitis, chorioretinal nodules, papilluis, and scleritis. The sequelae ol chronic
inflammation include keratoconjunctivitis sicca, hand keratopathy, synechiae, and
glaucoma. Follicular conjunctivitis is commonly found in viral conjunctiva!
infections.
7. 11) retinal ueovascularization
Treatment of sarcoid uveitis often requires topical, sub-Tenon's, or systemic
corticosteroid therapy. Many ocular and syslemic complications may occur as a
resuli o! this medication. Ocular complications include cataract formation, steroid-
induced glaucoma, scleral thinning, and delayed wound healing. Neovascularization
i>l the retina may occur with sarcoid, but this i.s a result of inflammation and
ischemia, no! a consequence of steroid therapy.
S. l>) Multiple sclerosis
This presentation ol band keratopathy, a mild anterior chamber reaction, vitreous
cells, and a pars plana "snowbank" in a young patient, is highly suggestive of the
diagnosis of pars planitis. Multiple sclerosis has been associated with ibis condition
in approximately 5% of cases, and ihe condition is bilateral approximately 80% of
the time. Other associated findings include posterior subcapsular cataracts, anterior
chamber synechiae, macular epiretinal membranes, retinal phlebitis, and cystoid
macular edema.
9. Hi Rbroglial and vascular components
I listo logically, the .snowbank is composed of fibroglial and vascular elenienis. his
not an exndate, bul a preretinal membrane that forms in response to an
inflammatory stimulus. The membrane also contains vascular elements thai
occasionally bleed and result in a vitreous hemorrhage. The peripheral retinal veins
often show a perivascular cuff of lymphocytes, and the vitreous snowballs are
composed of epithelioid cells and multinuclcated giant cells.
10. A) I 'veiiis-glaucoma-hypheraa (UGH) syndrome
This patienl has a severe anterior uveitis with a hy|)hema. Any seven- uveitis can
cause damage to the iris vasculature resulting hi anterior chamber bleeding. In

--- Page 330 of 562 ---
Uveitis 329
particular, VZV iridocyclitis and HLA-B27 (see Fig. 8-4) can result in this picture. A
traumatic injury can cause a tear at the iris root in addition to inciting an anterior
uveilis. Dispersed blood and fibrin can appear similar to active uveitis. The UGH
syndrome is the result of movement of an anterior chamber IOI., which chafes the
iris and angle causing inflammation and bleeding. This paiieni is phakic; therefore,
this etiology is not possible.
11. C) Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada
The following conditions may be associated with diffusely distributed kcratic
precipitates over the corneal endothelium: Fuchs' heterochromic iridocyclitis,
sarcoidosis, syphilis, kerato uveitis, and, rarely, toxoplasmosis. Most inflammatory
conditions, including Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada, display kcratic precipitates
predominantly over the inferior portion of the corneal endothelium. The finding of
diffusely distributed keratic precipitates may be a useful diagnostic sign.
12. A) Behcet's disease
The HLA-B27 genotype is present in approximately 1% to 6% of the general
population; however, it may be found in a larger percentage of patients with acute
iritis. HLA-B27 is found in almost 90% of patients with ankylosing spondylitis. It also
is commonly associated with conditions such as psoriatic arthritis, Crohn's disease,
ulceralive colitis, and Reiter's syndrome. Patients with Behcet's disease, however,
have an increased incidence of HLA-B5 or subset Bw51.
13. D) Positive rheumatoid factor
The triad of urethritis, conjunctivitis, and arthritis has described classic Reiter's
syndrome. Other associations include keratoderma blennorrhagicum; iritis, keratitis,
balanitis, prosiatitis, cystitis, spondylitis, fasciitis, tendonitis, oral mucosal lesions,
and a recent history of diarrhea. HLA-B27 genotype is present in approximately 75%
of cases. Rheumatoid factor is usually not present.
14. 1)) Rochalimaea
Reiter's syndrome may occur afteiLdysejilejy or after nongonococcaJ-iiietluitis.
Ureaplasma urealyticum and Chlamydia trachomatis have been associated with the
postgenitourinary form. Shigella, Salmonella, and Yersinia have also been shown to
be triggers for this condition after episodes of diarrhea or dysentery caused by these
agents. Rochalimaea has been implicated as causing cat-scratch fever and
neuroretiuitis but not this condition.
15. C) keratoderma blennorrhagicilm
Skin involvement may be seen in Reiter's syndrome in approximately 20% of cases.
The classic lesion is known as keratoderma blentwrrhagicum and is pictured in Figure 8-
5. When present, this condition is considered a major criterion in making the
diagnosis of Reiter's syndrome. The other major criteria include polyarthritis,
conjunctivitis, and urethritis. Minor criteria are signs, such as fasciitis, sacroiliitis,
spondylilis, keralilis, cystitis, prosiatitis, oral mucosal lesions, and diarrhea. Definite
diagnosis of Reiter's syndrome is defined by three or more major criteria, or two
major criteria and two minor criteria.

Hi. ('.) This patient has paudarticular disease involving ihe lower extremities without
involvemeni of the wrist
Juvenile rheumatoid arthritis (JRA) presents in children younger than Mi years of
age. Approximately <S()','(. of patients with JRA and iritis are ANA positive and
rheumatoid factor negative. The joint involvemeni in JRA may be polyarticular or
pauciai ticular {four or fewer joints). When iridocydilis is present, the joint
involvement is most commonly of the paudarticular type (91)% of cases). Systemic
[RAwith fever and rash is rarely associated with iritis. ANA-pOsitive females with
pauciarticular involvement of a lower extremity joint and lack of involvement of the
wrists_are_at highest risk for developing iritis^The anterior chamber inflammation in
chronic |RA is characterized by a mild variable degree of anterior chamber cellular
reaction with predominance of flare. Tin- conjunctiva is generally quiet.
17. ('.) Pathologic specimens of this condition have revealed the presence of plasma
cells within the ciliary body.
This patient has Fuchs' beterochromic iridocyclitis. Ibis condition is generally
characterized by heierochromia, although up to 15% of cases may have bilateral
involvement without obvious heterochromia. Diffuse ins_siromaULiropliv with
Variable pigment epithelial layer atrophy accounts for the change in iris color in the
involved eye. Because of the pigment loss, brown eyes will appear less brown; blue
eyes will appear more blue. This condition is also characterized by small, white,
stellate kcratic precipitates that are diffusely present over the endothelial surface.
Synechiae are almost never present. The inflammatory nature of this disease is
supported In the presence of plasma cells and lymphocytes on pathologic
examination of ocular (issue.
18. 11) Toxoplasma
This condition is clearly associated with chorloretinal scars consistent with
loxoplasmosis; however, whether I'oxoplasma is a causative agent is unclear'. Not all
cases of Fuchs" heterochromic iridocyclitis have choriorclinal scars.
19. C) latanoprosi
Ihe prostaglandin analogues have several proinflanimatory side effects, including
inciting development of an anterior uveitis and a cystoid macular edema. I 'nilaieral
use can also result in changes in iris pigmentation and eyelash growth. The anterior
uveitis resolves with topical predmsolone and discontinuation of the causative drug.
20. A) Igl)
igD has not been detected in any study of human tears. The major immunoglobulin
class found in human tears is IgA. IgG and IgM are detectable in only small
amounts. igK is thought to be one of the major mediators of allergic reactions. In
contrast, all live immunoglohulins are found routinely in ihe human conjunctiva
within the suhepiihelial iissue.
'21. Et) IMiacoanaphylaxis is a Type III hypcrscnsiiiviiy reaction.
There are lour general types of hypersensitivity reactions. One of the four types will
predominate in any inflammatory response, although several types may be involved

--- Page 332 of 562 ---
UvetHs S31
;ii the same time. jPj^w/inflammatory responses are antibody mediated. The binding
of antibody i<i masl ceils releases histamine, leukotrienes, and other inflammatory
mediators. Allergic reactions, such as hay fever, are type I reactions. Type II reactions
are mediated by cytotoxic antibodies such as those seen with ocular pemphigokl,
Type III reactions arc immune complex mediated. Antibody binds with free floating
or fixed antigens and then deposits as ;\ complex, activating the complement
cascade. Phacoanaphylaxis fits into iliis category. Finally, type /V reactions arc T cell
mediated reactions. The granulomatous responses seen in sarcoid or tuberculosis
are examples of type IV reactions.

23. 151 Application of cyanoarrylate glue
This patient has severe rheumatoid anhritis with peripheral ulcerative keratitis. The
hands of this patieni (Fig. 8-M)} show the stereotypic deformities from rheumatoid
arthritis. Other conditions to consider include other collagen vascular diseases
(Wegener's granulomatosis, polyarteritis nodosa, relapsing polychondrttis),
Mooren's nicer, and infections such as tuberculosis and syphilis. Peripheral
ulcerativc kenuitis is nut a hallmark of HIV infection or AIDS.
Rheumatoid patients can have spontaneous painless eorneaJ melting. In the acute
phase, topical steroids ure coniniindicated because they can potentiate the conical
collagenases and increase the melting. These patients can lie pitied temporarily until
systemic prednisone and imraunosuppresstve agents are started. Grafts in an
inflamed eye are at high risk for failure or recurrent melting.

24. A) Recurrent, indolent course
Serpigiaous choroidapathy (geographic helicoid peripapillary choroidopathy) is a
chronic, recurrent, indolent disease of unknown etiology, li usually affects adults
with painless loss of vision. Lesions arc peripapillary or perimacular and. in Lhe
active phase, have inllainniation along the edges. Centrifugal spread iu a snake-like
pattern occurs over months to years. Treatment with steroids or immunosuppresslve
agents has had variable success.

--- Page 333 of 562 ---
332 Review Questions in Ophthalmology
25. C) Endophthalmitis would be expected to occur in less than 10% of similar
trauma cases.
Posttraumatic endophthalmitis after penetrating trauma typically occurs in fewer
than 10% of cases. The most common agents responsible for this infection include
Staphylococcus epidennidis and Bacillus species, although many other organisms have
been recovered. The prognosis for visual recovery in this setting is generally poor,
especially when Bacillus cereus is implicated.
26. A) The responsible organism grows on blood agar and Sabouraud's glucose within
24 to 48 hours.
This fundus lesion in the setting of a debilitated patient on hyperalimentalion and
previous antibiotics is highly suggestive of endogenous endophthalmitis often
caused by Candida albkans. Most cases of Candida endophthalmitis occur without
ongoing fungemia or positive blood cultures. All Candida species exist in two
morphologic forms: ayeastlike form known as blastoconidia (pseudohypha) and
elongated branching structures (pseudomyeclia). Candida species typically grow on
blood agar and Sabouraud's glucose within 24 to 48 hours. They grow as large
creamy-white colonies. A germ tube test from a colony of only 24 hours' duration
can result in a presumptive diagnosis of Candida albkans. Although this organism is
frequently cultured from the respiratory tract, it rarely causes pneumonia.
27. D) Haemophilus injluenzae
Endophthalmitis as a complication of IV drug abuse has been widely reported. The
most common organisms in these cases are fungal in origin and often occur in the
absence of fungemia. Bacterial causes are predominantly a result of Staphylococcus
species; however, recently Bacillus cereushas been reported as an important
pathogen. Although Haemophilus injluenzae can cause infection, it would be unusual
in this setting.
28. C) psoriasis
Patients with Bchcet's disease frequently have a variety of associated skin lesions. Le
sions resembling erythema nodosum are frequently present over the anterior surface
of the lower extremities. These lesions typically resolve over a period of several weeks.
Patients may also display typical acne-like lesions, folliculitis, or thrombophlebitis.
Cutaneous hypersensitivity is also characteristic of this condition. The behgetine skin
test performed by puncturing the skin with an empty hypodermic needle may be use
ful in making a diagnosis of Behcet's disease. A positive test is indicated by the forma
tion of a pustule at the puncture site within minutes.
29. C) Colchicine may be helpful in preventing recurrences.
Commonly used agents in the treatment of Behcet's disease include systemic corti-
costeroids, cytoioxic agents (alkylating agents such as chlorambucil), colchicine, and
cyclosporine. Systemic corticosteroids may be initially effective in treating the ocular
inflammation in this condition; however, its use has clearly not been found to arrest
the long-term progress of this condition. Colchicine is known to inhibit leukocyte mi
gration and has been found to be useful in preventing recurrences. Cyclosporine has
also been found to be particularly useful in the treatment of Behcet's disease; how
ever, its use must be carefully monitored because of renal toxicity.

--- Page 334 of 562 ---
Uveitis 333
30. ('.) The ti-1in11is associated with ihis condition may be confused with a viral
rrliniiis.
Inflammation in lVhcet's disfa.sc may affect die anterior and posterior segments

31. ci HLA-B27-associated iritis
The most common cause of hypopyon iriiis is H1A-B27 associated disease.

iridocyclitis that responds well to corticosteroid iherapy.

42. IS) I lypoglycemia
Systemic corticosteroid therapy has been associated with numerous complications.
Ocular complications include cataracts and glaucoma. Systemic complications
include exacerbation of hypertension or diabetes mellitus, osteoporosis, gastric
ulceraiiun, liiistiiisin. weigh I gain, capillary fragility, and cushingoid appearance.
43. (..) Immune modulator ol interleukin 2
44. II) Cytostatic anti-inflammatory
45. A) Cytotoxic antimetabolite
Cyclosporine and FK506, a related agent, are poteni immune modulators thai have
very specific activity against the production of interleukin-2 and interleukin-2
receptors and inliibii proliferation of lymphocytes. Prednisone is a cytoslatic anti-
iiiihunmalory agent ilial is a nonspecific immunnsupprcssivc.
Cyclophosphamide and chlorambueil are both cytotoxic alkylating agents. They
act by creating cross-linkage between DNA strands that result in inhibition of
transcription of messenger UNA. Chlonunbticil is the slowest acting of all the
alkylating agents.
Azathioprine and methotrexate both inhibit purine ring biosynthesis. In addition,
methotrexate also inhibits the synthesis of deoxyihymidine monophospliate
rtucleotide.
46. A) Cotton wool spots
Cotton won! spins may occur in over 50$ of AIDS patients. Tlif cotton wool spins
may spontaneously resolve and recur. The innsi common opportunistic infection of
the rye is cytomegalovirus reiiniiis. Acuu- retinal necrosis is secondary to herpesvirus
retinal infection.
47. ()) tachyzoite
Toxoplasma organisms lend to be dormant in the cysl form in the intermediate
human host. In the encysted form, several dormant organisms may be found.
These relatively inactive organisms within the cysis are called hmdfznilrs. Once
bradyzoites are released and become free, they are called lachyemtes, These
niclahiilicaliy energized protozoans are responsible lor stimulation of intraocular
inflammation.

336 Review Questions in Ophthalmology
48. C) Clindumycin can effectively kill Toxoplasma organisms.
Pyrimethamine actually inhibits dihydrofolate reductase. Sulfadiazine blocks the
production of dihydrofolate from para-aminobenzoic acid. Clindamycin is an
antibiotic that can effectively kill Toxoplasma organisms. Systemic and periocular
steroids can be used in the treatment of ocular toxoplasmosis in immunocompetent
patients.
49. A) early-onset pauciai ticular disease
Pauciarticular early-onset JRA is the entity associated most commonly with chronic,
recurrent iridocyclitis. It is seen in young women. The antinuclear antibody is often
present in these patients, but rheumatoid factor is uniformly absent. Arthritis of the
lower extremities is more common in patients with iridocyclitis.
50. D) photoreceptor outer segments blocking the trabecular meshwork
Schwartz's syndrome is high IOP associated with a rhegmatogenous retinal
detachment. Photoreceptor outer segments migrate iransvitreally into the aqueous,
block the trabecular outflow pathways, and result in IOP elevation.
51. D) is often self-limited
Posner-Schlossman syndrome presents with unilateral ocular pain, mild anterior
uveitis, and elevated IOP. The episodes that occur are typically self-limited, but they
often require the use of topical glaucoma medications to control IOP. Mild topical
corticosteroids may be used to control intraocular inflammation. Systemic and
periocular corticosteroids are not indicated. Recurrent attacks of this syndrome may
lead to eventual optic nerve damage and late-onset glaucoma.
52. B) Zonal granulomatous inflammation
53. C) Macrophages filled with lens material
Lens-induced uveitis syndromes may produce granulomatous or nongranulomatous
inflammation. Although phacoanaphylaxis implies Type I hypersensitivily reactions,
the word is a misnomer because it represents a Type III immune hypersensitivity
reaction to lens protein that results in a zonal granulomatous inflammation.
Macrophages filled with lens materials often clog the trabecular meshwork and
result in phacolytic glaucoma. IOLs may produce phacotoxic nongranulomatous
inflammation by physically irritating the iris or ciliary body. The UGH syndrome is a
type of phacotoxic uveitis.
54. C) Posterior pole is involved initially with centrifugal spread.
Acute retinal necrosis was first described in healthy, immuuocompetent patients.
Herpes simplex and zoster viruses have been implicated as causing this condition.
Patients present with vitreitis, confluent peripheral necrotizing retinitis, and retinal
arteritis with vaso-occlusion. The posterior pole typically is spared until late in the
disease. Retinal detachments are common because of the multiple, large areas of
retinal necrosis.

--- Page 338 of 562 ---
Uveitis 337
55. B) filiform hemorrhage with paraccntesis
Fuchs' helerochromic iridocycliiis is typically an insidious, chronic intraocular
inflammation that may not respond well to topical corticosteroids. Characteristic
findings include diffusely distributed stellate kcralitis precipitates and mild anterior
segment cell and Hare with loss of iris crypts and detail. Blue irides become more
blue, and brown irides become less brown in cases of Fuchs' helerochromic
iridocyclitis. Gonioscopy will reveal evidence of abnormal bridging vessels in the
angle, although neovascular glaucoma with angle closure does not develop. A
paracentesis of the anterior chamber may result in a small, splinter-shaped filiform
hemorrhage in the angle. Fifty percent of patients with Fuchs' heterochromic
iridocyclitis develop cataracts; 60% develop glaucoma. Cataract surgeiy is not
associated with any higher rate of vitreous loss or posterior capsular rupture
compared to normal eyes.
56. D) syphilis
Although syphilitic uveitis may mimic any other form of uveitis, intermediate uveitis
is a distinctly uncommon presentation. Pars planitis is by far the most common inter
mediate uveitis entity, a diagnosis of exclusion. Between 5% and 25% of patients with
multiple sclerosis may have evidence of pcriphlebitis and intermediate uveitis.
57. B) choroidal neovasculari/ation
Calciflc band keratopalhy can develop in young patients with pars planilis.
Peripheral retinal neovascularization can result in vitreous hemorrhage and
tractional detachments. Progression of fractional detachments can result in
rhegmatogenous components, as well. Choroidal neovasculari/ation is not an
associated complication of pars planitis. Cystoid macular edema is the most common
macular complication of pars planitis and the most common cause of vision loss.
58. C) reveals diminished scotopic responses
Because of the peripheral creamy choroidal infiltrates, retinochoroidopathy,
peripheral visual field loss, nyctalopia, and rod dysfunction seen in birdshot
retinochoroidopathy, electroretinogram reveals diminished scotopic responses.
59. A) Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome
Cystoid macular edema is not typically seen in patients with Vogt-Koyanagi-Uarada
syndrome. Patients with this disorder present with bilateral panuveitis and evidence
of disc edema ami bilateral multiple serous detachments. In chronic cases,
pigmentary changes may be present in the fundus, but cysioid macular edema is not.
In addition to the entities listed, any iridocyclitis can cause cystoid macular edema,
especially chronic, recurrent cases.
60. D) HLA-A29 and birdshot retinochoroidopathy
The association between HI A-A29 and birdshot retinochoroidopathy is the most
consistent of any medical disorder. Approximately 90%; of patients with birdshol
retinochoroidopathy are HLA-A29—positive (Table 8-1).

61. C] Reduced vision, vitreous cells, and optic disc edema are early findings.
Diffuse unilateral subacute neuroretinitis is thought to be caused by numerous

consistently. In early phases of the disorder, reduced vision, vitreous cell, and disc

66. D) polymyalgia rheumatica

immiinosuppressivcs. A thorough investigation for associated systemic vasculitides

67. A) lumbar puncture
68. B) intrathecal methotrexate, IV cytarabine (Ara-C), and whole brain and eye

69. A) There is poor prognosis for survival.
Figure 8-16 shows the characteristic presentation of primary intraocular lyniphonia.
This leopard skin pattern of yellowish retinal pigment epithelium and subretinal
infiltration of lymphomalous cells are typically seen in patients over the age of 70
years. These patients often present initially with vitritis that may be unilateral or
bilateral. The initial laboratory evaluation of the vitritis may be noncontributory.
However, evaluation of the CNS with MRI, C7T scanning with contrast, and lumbar
puncture may reveal evidence of CNS lymphoma. Pars plana vitrectomy may be
required to establish diagnosis and to stage the neoplasm. Once the diagnosis has
been made and staging has been completed, therapy typically consists of
combinations of intrathecal methotrexate, IV cytotoxic agents, and whole brain and
ocular irradiation. Periocular and systemic steroids may mask the vitritis and reduce
the intraocular inflammation, but inflammation would recur on discontinuation of
the medications. The prognosis for survival is dismal in patients who have
intraocular lymphoma. Primary intraocular lymphoma is a subset of primary CNS
lymphoma. Once symptoms of CNS involvement have occurred, median survival
drops to approximately 6 months.
70. D) all of the above
71. D) corticosteroids
72. Ii) multiple pinpoint areas of fluoresceiii leakage into the subretinal space
73. D) acute neuroretinitis
The patient in Figure 8-17 has Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome. This diagnosis is
one of exclusion, and appropriate laboratory evaluation for this entity should be
performed. Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome is a bilateral granulomatous panuveitis
that causes disc edema and bilateral serous retinal detachments with panuveitis in
the acute phases of the disease. It is important to rule out infectious entities,
including syphilis and possibly tuberculosis. An antinuclear antibody test may also In-
done to rule out lupus choroidopathy. B-mode echography may be useful in
showing the amount of choroidul infiltration, which is quite profound in the
posterior pole. This condition is treated with systemic corticosteroids. Fluorescein
angiography characteristically reveals multiple pinpoint areas of hyperfluorescence
that gradually leak fluorescein into the subretinal space. Retinal vascular staining is

--- Page 341 of 562 ---
340 Review Questions in Ophthalmology
uncommon. Differential diagnosis would include posterior scleritis, sympathetic
ophthalmia, hypotony, uveal effusion syndrome, and lupus choroidopathy. Acute
neurorctinitis would not present with serous retinal detachments.
74. D) eczema
Cutaneous manifestations of Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada include alopecia, vitiligo, and
fmliosis (whitening of ihe lashes). These manifestations confirm the notion that
melanin is the immunogenic agent that results in the intraocular inflammation in
this condition. Eczema has been associated with atopy.

81. B) Figure 8-19 disorder
Figure 8-18 shows branch retinal vein occlusion in a patient with Behcet's disease.
Figure 8-20 shows the hypopyon, and Figure 8-21 shows an aphlhous ulcer in a pa
tient with Behcet's disease. Figure 8-19 shows a patient with serpiginous
reiinochoroidopathy. Figure 8-21 shows a patient with retinal periphlebitis, which
could be compatible with sarcoidosis, Lyme disease, pars planitis, multiple
sclerosis-associated intermediate uveitis, and possibly tuberculosis and Eales' disease.
Figure 8-23 shows acute hypopyon/plastic iritis in a patient who is HLA-B27-poskive.
Behcet's disease may be associated with gainful hypopyon iridocyclitis. However,
fibrinous anterior segment inflammation is uncommon. The eye can be remarkably
quiet in the presence of a hypopyon in Behcet's disease, unlike in HLA-
B27-associated disease, in which pain and photophobia dominate as symptoms. A
strong association with IILA-B5 is often present in Behcet's disease. Systemic
changes include acne, erythema nodosum, aphthous ulcers, genital ulcers, and
interstitial lung changes. A pulmonary artery aneurysm is a pathognornonic. chest
radiographic finding in a patient with Behcet's disease. Treatment is with systemic
and periocular corticosteroids. 'Colchicine^Day be useful in reducing recurrences of
the disease. Cyclosporine and cytotoxic agents may also be required.
Serpiginous choroidopathy is a condition of unknown etiology with recurrent,
progressive episodes of painless visual loss. Inflammation occurs at the edges of
previous lesions with centrifugal spread.
Periphlebitis is seen characteristically in sarcoidosis and Eales' disease. Ealcs'
disease is endemic in India and is often associated with pulmonary tuberculosis. The
exact etiology of Eales' disease is unknown.
Cutaneous changes are seen in Behget's disease, sarcoidosis, and Lyme disease.
Sarcoid skin changes include erythema nodosum and subcutaneous nodules or
granulomata. Lyme disease is associated with erythema chronicum migrans
(Fig. 8-32).

ANSWERS
1. B) Ascorbate
Compared with plasma, aqueous is slightly hyperionic and acidic. Aqueous has;
marked excess of ascorbate (I") times greater than that of arterial plasma) and ii
marked deficit of protein (0.2% in aqueous as compared to 7% in plasma).
2. D) Central n-iinal artery
The four divisions of the optic nerve head conflate roughly with a four-pan blood
supply (Fig. lJ-l2\). The surface fiber layer is supplied mainly by branches of Ihe
centra] retinal artery. The prelaminar region is supplied by capillaries of the short
posterior ciliary arteries. The lamina cribrosa region is also supplied by vessels thai
come directly From die short posterior ciliary arteries to form a dense plexus in the
lamina. The leitolaminar region is supplied by boih tEtc ciliaiy and lemial
circulations, with the former coming from recurrent pial vessels. The central retinal
artery provides centripetal branches from the pial region.

3. ('■) Lens extraction alleviates the condition.
PXFisan age-related disease involving an accumulation of abnormal fibrillar
extracellular man-rial within ocular and systemic tissues. Glaucoma occurs more
commonly in eyes with PXF than in those without it. In Tact PXF has been
recognized as the most common identifiable cause of glaucoma. The glaucoma is a

--- Page 375 of 562 ---
Glaucoma 375
secondary open angle glaucoma and can be unilateral or bilateral. IOPs can be quite
elevated and are often higher than in POAG. Glaucoma associated with PXF tends
to respond less well to medical therapy than does POAG, to be more difficult to
treat, to require surgical intervention more often, and to have a worse overall
prognosis. Unfortunately, lens extraction is not curative, although the pressure may
be lowered by a few mm of Hg following simple cataract extraction.
4. A) Topical corticosteroid response
POAG is a mullifactorial disease that may occur on the basis of inherited risk factors.
Risk factors for POAG include family histoiy of glaucoma (five to six limes greater
risk), African American heritage (live to eight limes higher incidence than in
whites), and age over 50 (the risk of glaucoma increases with each decade of life to
nearly a 15% incidence in the population over 80 years of age). It is seen with
greater frequency in patients with diabetes mellitus, high myopia, retinal
detachment, and central retinal vein occlusion. Patients with POAG and relatives of
POAG patients have a higher incidence of elevated IOP in response to topical or
systemic corticosleroids, but this is not a risk factor for POAG; rather, it is a risk
factor for steroid-induced secondary open angle glaucoma.
5. B) Ketamine
In most cases, patients have a decrease in IOP with general anesthesia. In particular,
halothanc and the inhalational anesthetics can decrease IOP. Ketamine and
trichloroethylene, however, can cause IOP to increase. Tranquilizers and
barbiturates may cause a slight decrease in IOP.
6. D) all of the above
Homocystinuria is a rare autosomal recessive condition. Affected patients are
generally tall with osteoporosis, scoliosis, and chest deformities. About 50% of the
time there is associated mental retardation. These patients are at increased risk of
thrombotic vascular occlusions, and this should be taken into consideration if
general anesthesia is planned. Lens dislocation occurs in 90% of patients and is
generally inferior and bilateral. Homocystinuria may lead to angle closure glaucoma
if the lens dislocates into the anterior chamber.
7. G) excessive fluorescein
If the fluorescein rings are too narrow, the IOP is underestimated. If they are too
thick, the IOP is overestimated. Use of excessive fiuorescein causes thick mires and
an overestimation of IOP. The thickness of the cornea affects IOP readings. If the
cornea is thin, the IOP is underestimated. In high myopia, there is decreased scleral
rigidity, which may lead to an underestimation of IOP. If cortical astigmatism is
greater than 3 D, IOP is underestimated for wilh-the-rule astigmatism and
overestimated for against-the-rule astigmatism.
8. B) 3 mm
A pupillary diameter of less than 3 mm can cause general depression of the field. It
is best to test the field with a pupil larger than 3 mm. Patients taking pilocarpine
may need to refrain from taking the medication for 24 hours before the test or be
dilated at the time of their examination.

--- Page 376 of 562 ---
376 Review Questions in Ophthalmology

neutral density filters. Fillers 1-1 arc in increments of 5 dli each. Filters a-e are in

times ihe area of the II k- test object.
10. B) fixation toss
I'lie I [umphrcy automated perimeter lias a number of ways to test the reliability of
the test taker. A fixation loss occurs when the patieni responds as if seeing a light
when a targe I is displayed in his blind spot. A false-negative response occurs when
the patieni fails lo respond to a snprailiicshold stimulus at a location that would be

losing interest. Intermittently, the perimeter will pause and die motorized liglit will

12. C) Figure 9-3D
figure 9-3D shows narrowing (notching) of the rim. For the early delection of
glaueomatous opiic nerve damage, ihe most important variable appears to be local
narrowing or notching of ihe netiro-reliiial rim. Other important variables are optic

13. D) r>0%
ll is known llialpaihologic changes of llie opiic nerve precede visual field changes.
In fact, up toeO^auf optic nerve axons can be lost before any change is delected on

14. B) peri papillary atrophy
Focal enlargement oi the cup appears as localized notching of ihe rim. The cup can
become vertically ova! if narrowing of ihe rim occurs at either ihe superior or

--- Page 377 of 562 ---
Glaucoma 377
inferior pole of the disc. Splinter hemorrhages usually clear over several weeks but
are often followed by localized notching of the rim. Glaucomalous optic atrophy is
associated with loss of axons in the nerve fiber layer, which can be best evaluated
with red-free illumination. Pcripapillary atrophy is not considered lo be a sign of
early glaucomatous damage. Other conditions such as ocular histoplasmosis or
myopia can result in peripapillary atrophy.
15. D) all of the above-
In a young normal eye, it is unusual to see any trabecular pigment band. This is
because insufficient pigment has filtered through the trabccular meshwork lo form
a visible pigmented line. If pigmentation is apparent, it is usually most prominent in
the inferior angle. The two most common conditions in which the pigment band is
very prominent are pigment dispersion syndrome/pigmentary glaucoma and
PXF/pseudoexfoliation glaucoma. Lesser amounts of trabecular pigmentation can
be seen in iritis, diabetes, or following intraocular surgery, trauma, or laser.
16. C) plateau iris syndrome
In oculocutaneous albinism there is diffuse transillumination of the iris. Patients
with exfoliation syndrome have peripupillary transillumination defects. Peripheral
discrete or confluent iris transilluminalion defects may be seen in pigment
dispersion syndrome. Plateau iris syndrome does not customarily produce
transillumination defects.
17. A) Figure 9-4A = Shields, pi lb (Koeppe lens)
In direct gonioscopy, the angle is visualized directly through the contact lens. In
indirect gonioscopy, the light rays are reflected by a mirror in the contact lens. The
Zeiss four-mirror lens is an example of an indirect gonioprism, where all four
mirrors are inclined at 64°. The Sussman lens is a hand-held Zeiss-type gonioprism.
The (ioldmann mirror is inclined at 62° for gonioscopy. The Koeppe lens is the
prototype diagnostic direct goniolens.
18. C) indirect ophthalmoscopy using a 20-D lens
The small image obtained using indirect ophthalmoscopy does not allow for
adequate evaluation of the optic nerve details. Careful examination with the direct
ophthalmoscope can provide important information about the pallor of the optic
cup; however, the mosi effective methods include stereoscopic examination using
the slit lamp in combination with a posterior-pole contact lens, a 9()-D lens, or a
Hruby lens.
19. C) Corneal diameter of 10.0 mm
Although congenital glaucoma has an incidence of 1 in 12,500 births, it accounts for
about ">% of students in schools for the visually handicapped. It is bilateral in two-
thirds of patients; two-thirds of these patients are male; and about 10% ol congenital
glaucoma is familial (autosomal recessive). Symptoms include tearing, photophobia,
and blepharospasm. Signs include:
1. IOPovcr21 mmllg
2. optic nerve cup:disk ratio greater than 0:3 (present in only 2.(>% of normal
newborns)

378 Review Questions in Ophthalmology
:'>. horizontal conical diameter greater ilian 12.5 mm with orwithoul cornea]
edema or breaks in Descemet's membrane (Fig, <J-22)
■1. open angle with anterior iris insertion (either flat insertion into the trabecular
meshwork or. less commonly, concave insertion with the plant1 of the iris
posterior io sclent! spur and anterior ins siroma sweeping upward and
inserting into the meshwork)
5. no iris abnormality other than that previously described (i.e., no h\ popJasia or
core c topi a)
lmponant diagnoses to exclude include neural crest dysflenesis (aniridia, Peter's
anomaly, Axenfeld-Kieger), phakomatoses, metabolic abnormalities (Lowe's,
homocystinuria, mucopolysaccharidoses), inflammatory conditions (congenital
rubella, herpes simplex iridorydilis), neoplasms (reiinobkisionia, juvenile

20. 11) argon laser trabecnlnplasiy (ALT) may be a helpful adjunct il medications are
ineffective
Ocular inflammation can learl to glaucoma via a variety of mechanisms including 1)
obstruction ol the trabecular meshwork by inflammatory debris, 2) increased"
viscosity of aqueous humor, 3) neovascularization, 4) uveal effusion, -ri) pupillar)
block, and (>) Formation of peripheral anterior synechiae. Treatmenl depends on
the underlying condition, bin in most cases, inflammation is suppressed by some
combination of topical, periocular, or oral corticosteroids. Occasionally, other
immunosuppressive agents ate needed. Elevated IOI* is managed by topical and oral
glaucoma medications as needed. Miotics are generally avoided because they lead to
increased pain and congestion and promote the formation of posterior synechiae.
Pjusiaglajidui agents are used willi caution because they may exaccrbaie uveiiis.
ALT is not very helpful in eyes with active inflammation.

--- Page 379 of 562 ---
Glaucoma 379
21. D) Systemic hypcrcholesterolcmia
Normal tension glaucoma is not one disease but rather a collection of disease
processes characterized by characteristic glaucomatous visual field loss without
elevated IOPs. Nocturnal systemic hypotejision caused by excessively large late
evening or bedtime doses of antihypertensive agents has recently been suggested to
cause low tension glaucoma on the basis of compromised optic nerve blood supply.
Yasospasm and shock may also compromise optic nerve blood supply. Systemic
hypercholcsterolemia has not been implicated in the pathophysiology of low tension
glaucoma.
22. C) Aniridia
The common pathogenesis of glaucoma in the Sturge-Weber syndrome, thyroid eye
disease, and carotid-cavernous sinus fistula is an increase in cpiscleral venous pressure
through elevated venous outflow pressure or orbital congestion. The glaucoma
associated with aniridia is caused by congenital agenesis of the angle structures.
23. D) Trabeculotomy
Management of congenital glaucoma involves surgery, either goniotomy (70%
success) or trabeculotomy (70% to 80% success). A goniotomy requires that the
cornea be clear enough to view the meshwork. In the scenario presented, a
trabeculotomy ab externo is the procedure of choice because of the cloudy cornea.
In general, for primary congenital glaucoma, if repeated attempts at goniotomy and/
or trabeculotomy fail, filtering surgery with mitomycin C or scton placement is
considered. Topical medication, such as beta-blockers, miotics, or carbonic anhydrase
inhibitors can temporize and potentially clear the cornea enough to allow goniolomy
to be performed. Cyclophotocoagulation and other cyclodestructive procedures
usually are reserved for eyes that have undergone other unsuccessful surgeries.
24. B) Neovasculari/.ation
Malignant melanomas can be associated with normal, elevated, or depressed IOI\
Elevated IOP occurs more frequently with melanomas of the iris/ciliary body than
with choroidal melanomas. Glaucoma may occur by a variety of mechanisms,
including 1) obstruction of the trabecular meshwork by melanin-containing
macrophages (melanomalytic glaucoma), 2) direct extension of tumor into the
trabecular meshwork, 3) angle closure from anterior displacement of the lens-iris
diaphragm or peripheral anterior synechiae, 4) inflammation, and 5)
neovascularizalion of the angle. N'eovascularizaiion of the angle appears to be the
most common cause of elevated IOP, especially among eyes treated with radiation.
25. D) Sodium hydroxide
Glaucoma is most often associated with alkali burns but can also be seen after severe
alkali burns. IOP rises initially because of scleral shrinkage and release of
proslaglandins. Later, IOP rises because of inflammation, posterior synechiae
causing pupillary block, or acute lens swelling. Finally, IOP may continue to be high
due to direct injury to the trabecular meshwork. Filtering surgeiy may be needed
but can be difficult due to conjunctiva] scarring. A cyclodestrudive procedure or
seton valve may be required.

--- Page 380 of 562 ---
380 Review Questions in Ophthalmology
26. C) most cases are caused by long-term oral administration of steroids
Most cases of corticosteroid glaucoma are caused by dronjs or ointments instilled in
the eye. Steroid creams, lotions, and ointments applied to the face or eyelids may
reach the eye in sufficient quantity to raise IOP, as may systemically administered
corticosteroids. There have been case reports of increased IOP following the use of
steroid inhalers for asthma or steroid nasal spray for allergic rhinitis. The IOP
elevation may occur within a week of initiating treatment or may be delayed for
years. The first step in managing steroid glaucoma is to discontinue the drug. In
most cases, the IOP will return to normal within a few days to several weeks. The
residual glaucoma can be treated with glaucoma medicines, laser, or surgeiy.
Corticosteroids are thought to raise IOP by lowering outflow facility perhaps due to
an accumulation of glycosaininoglycans in the trabecular meshwork.
27. B) Ganglion cells
The optic nerve carries axons from ganglion cells into the inner retina. The nerve
liber layer is comprised of these ganglion cell axons.
28. C) Mydriatic-cycloplegic therapy
Malignant glaucoma, or aqueous misdirection, occurs when aqueous is secreted into
and sequestered in the vitreous cavity, pushing the lens, hyaloid face, and iris
forward, thus collapsing the anterior chamber and blocking the trabecular
meshwork. The IOP often rises to 20 to (50 mm Hg, and the anterior chamber is
shallow both centrally and peripherally. The common clinical features include
1) shallow or Hat anterior chamber, 2) increased or normal IOP, 3) poor response
to miotics, 4) favorable response to cycloplegics-mydriatics. Mydriatic-cycloplegics
help by pulling the lens-iris diaphragm posteriorly.
29. A) Visual loss
Optic nerve drusen occurs in 2% of optic nerve specimens on histopathology and is
bilateral in 50%. Pathologic studies have revealed intra-axonal calcification of
niUochondria^The nerve head may be elevated, but no axonal swelling or edema
exists. Optic nerve drusen may be inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion and
occur more frequently in whites. Visual loss can occur and is thought to result from
axonal compression, subretinal hemorrhage, or choroidal neovascular membrane
formation. Systemic conditions associated with optic nerve drusen include Paget's
disease, Ehlers-Danlos, sickle cell, and pseudoxanlhoma elasticum.
30. D) panretinal photocoagulation
Initial treatment of a patient with an acute episode of neovascular glaucoma
includes a topical beta-blocker, an alpha agonist, and/or carbonic anhydrase
inhibitor as well as atropine and topical steroids. Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors may
be less effective in the presence of marked corneal edema. Miotics, prostaglandins,
and epinephrine should be avoided. In most cases, it is important to proceed rapidly
with panretinal photocoagulation to prevent total angle closure. Following
panretinal photocoagulalion, new vessels may regress within a few days to a few
weeks. If IOP remains elevated despite panretinal photocoagulation and medicines,
filtering surgery, seton valve placement, or cyclodestruction may be needed.

--- Page 381 of 562 ---
Glaucoma 381
31. A) Phncolytir glaucoma
Phacolytic glaucoma results when mature or hypennature cataracts leak soluble lens
protein through microscopic delects in the lens capsule. This heavy-molecular
weight protein directly obstructs the outflow of aqueous. Macrophages engorged
with this material may also obstruct the outflow channels. Although it is desirable to
first bring the IOP under medical control with hyperosmotics, carbonic anhydrase
inhibitors, and topical beta-blockers, definitive therapy requires removal ol the lens.
Lens particle glaucoma may occur after trauma to the lens capsule or after cataract
surgery with retained cortical material. The lens material may rcsorb spontaneously.
Medical management is used to control IOP; however, surgical removal of the lens
material may be necessary if medical management fails.
32. C) myopia
Patients at particular risk for aqueous misdirection are those with crowded anterior
segments (i.e., angle closure, nanophthalmos). Postoperative inflammation may
cause swelling of the ciliary body and ciliary processes leading to aqueous
misdirection. Myopia is not associated with an increased risk of aqueous
misdirection.
33. A) increased plasma high-density lipoprotein cholesterol levels
Blockade of betai-adrenergic receptors slows the pulse rate and weakens myocardial
contractility, which can ailed the time of exhaustion with heavy exercise. Blockade
of beta-.Kidrenergic receptors produces contraction of bronchial smooth muscle.
Topical timolol has been shown to decrease plasma high-density lipoprotein
cholesterol levels. Carteolol is a topical nonselective beta-ad re nergic antagonist that
has intrinsic sympathomimetic activity and has been shown to have less adverse
effect on plasma lipids. Belaxolol is relatively beta i-selective and may have less of an
effect on the respirator)' muscles.
34. D) pilocarpine
Aqueous suppressants have the best chance of controlling the IOP. Because the
trabecular outflow has been occluded by neovascularization, miotic medications will
be ineffective and may, in fact, reduce the uveoscleral outflow, further elevating the
IOP. Miotics are also less effective in eyes with severely increased IOP.
35. A) hyperopia
Ocular side effects of pilocarpine include conjunctival vascular congestion, miosis,
induced myopia, cataract formation, and temporal or periorbital headaches. Rarely,
pilocarpine can be associated with retinal detachment. Pilocarpine does not cause
hyperopia.
36. C) Worsening of obstructive pulmonary disease
Hyperosmotic agents may aggravate congestive heart failure by an increase in
extracellular volume. Backache; headache; mental confusion; and subdural, even
subarachnoid, hemorrhages have also been reported.

antihyperiensive agent. Several studies have shown the lack of effect of
apniclonidine on blood pressure and pulse. However, a transient dry mouth or diy
nose is commonly reported. Ocular side effects include eyelid retraction, mydriasis,
and conjunctival blanching.
44. B) decreasing aqueous production
Dorzolamide is a topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that works in an analogous
fashion to systemic carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (aceta/olamide, inethazolamidc)
by inhibiting carbonic anhydrase on the ciliary epithelium and decreasing aqueous
production. Dorzolamide's main side effects are burning or blurring on instillation
and a bitter or metallic taste in the mouth. It appears to produce far fewer systemic
side effects than do the oral agents.
45. A) Timolol and a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor
The combined effect of timolol and a miotic or timolol and a carbonic anhydrase
inhibitor is significantly greater than the effect of any of the medications alone. The
combination of timolol and an epinephrine compound, however, has less additional
IOP-lowering effect. Because epinephrine stimulates and be la-bloc ke is inhibit bcta-
adrenergic receptors, one drug may interfere with the action of the other. When an
epinephrine compound and a miotic are given in combination therapy, the
reduction in IOP is usually not to the same degree as timolol and a carbonic
anhydrase inhibitor in combination. When other miotics are administered in
combination with pilocarpine, they noi only fail to increase the IOP-lowering effect
but also may interfere with the action of pilocarpine.
46. A) Increased uvcoscleral outflow
The prosiaglandin analogs, of which laianoprost is a member, appear lo lower IOP
by increasing uveoscleral outflow. Aqueous outflow follows both conventional and
nonconventional pathways. The conventional pathway, which accounts for 85% to
90% of aqueous outflow, consists of the irabecular meshwork, Schlemm's canal, and
episcleral/conjunclival veins. The nonconventional pathway's principal route is via
the uveal tract and sclera. This accounts for about 10% to 15% of total outflow.
Whereas conventional outflow is dependent on the baseline level of IOP, the
nonconventional pathway is not.
47. D) Repeating ALT in eyes in which ALT was initially effective and in which IOP
control was eventually lost may provide pressure control in one-third to one-half
of cases, although 10% to 15% may have a sustained elevation of IOP.
The technique of creating laser holes through the trabccular meshwork is known as
laser Irabeculopuncture (trabeculotomy). This technique is the earliest attempt lo treat
glaucoma using laser technology, but it has not been successful in people or in
animal models. The risk of posttreatment IOP rise increases with increasing
numbers of laser burns. For this reason, some clinicians treat 180° of the angle with
50 laser burns in each session. Laser burns should be placed in the anterior
trabecular meshwork.
48. A) Pigmentary glaucoma
Laser trabcculoplasty effectively lowers IOP in patients with POAG, pigmentary
glaucoma, or pseudoexfoliation. It is ineffective and may actually worsen the IOP in

52. Pi) Neovascnlar glaucoma
In acme angle closure, ihe iris occludes the trabecular meshwork. This may be

photot oagulaiion lo cause regression of the vessels, not a peripheral iridectomy.
53. II) Fewer total applications
The continuous-wave argon laser was the uiiil mosl commonly used for creating
