probably the more commonly used today. Iridotomies created with an argon laser
have more extensive early edema and tissue destruction at ihe margins of treatment
histologically as compared with those created with the NdiYAG laser. Argon laser has
the disadvantage of more iritis, pii| >i I laiy distortion, and lale closure of the
tridotoniv. Clinically, the Nd:YAG laser has the disadvantage of frequent bleeding.

Nd:YAG for its disruptive properties.

65. C) rebleeding

66. C) Pars plana vitrectomy
There are multiple causes for shallowing of I he anterior chamber after glaucoma

unlikely. Pars plans vitrectomy has a role in the management of aqueous

67. C] uiioiic medications
The most likely diagnosis in this clinical setting is aqueous misdirection (ciliary
block glaucoma, malignant glaucoma). Patients at greatest risk include those with
shallow anterior chambers: older patients, women, and hyperopes. Maximum
cycloplegia and aqueous suppression may help to break the cycle of aqueous being
misdirected into the vitreous cavity. Reformation of die anterior chamber with
viscoelastic material may also help rotate the ciliary body posteriorly to break the
misdirection. I lalf of eyes with aqueous misdirection may be successfully managed
medically. Pseudophakic eyes can, on occasion, be treated successfully with Nd:YAG
laser to disrupt the posterior capsule and/or anterior hyaloid face. The resi will
require surgical intervention (pars plana vitrectomy with or without lensectomy). A
wound leak would present with a shallow chamber and flat bleb; however, the IOP
would be low.

hyperpigme.nted meshwork.
70. B) Phacolyn'c glaucoma

388 Review Questions in Ophthalmology
71. C) Extracapsular cataract extraction
Phacolytic glaucoma occurs when lens proteins leak through an intact lens capsule,
inducing a heavy macrophage response. The trabecular meshwork is obstructed with
high molecular weight lens proteins and bloated macrophages. It usually occurs in
older patients with a mature, hypermature, or even Morgagnian cataract. The lens
needs to be removed to cure this problem. Phacomorphic glaucoma occurs in
patients with large crystalline lenses, causing pupillary block and secondaiy angle
closure. Phacoanaphylactic glaucoma occurs after a traumatic injury ruptures the
lens capsule, allowing a granulomaious inflammation and secondaiy glaucoma.
72. B) the majority of patients treated with laser first did not require any additional
medical therapy to maintain IOP control
The Glaucoma Laser Trial study has demonstrated thai ALT is a reasonable
alternative to medication (timolol) in the initial treatment of POACJ. However,
2 years into the study, 56% of laser-treated eyes needed supplemental medical
therapy to control IOP. Laser-treated eyes had a lower mean IOP.
73. D) optic nerve hyperemia and swelling
In acute angle closure glaucoma, hydropic degeneration and impaired axoplasmic
flow cause swelling and hyperemia of the optic nerve. f'r|fiiikf>nn(lefl^fn and optic
nerve pallor and cupping would indicate previous episodes of angle closure
glaucoma, which the patient denies. Cell and flare may be seen with prolonged
attacks, but keratic precipitates are rarely seen.
74. A) Peripheral iridectomy
Large lenses in hyperopic eyes can cause pupillary block and subsequent angle
closure glaucoma. In these situations, a peripheral iridectomy may relieve the
pupillary block and relieve the angle closure attack. If the cornea does not clear
sufficiently for a laser peripheral irideclomy, a surgical iridectomy may be necessary.
Trabeculoplasiy has no role in angle closure episodes. Trabeculectomy and scton
implants may be needed in the future, but they are not the initial therapy of an
angle closure attack.
75. C) Ocular hypertension
Elevated IOP does not necessarily result in shallowing of the anterior chamber. The
other conditions may result in shallowing of the anterior chamber or narrowing of
the angle.
76. D) Schwartz's syndrome
Schwartz's syndrome is the name given to open angle glaucoma after
rhegmatogenous retinal detachment. The elevated IOP results from obstruction of
outflow by inflammation, pigment released from the retinal pigment epithelium
(RPE), glycosaminoglycans released by the photoreceptors, or photoreceptor outer
segments. It usually resolves after repair of the retinal detachment (RD). Scleral
buckles may interfere with venous drainage of the uveal tract, leading to swelling
and anterior rotation of the ciliary body with resultant angle closure. In

--- Page 389 of 562 ---
Glaucoma 389
nanophthalmos, uveal effusions resulting from obstruction of venous blood flow
through abnormally thick sclera cause rotation of the ciliary body anteriorly with
resultant angle closure. Intraocular tumors may push the angle closed from
posteriorly with the development of chronic angle closure.
77. A) Transscleral cyclopholocoagulation -
Transscleral cyclophotocoagulation is useful in many types of refractor}' glaucoma,
such as glaucoma in aphakia or pseudophakia, neovascular glaucoma, glaucoma
associated with inflammation, and glaucoma in eyes with multiple failed filtering
procedures. Observations in animal and human eyes suggest that the most likely
mechanism of IOP-lowering is reduced aqueous production through destruction of
ciliary epithelium. Performing a trabeculectomy in an eye with 360° of scarred or
nonmobile conjunctiva would be very difficult and is likely to fail. Although
repeating trabeculoplasty is helpful in some eyes that have had a good response to
initial treatment, most studies have shown a much lower success rate with repeat
trabeculoplasty, and it is unlikely that the pressure-lowering response would be
adequate for this patient. This patient has an open angle configuration, so an
iridotomy would have no further IOP-lowering effect.
78. C) Anterior chamber tube shunt
In eyes with coexistent vitrcoretinat disease and glaucoma thattiave-previously
undergone scleral buckling surgeiy, placement of an implant device is difficult because
of the lack of adequate scleral surface area for securing the seton. In those cases, a
silicone tube may be used to shunt fluid from the anterior or posterior chamber to the
fibrous capsule surrounding the cpisclcral encircling element. This procedure allows
for drainage of aqueous to a preformed reservoir of large surface area.
79. D) Excision of involved iris tissues with cryotherapy to remaining membranes on
conical tissues
Epithelial downgrowth has been reported most commonly as a complication of
cataract surgeiy, but it can occur after penetrating keratoplasty, glaucoma
surgeiy, penetrating trauma, and partial removal of epithelial cysts of the
anterior segment. It is more likely when surgeiy is associated with hemorrhage,
inflammation, vitreous loss, or incarcerated tissue. It usually results in intractable
secondary angle closure glaucoma unless successfully treated. The treatment is
difficult and usually unrewarding. All the techniques attempt to close the fistula
and destroy the epithelium inside the eye. The extent of iris involvement can be
outlined using the argon laser. Irradiation and photocoagulation have been
abandoned as ineffective. Excision of involved iris tissue with cryotherapy to
remaining corneal membranes is currently the best technique, although good
vision is maintained in only a few cases. Prevention is much more effective than
the treatment of established disease.
80. C) It prevents pupillary block glaucoma that can occur with silicone oil.
A large inferior peripheral iridectomy (Fig. 9-23) is mandatory when silicone oil is
placed into an eye. The oil is lighter than water, and to prevent pupillary block
glaucoma, an inferior peripheral iridectomy is performed.

81. DJ All ol the above are true.
A standard medical treatment For newly diagnosed glaucoma is to use drops and/or
laser treatment to lower the pressure inside the eye. Recent studies have questioned
this approach, suggesting that the risk of vision loss From glaucoma could be
reduced by instead having immediate filtration surgeiy. The CIGTS was designed to
address iliis question by comparing the effects of medical treatment to early
filtration surgery in newly diagnosed open angle glaucoma.
The. CIGTS Found thai increased 1OI1 was significantly decreased in both ilie
medically and surgically treated groups, with the surgery group having a larger
decrease. I lowever, in the surgery group, the need for subsequent cataract surgery
was significantly higher, and patients were more likely to lose visual acuity and visual
field dining the f 11 si few years of follow-up study. After 1 years, patients in both
groups were similar in visual acuity and visual field. Few patients developed serious
vision loss From glaucoma after either treatment
The CIGTS investigators concluded thai the study results provided no reason to
change current treatment approaches to glaucoma. The CIGTS researchers also
compared the impact of these two treatments on the patients" health-related quality
ol life. Their findings provided no reason io change current treatment approaches
to glaucoma.

design, poor finishing characteristics, or excessive mobility, allowing them to chafe
ihe iris surface. Pseudophakic hullous kcraiopathy was also more common with
these types of lenses. Modern flexible hapUC anterior lenses are belter tolerated and
less api to cause these complications. Therefore, because this patient's anterior
chamber lens is causing problems, it should be replaced or, at least, removed. If it
were well-tolerated, surgical replacement would not be necessary.

84. D) peripheral iris trausilluinination defects
PXF syndrome is bilateral in 50% of patients and manifests itself in older-age
patients. It is recognized by the presence of dandruff-like particles on the pupillary
border, anterior lens capsule, zonules, and other areas in the anterior segment.
(Figure 9-8 shows PXF particles on the posterior corneal surface as well as PXF on
the anterior lens capsule). The material is distributed widely, including the
conjunctiva, orbital tissues, skin, and viscera, supporting the concept that PXF is a
systemic disease. Associated eye findings include a Kniken berg spindle, decreased
corneal endothelial cell density, a heavily pigmented trabecular meshwork, narrow
angle, poor pupillary dilation (iris muscle degeneration and/ or lack of iris stroma
elasticity due to accumulation of PXF), nuclear sclerotic cataract, zonular weakness
leading to forward subluxation or dislocation of the lens, and peripupillaiy (not
peripheral) transillumination defects. An accumulation of pigment may also be seen
along Schwalbe's line (Sampaolesi's line). From 20% to (}0% of patients can have an
associated open angle glaucoma.
85. A) unoprostone isopropyl (Rescula) reduces IOP by increasing uveoscleral outflow
The prostaglandin analogs are a relatively new class of anti-glaucoma agents. Unlike
latanoprost and travoprosl, which lower IOP by increasing uveoscleral outflow,
bimaloprost decreases IOP by increasing uveoscleral and trabecular outllow.
Unoprostone appeal's to lower IOP by increasing trabecular outflow only.
Latanoprost and travoprost are pro-drugs that penetrate the K and become
biologically active after being hvdrolvzed by corneal csterase. Neither bimaloprost
nor unoprostone appears to be a pro-drug. An ocular side effect unique to this class
of drugs is the darkening of" the iris and pcriocular skin. Other side effects include
hypertrichosis of the eyelashes, conjunctival hypercmia, exacerbation of herpes
keralitis, CMF., and uveitis.
86. ('.) ghost cell glaucoma
The candy stripe is a tip-ofTto ghost cell glaucoma. Two conditions are necessary for
the development of this unique form of glaucoma: vitreous hemorrhage (red blood
cells degenerate and become rigid in the vitreous) and a break in the anterior
hyaloid face (to allow the cells to enter the anterior chamber). The crenated khaki-
colored ghost cells layer out in the anterior chamber and can be distinguished from
fresher red cells, creating the effect of a candy stripe. Ghost cells cannot escape
easily from the meshwork and hence produce intcrtrabecular obstruction and raise
the IOP.
In hemolytic glaucoma, hemoglobin-laden macrophages block the meshwork,
whereas in phacolytic glaucoma, macrophages are engorged with lens protein.
I Iyphema can also produce elevated IOP, especially in the setting of sickle cell
hemoglobinopathies, which include sickle cell trait.

Fluid commonly collects in the suprachoroidal space in hypotunous eyes,

in protein (67% of plasma concentration). Most serous choroidal detachments
resolve spontaneously along with die normal rise in IOP during the first few

defect, but filtration may Simply be excessive as a result of a large Msiula or filtering
bleb. Malignant glaucoma is associated with an elevated IO1' and a flat anterior

98. C) congenital glaucoma patient with poor visual potential

filtration surgery would fail or has already failed. Therefore, glaucoma implant
surgery is indicated in the following situations:

neovascular glaucoma
inadequate conjunctiva
impending need for 1'K.

indsional surgery in an eye with poor visual potential given the risks and. ai times,
complicated postoperative course of tube shunt surgery.

The lol lowing are complications of glaucoma implant procedure: hypotony, shallow
chamber, migration/expulsion of tube, conjunctiva) melts, conical edema, diplopia,
and elevated [OP.
1(10. C) pressure-sensitive valve
1 he Baerveldl implant, as shown in Figure 9-20, does not have a pressure-sensitive
valve, so hypotouv in the early postoperative period lias to be managed by other
means. Ihpotom is common when drainage implants are installed in a one-stage
procedure without complete tube occlusion. Tin- absence of any resistance io
aqueous outflow invariably results in reduction ol" [OP to below physiologic levels. A
twiHsta^e procedure has been recommended to limii early hypotony. During die
first operation, the scleral plate is sutured to the globe without connecting the tube
into the anterior chamber. "Ibis procedure is followed by a second operation - to S
weeks later, during which the tube is inserted into ihe anterior chamber. An
alternative to limii over liltnnion after one-stage installation involves the temporary
occlusion of the tube lumen with a ligature, or with semi permeable, biodegradable
collagen lacrimal plugs.

D) Stevensjohnson syndrome
1 his patient has a (ollicular conjunctivitis. Follicles arc small avascular mounds of
lymphocytes and plasma cells. Papillae are formed by edema and leakage of fluid
from telangiectatic vessels. The differential diagnosis of ;i folHcuIar conjunctivitis
includes epidemic keratoconjunctivits (EKC), HS\', chlamydia, inolluscum
conuigiosum, as well as topical drug reactions (type I\' hypersensitivity to
medications such as bihmmidine or neomycin).
Sievens:|iihnsnii syndrome is ;i systemic autoimmune disease thai causes sloughing
of the mucous membranes (Including the conjunctiva). This can be a reaction in
medication from sulfonamides, Dilantin, and others. In milder cases, patients may
prcseiu with a papillary conjunctivitis.
2. A) Ligneous conjunctivitis
Chlamydia traclutmatis causes a variety of ocular and systemic diseases. Trachoma
caused by C. Irachomatis serotypes A, B, Ba, and (1 is considered to lie the leading
cause of blindness worldwide. In its early stages, trachoma presents as a folllcular
conjunctivitis. Severe inflammation can lead to tarsal scarring (which may lead to
trichiasis and entropion), corneal scarring, and blindness. Chlamydia tmthomatis
serotypes D-Kare associated with adult inclusion conjunctivitis, which can manifest
as a chronic I'ollicular conjunctivitis. Lymphogranuloma venereum is a result of
infection by serotypes Li. L!2. or LS. This disease docs nol usually affect the eyes.
Ligneous conjunctivitis can resuli from any ocular infection that produces such a
vigorous response that there is exudation of fibrin and formation of a hard, woody
membrane. The etiology of ligneous is nol known, but an inherited plasminogen
deficiency has been suggested.

VDRLand RPR tests delect amilipoidal antibodies produced by the hosi during
treponemal infection. Autoimmune diseases, such as rheumatoid arthritis and lupus
eryihcmaiosus, have similar antibodies that may give a false-positive reading.

15. D) posterior embryotoxon

endolhetium and varying degrees of iris changes, such as coreciopia, holes, and

](>. I)) sclerocornea
Fetal alcohol syndrome is associated with multiple systemic and ocuiar abnormalities,

IS. D) Iridocoraeal endothelial syndrome
Each one of the conditions listed in this question is characterized by corneal
clouding noied soon after birth. Congenital hereditary stromal dystrophy is
associated will) normal eye pressures, normal stromal thickness, and normal coi ncal
diameter. Peters' anomaly is pan of the spectrum of anterior segmem dysgenesis
and is associated with absence of corneal endotheliuni and Descemei's membrane in
i he opacified area of the cornea! leukoma. Congenital glaucoma is characterized by
photophobia, tearing, enlarged corneal diameters, and increased eye- pressures. ICE
may have abnormalities of the endothelium; however, ii does not usually have
corneal edema or opaciflcation as shown.
19. A) Dermolipoma
CJittrisiamm represent normal tissue in an abnormal location, whereas hamartomas
represent abnormal growth of tissue in its normal location. Choristomas include
dennolipomas and dennoids. Hemangiomas and lvni}»haitgionias represent
hamartomas.
20. Hi These are benign with no malignant potential.
Limbal dennoids are well-circumscribed, white-pale yellow, round lesions thai are
choristomas (heterotopic congenital lesion that results from normal tissue residing
in an abnormal location). The flattest meridian of the cornea is adjacent to the
dermoid. They have no malignant potential. They tend to grow as the child grows
but usually very slowly. They can be associated with Goldenhar's syndrome, a uiad ol
epibulbar dennoids, facial anomalies, and skeletal anomalies.
21. I!) Muscle
Dennoids arc chorislomas containing ecioderinal elements (skin, hair, fat, and
sebaceous glands). Muscle tissue is not expected to be found in such a lesion.

23. I)) All of the above

33. D) can produce keratin
Sqiiamous cell caiviimina of the I i in bus is rare. These lesions lend io he gelatinous,
leukoptakic, or, occasionally. papUliform in appearance. Histologically, eptdermoid

34. D) A salmon-colored lesion on the bulbar conjunctiva is characteristic.
Conjunctiva! lymphoma usually presents as a painless, salmon-colored lesion on the
bulbar ( onjunctiva. h may be an isolated finding or may be associated with systemic

--- Page 449 of 562 ---
450 Review Questions in Ophthalmology
35. B) it represents a more aggressive variant of basal cell carcinoma
Mucoepidermoid carcinoma of the conjunctiva is a more aggressive variant of
squamous cell carcinoma. It should be suspected in cases involving recurrence after
primary excision or in which no invasion into the globe occurs. It typically occurs in
patients over age 60 and involves cells that are able to produce malignant mucus-
secreting cells (goblet cells).
36. A) have immunoglobulins bound to the conjunctival basement membrane
Cicatricial pemphigoid is a chronic disease that affects mucosal surfaces typically in
the elderly population. Immunoglobulins at the level of the basement membrane
can be seen with immunofluorescent stains. Ocular cicatricial pemphigoid is usually
bilateral (but may be very asymmetric) and associated with a chronic conjunctivitis.
This leads to subconjunctival fibrosis and a drop in the number of goblet cells. The
subconjunctival Fibrosis also may lead to obstruction of the ducts of the lacrimal and
accessory lacrimal glands. Progression usually results in symblepharon,
foreshortening of the Cornices, and ankyloblepharon. Keratinization of the
conjunctival epithelium as well as corneal neovascularization and scarring are
characteristic of end-stage ocular cicatricial pemphigoid.
37. B) Acanthotic epithelium over fibrovascular cores
Figure 10-10 shows a conjunciival papilloma. In children and young adults, lesions
may be multiple and are usually found on the palpebral conjunctiva. They are of
viral origin, and spontaneous resolution is common. These lesions contrast with
noninfectious papillomas, which occur in older adults and are almost always single
in occurrence, often at the limbus. Atypical cells with dark nuclei may be found
histopathologically. Small caliber vascular channels in a collagenasc stroma are
found in pyogenic granuloma, and lined nonkeratinizing stratified squamous
epilhelia are found in epithelial inclusion cysts.
38. A) Observation ^fV -• ^ + fr^^j
Past episodes with spontaneous resolution suggests viral origin. Incomplete excision
or bleeding during excision may lead to seeding of other sites and multiple
papillomas. Cryotherapy alone or in conjunction with excision with 2- to 3-mm
margins is the preferred surgical treatment. Photocoagulation is not indicated.
39. B) Human papillomavirus
Human papillomavirus DNA has been found in papillomatous lesions of the
conjuncliva. This virus has also been implicated in cases of squamous cell carcinoma
of the conjunctiva.
40. B) Squamous cell carcinoma
Basal cell carcinoma is the most common malignant tumor of the eyelid skin, but
squamous cell carcinoma is the most frequently occurring malignancy of the
conjunctiva. Squamous papillomas are benign lesions that can, on rare occasions,
undergo malignant transformation.

Cornea 451
41. D) The entire thickness of the epithelium may be involved.
Conjunclival intraepithelial neoplasia is the preinvasive stage of squamous cell
carcinoma. By definition, there is no invasion of the basement membrane, but the
entire thickness of the epithelium may be involved in conjunclival intraepiihelial
neoplasia (at which point it is called carcinoma in-situ). It invariably occurs in the
interpalpebr.il area and is characterized by leukoplakia, thickening of the
epithelium, and abnormal vascularization. Treatment is by excisional biopsy with or
without supplemental ciyotherapy to the base.
42. D) Scleritis
Causes of secondary acquired conjunctival melanosis include Addison's disease,
radiation, and pregnancy. Black conjunctival adrenochrome deposits result from the
oxidative byproducts of epinephrine compounds.
43. C) Figure 1(>-13
44. C) Figure 10-13
45. B) Figure 10-12
46. A) Figure 10-11
Conjunctival malignant melanoma (Fig. 10-11) is a pigmented elevated lesion that
enlarges progressively with time. It occurs most commonly on the bulbar
conjunctiva. These tumors are veiy vascular, and dilated vessels may be seen feeding
the tumor. The prognosis is generally better than cutaneous melanomas. A systemic
melastatic work-up is necessary to direct treatment. Conjunclival melanomas can
arise de novo (30%), from nevi (40%), or from primary acquired melanosis (30%).
A conjunctival nevus (Fig. 10-12) is a flat or slightly elevated pigmented lesion of
the bulbar conjunctiva. It may have epithelial inclusion cysts within its substance.
These lesions can grow or enlarge during adolescence or with pregnancy. Suspicious
lesions should be biopsied to rule out melanoma.
Primary acquired melanosis (Fig. 10-14) is a unilateral condition found in middle-
aged white people. These multiple, superficial, flat patches of pigmentation develop
into malignant melanoma in 20% to 30% of these patients. Excisional biopsy should
be performed on any suspicious nodular lesions.
Racial or ocular melanosis (Fig. 10-13) is found most commonly in pigmented indi
viduals and represents benign collections of melanin in the conjunctiva. It appears as
a perilimhal dusting of light brown pigment. No malignant potential exists.

80. G) alcian blue
Lattice earnest dystrophy, shown in Figure 10-28, is an autosomal domiuandy inherited
anneal dystrophy that consists of amyloid deposition into the anterior corneal
stroma, which is best seen by retroillumination. There are two recognized forms of
the disease: type I is localized to the cornea, an'd type II (Mcreloja syndrome)
involves widespread deposition of amyloid and results in systemic findings such as
dry, lax skin; cranial nerve palsies; abnormal ears; and a mask fades. Lattice
dystrophy is a frequenl cause of recurrent erosion because the amyloid deposition in
the anterior stroma leads to weak adherence of the epithelium to Bowman's layer.
Amyloid stains with Congo red, mctachromalicallv with crystal violet, and also with
thiollavin T. Ii also exhibits birefringence and dichroism under the polarized
microscope.

89. C) it occurs as a unilateral disease
Anterior membrane (inap-dot-fingerprint) dystrophy of the cornea occurs bilaterally
but may be asymmetric. It may be dominantly inherited. Characteristic clinical
findings consist of cystic, geographic, or fingerprint formations that may be seen
with sclerotic scatter or relroillumination. Pathologic findings consist of thickened
basement membrane, abnormal epithelial cells with inicrocyst-s, and fihrillar material
between the basement membrane and Bowman's layer. Symptoms typically occur
between the fourth and sixth decades of life. Treatment options include topical
lubricants, 5% sodium chloride, scraping, patching, anterior stromal puncture, or
possibly phototherapeutic keratectomy.
90. A) Mullilaminar Descemet's membrane studded with excrescences
Patients with Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy have malfunctioning Na-K ATPase pumps
in the lateral cell wall of the endothelial cells. This diminished pump function
results in swelling of the conical stronia. Cytochrome oxidase is also reduced, which
may indicate a decrease in the metabolic activity of the endothelial mitochondria.
Histologic examination would reveal a inultilaminar Descemet's membrane studded
with excrescences (Fig. l()-56). This abnormal tissue is a nonspecific response. As the
disease progresses, specular microscopy may demonstrate a more pleomorphic
endothelium with a reduction of endothelial cell density.
Pigment deposition in the trabecular meshwork could result in elevated IOP and
conical edema. Descemel's breaks with keratoconus or birth trauma would be
apparent as discrete lines visible on retroillumination.

Fuchs'endolhelial dystrophy is an autosomal dominant (variable) conical dystrophy
that usually affects postmenopausal women. Clinical findings may include conical
guttae and Descemet's folds. In more advanced disease with endothelial
decompensation, stromal, epithelial edema, and epithelial bullac may be present.

92. A) Bandage contact lens may be used to treal ruptured bullae.
Treatmeni measures are aimed at limiting visual dysfunction and discomfort due to
epithelial breakdown and ruptured bullae. I [ypertonic solutions and ointments may
provide some relief, bin they do little when the edema is advanced. Some feel that
lowering [OP may help control edema. Bold hypertonic solutions and IOP-lowering
agents, however, are only temporizing measures and are not long-term solutions.
Ruptured bullae may be treated with patching or bandage contact lens. Penetrating
keraioplasiy carries a good prognosis in these patients. Anterior stromal puncture is
not indicated in Fuchs' dystrophy, but ii may be helpful in treating cases of
recurrent erosion.

The history described is classic ioracanthainoeba keratiiis. Patients often are contact

114. B) double-walled cysts
Conical ulceration in a contact lens wearer is most commonly caused by Gram-
ncgaiivc_rods, especially Pseudoinonas spp. In this case, however, acanthamoeba is the
infectious agent, and the double-walled cyst form or the tropho/oite is seen in
conical biopsy. Acid-fast pleomorphic rods may be Mycobacterium spp., which rarely
cause keratitis. Branching hyphae are seen in fungal infections, and branching
filaments may be found in infections caused by Nocardia spp. and Aclinomyces spp.
115. B) topical cela/olin
Pictured is a Pseudoinonas aeruginosa corneal ulcer in a contact lens wearer. Initial
antimicrobial therapy should provide broad-spectrum coverage for both Grani-
positive and Gram-negative microbes. Combination therapy with a cephalosporin or
vancomycin for Gram-positive coverage and an aminogylcoside (tobramycin or
gentamicin) provide excellent coverage. Monotherapy with fourth generation
fluoroquinolones (moxifloxacin and galifloxacin) is also used. They have shown to
provide coverage against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative microbes.
Monotherapy with a cephalosporin (cefa/olin) does not provide adequate Gram-
negative coverage.
116. A) Congenital syphilis
Tuberculosis, herpes simplex, and acquired syphilis would more likely cause a
unikucxal interstitial keratins. Congenital syphilis causes a bilateral interstitial
keratitis that may not become manifest until the patient is 10-20 years of age.
Cogan's syndrome affects middle-aged adults with hearing loss, vertigo, and
interstitial keratitis.

121. 1)) Staphylococcal hypersensilivity
Staphylococcal hypersensitivity may cause irritation, corneal infiltrates, and a
localized conjunctivitis. It does not result in conjunctival scarring.
122. I)) floppy eyelid syndrome
Filaments arc strands ofmnens and epithelial cells thai have not been sloughed by

123. 11) Long-term steroids are helpful.
Figure 10-49 shows a corneal epithelial defect. In a cornea with markedly abnormal
sensation without trauma, (his defect most likely represents ueiirotrophic keralitis.

--- Page 465 of 562 ---
466 Review Questions in Ophthalmology
This condition is caused by trigeminal nerve palsy or inflammation, especially the
ophthalmic division. Causes include herpes simplex or zoster, surgery, strokes, and
tumors. Evidence of herpetic etiolog)' includes a previous history of a red eye with or
without pain, shingles, lid and/or conjunctival scarring, corneal stromal haze or
scarring, endothelial keratic precipitates, iritis, and sectoral iris atrophy.
An oval epithelial defect is usually present in the inferior half of the cornea with
rolled, thickened edges. Chronic defects may be accompanied by stromal loss.
Treatment includes lubricants, antibiotic prophylaxis, patching, or lid taping.
Bandage contact lenses may be considered but must be used with caution. Punctal
occlusion may be beneficial. Chronic inflammation at the level of the epithelial
basement membrane may inhibit complete healing of the epithelial defect;
therefore, low-dose topical steroids may promote healing in some cases. However,
steroids must be used with great caution as they may potentiate collagcnase activity
and corneal thinning. Therefore, they are generally not indicated. Tarsorrhaphy
may be necessary in refractory cases, especially in those with markedly abnormal
corneal sensation and lid disorders.
124. D) All of the above
For a traumatic hyphema with normal IOP, observation is appropriate with the
modalities to reduce the risk of a rebleed; this includes bed rest, elevation of the
head, and patching of the eyes to reduce eye movement. Oral aminocaproic acid may
also be helpful to prevent a rebleed. In black patients, a sickle cell prep is essential.
125. A) Anterior chamber washout
If IOP is elevated in hyphema and there are no signs of corneal blood staining,
medical management of the IOP is appropriate. A carbonic anhydrase inhibitor may
not be the best agent to lower IOP with the possibility of sickle cell in a black
patient. If the IOP still remains elevated and/or signs of corneal blood staining
appear, then surgical intervention is necessary with an anterior chamber washout.
Intracameral l-PA may be able to lyse the clot; however, the blood breakdown
products will still remain in the anterior chamber, and the IOP will be unaffected.
126. D) Fleck dystrophy
Superficial phototherapeutic kerateclomy uses the excimer laser to ablate the super
ficial layers of the cornea {epithelium, Bowman's layer, or superficial stroma) to re
move dystrophic or scarred tissue. Granular and Reis-Buckler dystrophies are associ
ated with recurrent erosions, which are treated by superficial keratectomy or
phototherapeutic keratectomy. Epithelial basement membrane dystrophy (also
called map-dot-fingerprint or Cogan microcyslk dystrophy) is an abnormality in epithelial
maturation, turnover, and production of basement membrane. Removal of damaged
epithelium and anterior basement membrane is effective and can be done with pho
totherapeutic keratectomy. Fleck dystrophy is a nonprogressive, usually asymp
tomatic stromal dystrophy with gray-white opacities in the stroma. The epithelium,
Bowman's layer, Descemet's membrane, and endothelium are not affected.
127. D) Severe uveitis and glaucoma may occur.
Chemical injury with acid substances causes denaturation and precipitation of
proteins, which limits ocular penetration. Alkali burns, in contrast, penetrate the

--- Page 466 of 562 ---
Cornea 407
globe rapidly with progressive damage. Symblepharon formation, severe uvcitis,
anterior segment neovascularizatian, glaucoma, and cataract are all later
complications. Burns ;u the limbus cany a particularly poor prognosis because ol
potential ischemia ami growth of neovascular tissue

ISO. C) Topical antibiotics
"flie clinical entity pictured in Figure 10-52 is episcleritis. Although it is usually sell-

181. B) Xecroti/ing seleritis with in Ham mat ion
The most benign form of scleritis is diffuse anterior. In necroti/.ing seleritis with
inflammation, o()% develop complications such as keratitis, cataract, uveitis, and
sderal thinning, and 40% suffer visual loss. Many patients with this process die
within a few years of diagnosis secondary to severe systemic autoimmune diseases,
such as rheumatoid arthritis, polyartcriiis nodosa, and Wegem-r's granuloniatosis.
132. D) llchcefs disease
Scleriiis has been associated with infectious diseases (syphilis, tuberculosis, herpes
/osier, and leprosy), autoimmune diseases (rheumatoid arthritis, Wegener's
gramilomaiosis. systemic lupus erythematosus, and polvarteritis nodosa), and
metabolic diseases (gout).
L33. Ai Posterior sub-Tenon's steroid injection
Injection ol steroids into sub-Tenon's space is contraindicaied because it may
increase the risk ol sclcral thinning and melting. Initial management is usually with
an uial NSA1D agent followed by systemic steroids and topical cyclosporine.
Eventually, systemic immunosuppressives with azathioprine or methotrexate may be
necessary.

134. A) Shield ulcer
Several ocular surface disorders result from an abnormality of the limbal stem cells.
Acquired disorders include injuries, both chemical and thermal, contact lens
induced superior limbaJ keratoconjunctivitis, and Stevensjohnson syndrome. Some
hereditary etiologies include kerautis associated witli multiple endocrine
defirjennes and bilateral aniridki. Shield ulcers occur with limbal-vernal
keratocoiijuncuvitis. No limbal stem cell deficiency is present with this condition.
135. D) hypoventilation during general anesthesia
Reduction of positive vitreous pressure is important during most intraocular
surgeries, especially "open sky" procedures such as penetrating keratoplasty.
Intraoperative maneuvers to reduce vitreous pressure include reducing vitreous
volume by aspiration or dehydration (IV mannitol) and removing pressure on the
globe. I Ivnei-veniilatio]!. rather than hypoventilation, also can be used during cases
under general anesthesia to decrease vitreous pressure.
I8€, I)) Possible microbial contamination
Cornea! sun-age medium often contains colorimetric pi I indicators. Such a color
change indicates a change in pH, which may be consistent with microbial
contamination.
137. G) Pseudophakic bullous keratopathy
Pseudophakic bullous keratopathy is the most frequent indication for penetrating
keratoplasty in adults. This condition may decline in the future because of
improvements in die design of anterior chamber and iris-fixated lOl.s and in
phaco em unification techniques. Regrafis and keratoconus are also frequent
indications for keratoplasty,
138. {',) Peters' anomaly
Peters' anomaly is the most frequent indication for penetrating keratoplasty in
children.
139. A) oversized grafts
Penetrating keratoplasty in children can be very challenging. The eye wall is
extremely flaccid, and scleral support is often crucial. Anterior bulging of the
lens-iris diaphragm-is-t^mmon. and many surgeons prefer to use a small-sized graft
to reduce this problem as well as to reduce the chance of peripheral synechiae
formation. Frequent postoperative follow-up and early suture removal (as early as '2
lo 1 weeks postoperative!}1 in neonates) help reduce the incidence of
neovascularization of the graft.
140. C) slow or delayed healing
Corncal transplants in children and young adults heal very quickly with sutures
becoming vascularized and loosening within the first several months. Glaucoma and
graft rejection are possible just as in adult transplants. In very young children,
amblvopia presents a very real and difficult challenge. Killing these children with

contact lenses may restore vision more quickly mid lessen the degree ofamblyopia
that develops. In addition to the previous complications, self-induced trauma is a
frequent problem.
141. A) '.)()%
Conical graft survival is excellent with current techniques. Tin- incidence is even
hiigg her for conditions such as keratoconus.
142. B) 65%
Graft clarity is often higher for noninflamed eyes. Therefore, penetrating
keraioplasiv should be delayed until the eye is quiet (if possible).
143. C) 20%
Overall, the chance of an endothelial graft rejection episode is '20% lo 15%
according to most studies. If the rejection is recognized early, intensive treatment
may save the graft from failure.
144. C) A15O blood type incompatibility was shown to be a possible risk factor
Tlie most interesting conclusion of this study was that even for high-risk
keraioplasiics. HI_\ tissue matching was neither clearly advantageous nor cost-
effective. The possibility also existed of a correlation of risk and ABO blood type
incompatibility. Further study is needed to decide whether this is a necessary
screening lest.
145. D) all of the above
Patients with connective tissue disorders, traumatic or hereditary epithelial surface
disorders, poorly controlled blepharitis or kciaioconjunctiviiis sicca, and unrealistic
expectations are aiso poor candidates.
146. B) 2\ days
Most contact lens wearers are very reluctant to discontinue use of their lenses for any
significani amount of lime. Stability of refraction and topography are important
when deciding on a surgical plan. Most surgeons require at least 2 to .'! weeks of a
contact lens "holiday" to verify stability. Some individuals require a longer period
before they show stability.
147. B) 8 incisions
The addition of S incisions lo an H-incision radial keraiotomy will often add link-

148. n) 90°
Most surgeons will rarely perform arcuate incisions greater than 90° because of
decreased efficacy ;md increased instability of effect.

The following figure numbers enumerate ihe pictured cataract*:
Figure 11-1 - Christmas tree cataract, myotonic dystrophy
Figure 1 \-2 = sunflower cataract, Wilson's disease
Figure 1 [-3 — posterior subrapsular cataract
Figure 11-4 = morgagnian cataract
Figure I l--r> = phenotMazine cataraci
Figure 1 i-li = anterior polar cataract
The multiple polychromatic crystalline cataract, or "Christmas tree" cataract, is associated
with myotonic dystrophy. These are not present at birth. Inn develop as the patient
at^es. Patients do not typically have symptoms From these crystals. Associated systemic
findings with myotonic dystrophy include temporal bossing, muscular atrophy and
weakness, mental retardation, and abnormalities in cardiac conduction.
Wilson's disease is a multisystem disease as a result of abnormal copper metabolism.
Copper accumulates in ihe anterior lens capsule producing ihe sunflower
appearance. Copper deposition in Descemel's membrane produces the brownish
Kayser-Fleischcr ring as well. Systemic manifestations include cirrhosis, renal
impairment, and degeneration of the basal ganglia. Treatment is directed toward
lowering copper levels wiih penicillaniine.
The posterior subcapsular cataract can be found in many patients as a result of
age-related changes; however, coru'eosteroid use has also been implicated in causing
this opacity.
MfHgagnian catamcts&re mature cataracts in which the peripheral comical material
becomes liquefied and the dense central nucleus can sink inferiorly.
Phenothia/ine and amiodaronc can form deposits in the anterior lens capsule as
shown in the cataract in Figure 1 l-"i. These do not affeci vision.
.\ntrrior jitittn idltaiuis are idiopaihic (90%) remnants of the hyaloid system. These
are typically p_resc-m from birth, n on progressive, and tin not atTect vision. They have
been reported in association with anterior lenticonus as pan of Alpon's syndrome
(deafness, glomerulonephritis).

7. (!) Peters' anomal)
Peters'anomaly fatls into the spectrum of the anterior segmeni dysgeneses. fi is
characterized by lenlicular-corneal adhesions and absence of Descemet's membrane
in ilu- region of the adhesion, The lens ma) be abnormal in these children: however,
the lens nuclei are nol retained.
8. Hi Polychromatic crystalline cataract
Cataracts occur in almost :ill adults with myolonic dystrophy. Iridescent crystals,
usually red or green in color in the anterior and posterior subcapsular regions,
make up the classic early lens changes.
i). A) Prone io angle closure glaucoma aggravated by the administration "f
pilocarpine
Thf patient has a classic description of Weill-Marchesani syndrome, an autosonial
recessivcly inherited disorder, Given the spherical shape of the lens, patients are
pnmc to angle closure glaucoma, which is aggravated by tniosis. Cycloplegia may
cift ri%)sc ])[ipill;u\ I ill ii k hv tightening zonules, decreasing anieroposterior lens

10. A) Peili-Robson chari
The Pelti-Robson chart, Regan contrast sensitivity charts, and Vectorvision are
contrast sensitivitv tests. The Potential Acuity Meter uses the principle of the indirect
ophthalmoscope to project an image of a letter chart on the macula of the patient.
The I'lirkinje vascular phenomenon and the blue-field entopic test are visualizations
of the vasculature of the eye itself when lit>ln is projected through the eyelids or
sclera. These tests are highly subjective and variable.
11. G) In utero
Pictured in Figure 11-7 is a sutural cataract demonstrating opaciftcalion of the fetal Y
sutures. This is a congenital cataract with the formation occurring during
development of the fetal lens nucleus.
12. A) Amiodarone causes stellate anterioi axial pigment deposition.
Auiioilaroni- ;\\\i\ phrtjothin/inc cause stellate cataracts in the anterior lens capsule.
All Forms of steroids have been associated with cataract formation. Although
echothiophate has been associated with progressive cataract formation in adults, this
has nol been reported in children.
IS. C) Low risk of retinal detachment
Marian's syndrome is a genetic abnormality in iibi illin. a structural protein in
collagen. Patients with Marian's are tall; have long, thin lingers; have
hypeiextensible joints; and have aneurysms of the aorta. Ocular manifestations
include subluxation of the lenses superotemporally and a high risk of retinal
detai limcni.

14. 15) Spectacle or contact lens correction of the refractive error
(liven the high risk of retinal detachment, the increased risk of complications with
routine cataract surgery, and the generally nonprogressive nature of the
sublimation, spectacles or contaci lenses should lie tried first. Most cases are
amenable 10 spectacles or contaci lenses. Pupil dilation is sometimes helpful because
it allows the patient to sec around die subiuxcd lens. A reading add is needed
secondary to poor accommodation of the lens due to zonular dehiscence.
15. Di auiosomal dominant inheritance
Persistent hyperplastic primar) vitreous (PMPV) is a congenital, nonhereditary
ocular malformation. Associated findings include elongation of ciliary processes.
prominent radial iris vessels, persistent hyaloid artery, microphthalmia, and ectopia
lends.
Hi. C.) homocystinuria
1*11PV is included in die differential diagnosis of leukocorm. Homocystinuria does
not cause leukocoria but is associated with ectopia lent is along with PHPV.
17. D) I'rine protein and reducing substances
With no family hist cut nncf unilateral complete cataracts, screeniii'-; labs are nut
warranted. Urine protein and reducing substance analysis are screens for Lowe's
syndrome or galactosemia with bilateral cataracts. Bilateral cataracts are associated
with chromosomal abnormalities such as trisomy 13 and Turner's syndrome. If no
view to the Fund us is possible, li-scan ultrasound ol die eye is necessary to rule out
secondary causes such as intraocular tumors.
18. C) Nuclear sclerotic cataracts - homogenous, loss of cellular laminations
In posterior subcapsular cataracts, there is posterior migration of lens epithelial
cells, which swell along the posterior capsule. These swollen cells are called W'r/U or
bladder cells. In conical cataracts, there is hydropic swelling oflens libers. The
eosinophilic, globular material between lens fibers is i ailed uiti>jzft<iiiiLw ultihuJvs.
19. (',) Decreased serum levels ol niciluoninc
The history and physical finding ;ue consistent wilh a diagnosis of homocystinuria,
in which serum levels o^homocvsiini) andlmeihioninelarc elevated. Inheritance is
auiosomal recessive. Patients are normal al birth bin develop seizures, osteoporosis,
and mental retardation. (llassic lens dislocation is bilateral, symmetric, usually
infero nasal.
20. Q Vitamin Bfi
In homocystinuria, there is a disorder in the metabolism of methionine. Deiicient
cweine is thought to weaken /onules. which have a high concentration of cysteme.
Vitamin IS,;, a low methionine high cysteine diet, and vitamin supplementation have
shown promise in treating these patients and in reducing ecLupia lends. ('.ocn/yme
(.) has been touted lor patients willi liber's hercditai'v optic neuropathy, and
Vitamin A may be helpful in some cases olreiiniiis pigmentosa.

21. II) Posterior polar cataracts have been associated with remnants cA the tunica
vasculosa Iculis.
Anterior polar cataracts 2xe usually small and nonprogressive, do not usually impair
vision, and may be seen in association with microphthalmos, a persisteni pupillary
membrane, and anterior lenliconus. Posterior polar cataracts cause more visual
impairmeni and tend to be larger than anterior polar cataracts, lioili can be
autosomal dominant or sporadic. Posterior polar cataracts may be associated with
posterior lemiconus or a rernnani ol the tunica vasculosa lends,
22. 11] zonular dehiscence
Tins is an example of pseudoexfoliation syndrome. Nine the deposition of
pseudoexfoHaiive debris on [he anterior lens capsule and at ilie pupillary margin.
This syndrome is associated with an approximately five times higher rate of vitreous
kiss during extracapsular cataract extraction. In some cases, phacodonesis or
iridodonesis may be evident at the alii lamp, signaling lens subluxation. Other times,
however, lens subluxation and zonular dehiscence may noi be evident until during
surgery. Severe postoperative inflammation and cornea] decompensation are not
necessarily more, common in these patients. Retained viscoelaslic may cause a
temporary rise in IOP after surgery, which may complicate cases with
pscudoexloliaiive glaucoma.
23. D) Elderly, white women
I'seudoexi'iilianon_is typically IouihI in people of Scandinavian descent, and the
incidence increases with age. Young, myopic males may be predisposed to
pigmentary dispersion syndrome. The central serous choroidopathy patient
characteristically is a type A personality man. Tall, thin body habitus may be found
with Marian's syndrome.
24. A) Adheres to and protects endothelium from damage
A dispersive viscoelastic has a low surface tension and when used in the eve lias a
higher retention than a cohesive viscoelastic. With high vacuum, the dispersive vis-
(oelastic tends to fragment, making it harder to remove from the eye. A layer ol this
viscoelastic can protect the endothelium from lens fragments during phacoemulsin-
calion. All viscoelastics. if retained in the eye, can cause increases in IOI'.
25. II) Laser peripheral iridectomy
This case describes iris bombe, which can occur if the aqueous humor produced by
ihe ciliary body is unable to move around the It )I. to the trabecular meshwork, The
iris is pushed forward peripherally and causes a secondary angle closure. A similar
situation can occur in uveiiis ifsynechiae form 'M\i\ around the pupillary border. A
peripheral iridectomy is necessary to create an alternate location for the aqueous. A
paiacentcsis would uoi be easy 10 perform with the forward position of the iris.
Release of the aqueous may worsen the condition as well.
2f>. Ill The measured astigmatism has increased.
Toric lenses are designed to counteract the cornea! astigmatism. When the lens
rotates off axis, the amount of astigmatism correction decreases. When it rotates W
off axis, the astigmatism measured will be greater than the cornea! astigmatism. The

lens should !><■ rotated back to the correci position within iln- several week.s after
surgery before the anterior and posterior capsule fuse together and make it more
difficult in manipulate the lens. The lens should not be placed in the ciliary sulcus

28. C) Perform surgery on one eye with immediate aphakic correction; perform
surgery on the other eye before !l months of age.
Bilateral congenital cataracts of significant opacity, as in Figure 11-12. should be
addressed surgically as soon as possible. Aphakic correction with an aphakic contact
lens is performed immediately pos tope ra lively. Surgery on the second eye should be
done as soon as possible after the first eve. Vision may develop normally without
surgery if the lens opacity is axial and the pupiN are continuously dilated.
When indicated, surgery is ideally performed before the child is :t months of age.
Profound amblyopia is present by this age and nystagmus develops. Cataract surgery
after the development of nystagmus often does not improve visual ai uity and
nystagmus persists.
29. Hi i in plan i at ion of posterior chamber 1O1,
Cataract surgery in infants differs from that in adults in several important ways. In
infants, the nucleus, although opaque, is soft and gummv. Marked opa< ilic at ion and
membrane formation occur on the posterior capsule and anterior hyaloid if lite
posterior capsule is left untouched. Additionally, significant adhesions exist between
the lens and anterior hyaloid face. For these reasons, intiacapsular cataraci
extraction is not considered because vitreous loss may occur. Extracapsular cataract
extraction is performed by aspirating the nucleus and cortex with ;i
phacoemulsification handpiece orvitrectomy instrument A posterior capsulectomy
is followed by a limited anterior vitrectontv. Postoperative aphakic correction via
contact lens can be started immediately. Implantation of 1( )!.•> in children is
controversial. In infants under 2 years of age, lenses are generally con train dicated
because the eye undergoes a drastic change in refractive power as it grows. An
implanted IOI. would have to be removed or exchanged in later years.
;1O. <.) Proliferation of residual lens epithelium
Opacilkaiiiin of the posterior capsule following extracapsulai cataraci extraction is
not an uncommon problem. I he main causes include proliferation of retained lens
epithelium and capsular fibrosis and contraction. An opening in the capsule can be
made with a YAG laser, providing a clear visual pathway.

Complications of YAG capsulotomies resuli from the energy delivered to die eye.

put traction on the vitreous, causing a retinal break. Elevated IOI1, iritis, and

the laser is properly focused at the posterior capsule.
:S2. D) 1 lie speed of tollers in the peristaltic pump allows linear control of vacuum.
Three kinds of aspiration systems exist in phacoemulsification machines. The
peristaltic pump has rollers that move along lulling and create a relatively rapid rise in
vacuum. The diaphragm pump has valves over both the inlet and outlet of a fluid
chamber covered bv a diaphragm. This system allows a slower build of vacuum. The
Venturi fmmb produces the most rapid increase in vacuum. This, however, can he the
most dangerous because il allows almost instantaneous engagement of unwanted
tissues such as capsule or iris.
:i!i. (',) ex< essive phacoemulsi[i( ation power
Iris prolapse associated with an excessive!v deep anterior chamber suggests too liigh
a bottle height) which can be remedied by simply lowering the bottle. If iris prolapse
is associated witii a normal or shallow anterior chamber depth, this may be caused
by wound leak around the phacoemulsification lip or possibly suprachoroidal
hemorrhage. If leak around the phaco tip is evident, a temporary suture may he
placed. Peripheral iridectoim at the site of prolapse may also help reposil the iris.
Excessive phacoemulsification power does not result in iris prolapse,
3-f. A) 1'ais pi ana vitrectomy, lensectomy
When traumatic capsular rupture is present with lens subluxation and disruption of
the anterior hyaloid face, the pars pi ana approach For lensectomy and vitrectomy is
the most appropriate. The presence ofa hard nucleus, il'present, may make (his
technically difficult. Extracapsular cataract extraction would not be indicated with
loss of zonular integrity and presence of free vitreous. Intracapsuiar cataract
extraction is contraiudicated when vitreous is present and the capsule is ruptured.
35* li) A sutured posterior chamber lens is appropriate.
In this case, support for a posterior chamber lens, either in the sulcus or capsular
bag, would very likely be inadequate. Anterior chamber lens placement should be
considered, but the presence ofa large iridodialysis may complicate placement.
Other factors that are relative contraindications lor anterior chamber lens
placement, if preseni, include conical endoihclial dystrophy, abnormal angle vessels
or structures, and peripheral anterior synechiae. Placement of an anterior chamber
lens would, however, lie technically easiest. Snlcus fixation via iranssclerai
polypropylene (Prolcne) sutures would be appropriate but is technically more
difficult. Aphakic contact lens wear is an option but not die best one.
;{(>. C) remove static charges, which aiiraci debris

debris when opened. Therefore, the lens may be rinsed witli balanced sail solution
before insertion. Silicone lenses do not require this and, in fact, may be more
difficult to handle once they have gotten wet. Application ofa viscoj-'lastic, such as
sodium hyaluronaie, may facilitate um:ni<_>n ofa lens, especially a foldable one,
through a small incision.

37. D) Anterior sclerotomies may be Indicated at the time of surgery.
The indications for cataract surgery tn[nanophthaltntc|patients are similar to diose in

38. A) clear corneal incision
Risk factors lor expulsive choroidal hemorrhage include myopia, glaucoma.
atherosclerotic vascular disease, hypertension, and. previous expulsive hemorrhage
in the opposiie eye. I'sing as small an incision as possible along with being ready to
perform a sclerotomy is important. Decompression before opening the globe with
digital pressure or other device may be helpful. Close the wound with
nonabsorbable. preferably nylon, sutures to prevent delayed hemorrhage. It is also
beneficial to keep blood pressure controlled and level of anesthesia deep (if general
anesthesia is used) during surgery. Shelved or self-sealing incisions allow mine rapid
closure and repressiiri/aiion ol die globe should bleeding occur.
'Mi. 1!) Ideally, uveitis should be quiet lor 3 to (> months before elective surgery.
The indications for cataract surgery in patients with uveiiis are similar to those in
patients without uveitis. 1 lowever, complications in the early or late postoperative
period may arise in certain nvekides. Elective cataract surgery is besi performed alter
inflammation IS quiel for several months. Even minimal or baseline inflammation
preoperatively may result in marked inflammation postoperaiivelv. Prophylaxis or
treatment may require hourly topical steroids or even systemic steroids.
KM. implantation is contraindicated in patients with juvenile rheumatoid
arthritis—associated iridocyclitis. Development of cyclitic membranes and subsequent
< iliary body detachment following extracapsuiar cataract extraction suggest the need
for complete capsuiar removal. Therefore, combined Icnseciomy and subtotal
vitrectomy is recommended either through the Umbus or pars plana. Patients with
[■'m bs' heterochromic iridocyclitis are among the group that dot's best following
cataract extraction and after KH.s have been safely implanted. A transient
postoperative hyphema may develop but resolves without sequelae. Although those
with pars planhis do not apparently have a .significantly increased risk ol
i complications associated with routine cataract surgery, pars plana lensectomy-
vitrectomy may lie indicated as postoperative vitreous opacities may limit visual
acuity. Visual acuity may also be limited by cystoid macular edema.
40. ('.) the excessive mobility of the posterior capsule
The posterior capsule is capable of moving more posteriot than normal without
support ol ihe vitreous. Zonular dehiscence may result from attempted manual
expression Df the nucleus during extracapsuiar cataract extraction. The posterior

--- Page 494 of 562 ---
■196 Review Questions in
capsule is also more likely to move forward with tin- lens during phacoemulsification
and aspiration in exiracapsular cataraci extraction via phacoemulsifit aiion. As a
result, capsular tears may be more likely. Collapse of the globe is usually not
problematic with ;i well-formed phaco incision. Positive pressure from the posterior
segment occurs more frequently when the vitreous cavity is occupied by the vitreous.
41. 15) ;iu attempt should be made to perform photocoagulation before cataract
surgery
According to the Early Treatment of Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS), in
diabetic reiiunpathy. argon laser photocoagulation should be attempted to the
degree allowed by the lens opacity before cataraci surgery. In this case, concomitant
focal and pan retinal photocoagulation or focal laser followed by panretinal
photocnagiilation slumld be performed. As many laser spots as possible to control
neovascularization should be placed before surgery.
Although extracapsular cataract extraction with or without KM. implantation may
!)e indicated following photocoagulation, maintenance of an intact posterior capsule
appears to be important. The intact posterior capsule (or anterior hyaloid face)
appears to aci as a barrier, to some extent, to the proposed angiogenic factors thai
lead in development of neovascularization. Successful extracnpsular caiaraci
extraction with intaci posterior capsule does not appear to increase the risk of
anterior segment neovascularization leading to postoperative ncovascular glaucoma,
•12. 11) laser peripheral tridotomy and topical steroids
This is a case of phacolytit glaucoma due to a hypermature cataract. A dense, white
cataraci along with anterior segment inflammation is noted. Lens proteins, which
have liquefied, leak through an intact lens capsule and attract macraphages with
resultant inflammation. The combination of lens proteins and macrophages then
block the irane.cular ineshwork with elevation of the I OP.
Definitive treatmeni is cataraci extraction. When possible, however, it may be
beneficial to aiic-mpi control of inflammation and IOP medically before surgery.
Most surgeons will perform exiracapsular cataract extraction with l()l. implantation,
but intracapsular caiaraci extraction may be preferred if significant zonular
dehiscence is discovered. Laser peripheral iridotciniy is not indicated as glaucoma in
lliis case is not due to angle closure.

hours pnslnperatively. Removing the viscoelastic did not eliminate significant

1. D) Carotid stenosis
2. A) Hypopyon
3. A) Ophthalmodynamametry
Pictured In Figure 1 is a patient with ocular ischeraic syndrome. In this condition,
ihere are dot and blot hemorrhages in the midperipheral retina that may extend
into the posterior pole. Ocular perfusion pressure on ophthalmodynamometry is
reduced substantially with collapse of die centra] retinal artery with minimal
pressure. Anterior segment manifestations include ;i limbal flush, anterior chamber
cell and Hare, neovascularization of the iris and angle, and cataract. Hypotony from
decreased aqueous production or elevated !()!' from neovascularization of the angle
may be present. High-grade carotid stenosis is the usual cause. Many of these
patients also have systemic hypertension, atherosclerosis, and diabetes tncllitus. The
prognosis of this condition is usually poor, especially if neovascularization is present.
A central retinal vein obstruction would have cotton wool spots, nerve liber layer
hemorrhages, and more dilated and tortuous vessels than would this condition. In
both, fluorescein angiography would have delayed Tilling of the retinal vasculature.
4. C) Recurrent pneumonia, weight loss, and vascular skin lesions
The fundus photo in Figure 12-2 depicts multiple cotton wool spots and
hemorrhagic necrosis of the retina after a vascular distribution. In a young patient
with recurrent infections, immune deficiency should be considered, and I1IV status
should always be ascertained. From 15% to -10% of patients with AIDS develop CMV
retinilis. Common presenting symptoms include floaters and decreased vision.
Cotton wool spots and hemorrhages may be seen in branch retinal vein occlusions;
however, there would be no associated vitritis.
5. A) Retina! necrosis
CMV reiiuitis i.s a hemorrhagic necrotizing retinitis involving all retinal layers.
Intranuclear inclusion bodies may be found. Loss of pericytes and macroaneurysrns
can be seen with diabetes. Thickening and excrescences ofBruch's membrane
correspond to the druscn seen in ARMD.
6. A) Retinal detachment
CMV ret i nil is can lead to significant atrophy of the retina and subsequent retinal
detachment Oftentimes, multiple retinal defects are present, and the patients need
long-term internal tamponade with silicone oil to prevent recurrent detachments.
Siegrist streaks are alrophic areas of the RPE overlying areas of Infarction of a
choroidal lobule and may be found with hypertensive retinopathv.
7. 11) Retinal thickening greater than 1 disc area in size and within 1 disc diameter of
ihe center of the fovea

--- Page 538 of 562 ---
Retina and Vitreous 541
CSAIEis defined as one or more of the following criteria:
1. Retinal thickening within 500 (xin of the fovea.
2. Hard exudates within 500 u,m of the fovea with associated retinal thickening.
3. Retinal thickening 1 disc area or greater, part of which is within 1 disc
diameter of the fovea.
8. D) Focal laser photocoagulation
The efficacy of focal laser photocoagulation for diahetic macular edema has been
demonstrated by the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS). Treated
patients had half the likelihood of moderate visual loss as did untreated eyes. PRP
was found to exacerbate macular edema.
9. D) A pet cat at home
The picture shown in Figure 12-4 and this history are suggestive of Lebcr's
idiopathic stellate neurorelinilis. The exact etiology' of neuroretinitis is unknown but
has been linked to viral infections (mumps, influenza, varicella) and other diseases
(cat-scratch fever, leptospirosis).
10. B) CBC, VDRL, toxoplasma liter, viral titcr screen
Differential diagnosis may include syphilis, toxoplasmosis of the optic nerve, diffuse
unilateral subacutc neuroretinitis, trauma, systemic hypertension, and diabetes
mellitus.
11. C) Observation
The natural course of Leber's stellate neuroretinitis is spontaneous resolution over
several months. The prognosis is excellent, and over 80% of patients have visual
acuity better than 20/40.
12. C) Bimodal age distribution
Coats' disease (congenital retinal tclangiectasias) tends to occur unilaterally in
otherwise healthy boys. The majority of boys have the juvenile form, with a peak
incidence within the end of the first decade. An adult form occurs after age 16 and
may be associated with hypercholesterolemia.
13. B) When there is development of neovasculari/ation of any type
The Central Retinal Vein Occlusion Study investigated the role of PRP in preventing
the development of neovascularization of the iris or angle. The study found no
significant benefit of PRP as a prophylaxis for anterior segment neovascularization.
Patients with PRP at the onset of rubeosis had a higher rate of regression than those
with prior treatment. Photocoagulation for macular edema in a CRVO was not
found to be effective.
14. D) Prosthetic cardiac valves
Histopathologically, CRVO occurs as a result of thrombosis at the lamina cribrosa.
Risk factors for CRVO include hypertension, glaucoma, diabetes, hyperopia,
hypercoagulable states, and older age. Emboli are associated with arterial occlusion
rather than venous obstruction.

21. (') renal cell carcinoma
Figure 12-I> demonstrates a retinal arterial macroaneurysm. Macroaneurysms tend to
occur in the elderly population and have been associated with systemic hypertension
and atherosclerosis. Complications that may result include vitreous hemorrhage,
macular edema, and exudaies. Renal cell carcinomas iniiv be found in up to 25% of
patients with von llippel-I.indaii disease. These patients present wiih retinal
hemangioblastomas rather than with macroaneurysms. Other systemic
manifestations of this phakomatosis include pheochroniocyionias, pancreatic and
renal cysts, and hemangioblastomas of the CNS and visceral organs.

23. B) Macular edema
Idiopathic juxtafoveal telangiectasis may present in two forms. A congenital form
may be a subtype of Coats' disease. Acquired forms may be found in middle-aged
patients. The telangiectasias may be unilateral or bilateral. They are often located
temporal to the fovea. Complications that may develop include macular edema,
exudates, and choroidal neovascularization.
24. B) Focal leaking hot spot
Figure 12-37 shows a case of idiopathic central serous choroidopathy (ICSC)
demonstrating the classic serous elevation of the neurosensory retina over the fovea.
Notice the multiple hypopigmented patches of RPE indicative of previous episodes.
Fluorescein angiography of ICSC characteristically shows a focal site of leakage
from the choroid into the subsensory retinal space. The "smokestack" of dye
collecting under the retina (Fig. 12-37) is the classic description (actually seen in
<20% of cases).
Other possible causes of serous elevation of the retina include optic pits (serous
detachment would be adjacent to the optic nerve), choroidal neovascular
membranes (gray-green subretinal lesions, lipid, and hemorrhage), and serous
detachmenLs over nevi or melanoma (choroidal nevus/tumor would be visible on
opluhalmoscopy).

Baltimore: Williams & Wilkins, 1997.

25. A) Observation
For many cases of ICSC, the serous detachment will spontaneously resolve over 3 to
4 months. Laser photocoagulation hastens reabsorplion; however, there is no
difference in ultimate visual acuity compared with observation. Elevation of the
retina causes a hyperopic shift, and a new refraction may temporarily help until the
fluid resorbs. Periocular steroids are not beneficial. Panretinal laser
photocoagulation or sclcral buckling is not indicated.
26. D) Punched-out choriorclinal scars
This patient has a juxtapapillary choroidal neovascular membrane secondary to
POHS. Clinical findings of POHS may include a peripapillary ring of pigmentation
and RPE atrophy, punched-out chorioretinal scars in the periphery and posterior
pole, and linear chorioretinal scars. Bone spicules may he seen with retinitis
pigmentosa, sea-fan neovascularization may be seen with sickle cell retinopathy, and
snow-banking may be seen with pars planitis.
27. B) Ohio-Mississippi river valley
POHS is thought to be more prevalent in the Ohio-Mississippi river ralley than in
the rest of the United States. The Sanjoaquin ralley has been linked to
coccidiomycosis, and the Rocky Mountains have been linked with Lyme disease or
Rocky Mountain spotted fever.
28. B) Laser photocoagulation
The Macular Photocoagulation Study demonstrated a benefit of laser treatment for
juxtafoveal and extiafoveal CNVM in patients with POHS. Submacular surgery for
this condition is promising but still investigational. Steroids and triple sulfa are
treatments for toxoplasmosis.

29. (-) Araaurosis
Temporary blockage of a retina] arteriolc may cause obscuration of vision for
seconds to minutes, known as ammiTosis. In Figure 12-12, a cholesterol embolus
(Hollenhorst plaque) is present at the bifurcation ora retinal arteriole. Floaters and
Flashing lights may indicate vitreous traction or formation of a retinal break.
Metamorphopsia is more typical of conditions thai cause distortion of the fovea.
3D. 0] Geographic atrophy (age-related maculardegeneration [ARMD])
In dry ARMD, as in tins case, there is atrophy and loss of the Rl'E. The areas may
become confluent, producing geographic regions of atrophy.
81. H) Counsel and monitor with an Amslergrid
No effective treatment for dry ARMD has been found. Antioxidani vitamins and
nutritional supplements may play a role in the prevention of ARMD. tun once the
loss of the RPE and photoreceptors has occurred, they are nf no benefit Eyes with
the dry Form of ARMD may rarely develop exudative changes, and monitoring with
an Amsler grid is appropriate. Laser and subretinal surgery may be indicated il a
choroidal neovascular membrane is present; however, they are ool appropriate for
the nonexudative form of ARMD.
32. A) Subretinal blood
Subreiinal hemorrhage, alongwiili a gray—green crescent, subretinal exudaies, and a
localized serous retinal detachment, may he indicative of an active choroidal
neovascuiur membrane, Photocoagulation scars may indicate previous treatment for
exudative ARMD, placing this eye at a higher risk of recurrence of a CNVM.
Approximately 1(1% of patients with dry ARMD will progress onto the wet form.
33. C) Vitreous biopsy and injection of intrnvilreal antibiotics in patients with better
than hand-motions vision did equalh well as patients with immediate vitreciomy
and injection of intravitreaJ antibiotics in final visual outcome.
'fhe Endophthatmitis Vitrectomy Study specifically investigated the treatment of
patients with endophthalmitis occurring within (> weeks of cataract surgeiy. Patients
were randomized to receive or not receive IV antibiotics and to undergo a
viirectomy/injei lion of intravitreal antibiotics or vitreous tap/injection of
iniravilival antibiotics. The results of the study indicated the following:
1. There was no difference in final visual acuity/media clarity whether or not
patients received systemic antibiotics.
2. Hand motions or belter visual acuity on presentation did equally well with
immediate vitreous biopsy or vitrectomy.
!i. Kyeswilh light perception-only vision had much better visual outcome with
immediate vitreciomy rather than vitreous biopsy.

35. D) Atherosclerosis
The causes of CRAO in children and young adults differ From those <>l the older
population. In one study, one third of the patients had a history of migraine. Othei
factors include trauma (especially in males), hypercoagulable states (oral
contraceptives, pregnancy), cardiac emboli, collagen-vascular disorders, and l\' drug
abuse. Iii ihe elderly population, atherosclerotic disease is much more common.
:S6. \\ Dalen-Fuchs nodules
Sickle cell rcfinopailiy may have the following retinal findings: sunbursts (black

with sickle cell disease. Dalen-Fuchs' nodules are excrescences found at the level of
Hindi's membrane seen in sympathetic ophthalmia.
37. IS) Roils
Retinitis pigmentosa represents a collection of retinal degenerations with rod
degeneration as the hallmark finding. A cone degeneration may occur secondarily.
38. D) The only effective treatment is eniideation of the traumatized eye.
After penetrating ocular trauma to one eve. bilateral i>rauu]oinaioiis inflammation

41. D) 25%
In 15% to 25% of patients, a cilioretinal artery may be present to perfuse the macula.
Pulsatile flow of blood with each heartbeat may be seen in these arteries. In the case of
a CRAO, the cilioretinal artery may allow preservation of central acuity. Fluorescein
angiography (Fig. 12-38) shows the extensive retinal arterial nonperfusion, except in
the distribution of the cilioretinal artery in a patient with a CRAO.

42. C) Rubeosis iridis
The incidence of rubeosis may be as high as 15% to 20% after CRAO. These patients
may also develop neovascularization of the disc and retina.
43. D) Epirelinal membrane
Conditions that may have CME without leakage of fluorescein include Goldmann-
Favre, retinitis pigmentosa, and nicotinic acid maculopathy. Epiretinal membranes
may cause a CME that leaks from traction and distortion of paramacular capillaries.
44. A) choroidal neovascularization
A choroidal neovascular membrane will cause subretinal hemorrhage or intraretinal
hemorrhage. Preretinal hemorrhage would be very unusual. Sickle cell retinopathy,
trauma (e.g., shaken baby syndrome), and macroaneurysms may cause hemorrhages
at all levels of the retina.
45. C) Anterior chamber washout
Figure 12-14 presents a patient with a hyphema after blunt trauma to the globe. The
RBCs of patients with sykltUfill disease are often stiff and irregular in shape and do
not pass through the trabecular mcshwork easily. Increased IOP may be treated
initially with aqueous suppressants; however, carbonic anhyHroif inhihitoix may
exacerbate the sickling by causing a metabolic acidosis. An anterior chamber
washout removes the inflexible RBCs and will help return the IOP to normal.
Vasoconstriction with phenylephrine may also increase possible thrombosis and

--- Page 546 of 562 ---
Retina and Vitreous 549
vascular compromise. Cryopexy should be used with caution with the tenuous
vasculature. Cryopexy is not used to treat the hyphema per se, but it may be
indicated for retinal ischemia and neovascularization.
46. D) headaches and nausea
47. A) Optic nerve druscn
48. B) Gastrointestinal bleeding
49. A) Choroidal neovascularization
Pictured in Figure 12-15 are angioid streaks in a patient with pscudoxanthoma
elasticum. The radiating hyperfluoiescent lines are window defects from a break in
Bruch's membrane. The predominant conditions associated with angioid streaks
include Paget's disease of bone, pseudoxanthoma elasticum, Ehlers-Danlos, and
sickle cell disease.
Paget's disease is a spectrum of findings, including osteoclastic hyperactivity,
especially at the base of the skull and in long bones. These patients are prone to
pathologic fractures.
Sickle cell patients may have a number of retinal findings, including salmon
patches, sea fan neovascularization, black sunbursts, and angioid streaks. They have
a higher incidence of thrombotic episodes and may autoinfarct their spleens.
Ehlers-Danlos is a genetic disorder caused by a defect in collagen synthesis. The
patients may have hyperextensible joints and lax skin. Ocular findings include
dislocated lenses, retinal detachment, and blue sclera.
Pseudoxanthoma elasticum classically has the findings of angioid streaks, .ojjde.
nerve liead drusen, and a "peauKTorange" appearance to the retina. These patients
have cutaneous manifestations with the "plucked-chicken" appearance shown in
Figure 12-39. They are prone to widespread vascular malformations. Abnormalities
in the mucosal vasculalure of the stomach and bowel can lead to recurrent
hemorrhage.

than the argon. 'I'h t* xenon arc emits a large spectrum of wavelengths and lias been
replaced with the monochromatic lasers. The CO« laser is well-absorbed In water
and is used for surface tissue ablation.
51. D) they occur in less than \'\ of autopsy eyes
Pars plana cysts are cjjiuiiion and occur in 16% to 18% ofautopsied eyes. They
contain tnucopolysaccharides on histopalhology and can be seen in otherwise
normal eyes. Patients with multiple myeloma may have several large pars plana cysts
thai contain myeloma proteins.
52. C) Maroteaux-Lamy
RIM', degenerations have been identified in patients with 1 lunier's, I lurler's,
SanfiHppo's, and Scheie's mucopolysaccharidoses. Maroteaux-Lnmy syndrome is not
usually associated with RPF, degeneration.
53. D) chloroquinc
Retinal crystals may he seen in patients using tamoxifen, canthaxanthine, or
rnethoxyflurane. Retinal crystals can also he seen in patients who have laic
retinopathy. l\' injection of talc crystals result in their distribution via the retinal
circulation. Chloroquine or hydroxyclilorontiine ran result in a hull's-eye
maculopathy as a result of disturbance of the RPE, but crystals arc not present.
54. C) Stargardt's disease
True silent choroid is defined as the blockage of underlying choroidal fluorescence
during [luorescein angiography by maieria] in the KIT, cells. This occurs classically
in patients who have Stargardt's disease. I.ipoiuscin accumulation within RIM'. rells_
results in the blockage of under King choroidal fluorescence and gives the
appearance of a very dark or silent choroid. Systemic {.irgyrosis.'-wliich occurs in
patients who arc- receiving systemic si^er {e.g., From tanning agents), can also cause
an angiographically dark choroid,
55. D) Vitrectomy and silicone oil tamponade
Rhegmatogenous detachments occui" in ii|) to clb% hi patients with CMV retinitis.
They are associated with a diffusely necrotic peripheral retina with numerous small
retinal holes. These detachments cannot he repaired by conventional scleral
buckling surgery, and they require pars plana vitrectomy widi internal Bilicone oil
tamponade and endolaser.
56. B) myopia
Although exudative retinal detachments may occur in CMV retinitis, ii is very
uncommon. Myopia is not associated with exudative retinal detachments.
